```
Thank you very much for the attention Steve.

   Just one more detail than I forgot to mention: this mistake also
happens in the IF command (in .GNT). For instance:
...
             IF W-INDEX IS GREATER THAN ((W78-FIRST - 1) * W78-SECOND)
...

Good Bye,

Rovani Marcelo Rech

> >   The program below demonstrates a calculation mistake that happens
in
```

#### ↳ Re: Level 78 - Wrong Result in COMPUTE - NE v3.x

- **From:** mano@mano.com (free)
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T02:28:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a78c3ad.8199162@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<959cp9$gg9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have an older DOS/OS2 set of Micro Focus Cobol manuals.   The COBOL
User Guide says the following about the COMPUTE statement...

*Do not use the COMPUTE statements except for performing calculations
involving floating-point data*

Obviously COMPUTE works nicely with other data types in many
situations, however, when considering the behavior of level 78s in
general, which are an MF extension, always avoid them in arithmetic
expressions - especially compound ones.   I seem to recall them
causing trouble elsewhere too.  

I don't see this behavior as a mistake, rather I see it as an
implementation issue.  I have found other languages to have unusual or
unexpected behavior with constants as well, depending on just where
they are defined and how they are used.   What makes sense in one
language may not make work in another.  One compiler I saw, which had
no statement terminators for constants would interpret any trailing
spaces following a constant definition as part of the literal value -
including numeric literals which of course became strings.

EF


On Wed, 31 Jan 2001 15:59:44 GMT, Rech Informatica Ltda/Brazil
<rovani@rech.com.br> wrote:

>Good afternoon,
>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
In article <EtZd6.46568$lh.3010068@typhoon.mn.mediaone.net>,
  "lurker" <johndoe@blah.com> wrote:
> I have a homework assignment for COBOL class due this evening that I
totally
> forgot about.
>
> I need to ask someone who is currently a COBOL programmer, the
following
> questions:
>
> 1.  Name (first okay), Company, Title
Charles
> 2.  What platform do you program COBOL on?
IBM VSE MAINFRAME COMPILER = COBOL LE
> 3.  What do you like about COBOL?
It is easy to learn and easy to program in with the least amount
of knowledge and aptitude for the programmer.
> 4.  What don't you like about COBOL?
you can not do a for loop like C++ or Java.
Example:  for (i=1; i<5; i++)
             { some command }
You can not convert from packed very easily in a single command
without an extra storage location.  I think you should be able to do
the move and edit at once.  In Java you can use casting to chage data
from one type to another.  Also, COBOL can handle OO programming
techniques such as polymorphism.
>
> Can anyone help me out?  Short but sweet answers are just fine.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can you answer these 3 simple questions for me?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T22:19:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yBpe6.30079$Ch.6791719@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<EtZd6.46568$lh.3010068@typhoon.mn.mediaone.net> <95c6ou$vnr$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <5dbj7ts7767n5g4is7epqfni6o4iulkemt@4ax.com> <95cmar$fdn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You're working on OS390, there are 2 (TWO) ways to do bit manipulation:
1) LE routines - Bit test, Bit change, Bit XOR, etc

2)
    05  WS-LOW-VAL           PIC X(01)  VALUE X'00'.
    05  WS-HI-VAL                 PIC X(01)  VALUE X'FF'.
    05  WS-HEX-01               PIC X(01)  VALUE X'01'.
    05 WS-HEX-1001           PIC X(01) VALUE X'09'.
    05 WS-HEX-1010           PIC X(01) VALUE X'0A'.
    05 WS-HEX-10101010  PIC X(01) VALUE X'AA'.

 or if you need to do math / creating masks 'on the fly'.

    05  WS-DLOW-VAL                                PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 0.
    05  WS-DHI-VAL                                     PIC 9(04) BINARY
VALUE 65535.
    05  WS-DEC-01-00000001                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 1.
    05  WS-DEC-02-00000010                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 2.
    05  WS-DEC-03-00000011                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 3.
    05  WS-DEC-04-00000100                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 4.
    05  WS-DEC-05-00001001                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 5.
    05  WS-DEC-06-00000110                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 6.
    05  WS-DEC-07-00001011                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 7.
    05  WS-DEC-08-10000000                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 8.
    05  WS-DEC-09-10000001                   PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 129.
......
    05  WS-DEC-129             PIC 9(04) BINARY VALUE 129.
etc


MOVE WS-DLOW-VAL  TO WS-WORK.
ADD WS-DEC-01-00000001, WS-DEC-02-00000010, WS-DEC-129 TO WS-WORK.

Ws-work now has bit pattern: 10000100 or 132


1000001

> project. What a bunch of jerks!
```

## Thread: Numeric testing — 15 msgs

### Numeric testing

- **From:** "Bloggs" <bloggs@neil-1234.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T05:42:15
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi everybody,
                      I am using Merant Microfocus Personal COBOL compiler.
I have an assignment in which I have to test a customer code for numeric. So
I define the recieving field as :-
 01 Customer-code.
        03 numeric-part         pic x(4).
        03 alpha-part             pic x.
the alpha-part field is to hold a check digit as I am calculating a Modulus
11 check digit to test that the customer code is valid and has not been
corrupted. I have coded the following test :-

     if numeric-part numeric then
        do this-part
      else
         do the-other-part
     end-if

when I run the program, though the "do the-other-part" never gets executed
when the numeric-part holds non-numeric data. Unless, that is, i change the
test to :-
   if numeric-part not numeric then
      do-the-other-part-first
   else
      do-this-part-last
   end-if

any ideas?
 also when I print out my source code the y2k bug strikes, yes I get 1901 as
the date printed at the bottom of each page.(this has only started happening
this year) Nothing serious for a student version of a compiler, but it still
annoys me.

   Barney.
Happy New Year for 2001 !!
```

```
Hi Barney,

It would be good to see what values numeric-part had before doing the
numeric class test.

I've just tried various compilers I have and get the expected results.
It could be a bug in an older revision of your compiler. We have also fixed
all our y2k compiler problems along time ago. Not sure how you get a later
version of your product I'm afraid.

If this program also fails, you could send me the .int code file and I can
check the code the compiler is producing is okay.

123456 01 .
        03 numeric-part         pic x(4).
        03 alpha-part             pic x.

       move "123 " to numeric-part
123456 if numeric-part numeric then
         display "numeric"
       else
         display "not numeric"
       end-if

       move "123 " to numeric-part
       if numeric-part not numeric then
         display "not numeric"
       else
         display "numeric"
       end-if

Regards,
Steve.
```

```
To William M Klein and Steven Lalewicz,
 Thanks for the reply, heres the section of code in question :-
CODE-CHECK.
           MOVE 0 TO WS-SUM
               if numeric-cust not numeric then
                   move "Y" to ws-error-flag
                   move numeric-cust to w-errcust
                   move rec-type to w-errtype
                   move spaces to w-errpart
                   move 0 to w-errbal
                   move 0 to w-errlim
                   move spaces to w-errnam
                   move "1" to w-errcode
                   perform write-error-report
               else
                   MOVE CUST-CODE TO WS-CUST-CODE
                   PERFORM VARYING Ws-d-sub FROM 1 BY 1
                           UNTIL Ws-d-sub > 4
                           MULTIPLY W-CUST-DGT(Ws-d-sub) BY
                                   W-cust-weight(Ws-d-sub) GIVING
                                   WS-PRODUCT
                           ADD WS-PRODUCT TO WS-SUM
                   END-PERFORM
                :
                :
                end-if.
In my original If statement construct the "move cust-code to ws-cust-code"
section was the first consideration ie if numeric-cust numeric then ......
  However, using this construct the else part was never executed, even when
I used non numeric test data. So upon changing the coding of the if
statement to that above, the if statement "traps" the non-numeric test data.
The statement works now, but why. and why did it not work before when I
coded essentially the same test, only the opposite way around?
***Please note that this program I am working on is for a project which
forms part of an exam so I obviously dont want exact answers, only a gentle
nudge in the right direction. Thanks***
  Barney.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric testing

- **From:** jacksleight@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-02-04T18:48:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <95cbpn$v7p$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <95g5kf$ear$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi Barney,

I think the key to the solution is, as Bill Klein states, is to look at
the hex representation of numeric-cust just before the numeric test. I
seem to remember that, (in a mainframe environment, anyway) the test
delivers differing results depending on the PIC of the subject field.
You may want to redefine your PIC X field to PIC 9 (or COMP-3 if thats
what you're expecting as numeric) and do your test against that field.

Hope this helps. Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric testing

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 2001-02-05T01:33:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a7e0125.274686921@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <95cbpn$v7p$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <95g5kf$ear$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <95k86g$8d9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
jacksleight@my-deja.com schrieb:

>the hex representation of numeric-cust just before the numeric test. I
>seem to remember that, (in a mainframe environment, anyway) the test
>delivers differing results depending on the PIC of the subject field.
>You may want to redefine your PIC X field to PIC 9 (or COMP-3 if thats
>what you're expecting as numeric) and do your test against that field.

In a MicroFocus environment, NUMERIC testing can only be done on an
alphanumeric field since the runtime tests a numeric field on numeric
contents before accessing it (runtime error 163).

This test can be disabled with the -F switch, but as a nonnumeric
value in a numeric field almost always implies a logical error in the
program, I don't use this switch in my developming environment.
Therefore I test for the NUMERIC condition on alphanumeric fields
only.

Best regards

Sven
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

```
On Mon, 05 Feb 2001 01:33:35 GMT SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
wrote:

:>jacksleight@my-deja.com schrieb:

:>>the hex representation of numeric-cust just before the numeric test. I

:>>delivers differing results depending on the PIC of the subject field.
:>>You may want to redefine your PIC X field to PIC 9 (or COMP-3 if thats
:>>what you're expecting as numeric) and do your test against that field.

:>In a MicroFocus environment, NUMERIC testing can only be done on an
:>alphanumeric field since the runtime tests a numeric field on numeric
:>contents before accessing it (runtime error 163).

I assume that you mean before using it as a number.

:>This test can be disabled with the -F switch, but as a nonnumeric
:>value in a numeric field almost always implies a logical error in the
:>program, I don't use this switch in my developming environment.
:>Therefore I test for the NUMERIC condition on alphanumeric fields
:>only.

A non-numeric in a numeric field can be an data entry error as well.

Don't you think it is better for the program logic to handle the case rather
than letting the program crash?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric testing

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T14:52:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a86a1c5.496574210@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <95cbpn$v7p$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <95g5kf$ear$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <95k86g$8d9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3a7e0125.274686921@news.btx.dtag.de> <b1rs7tkkhj8cf8khndl46b5l1cvsv5rjg2@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> schrieb:

>:>In a MicroFocus environment, NUMERIC testing can only be done on an
>:>alphanumeric field since the runtime tests a numeric field on numeric
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>than letting the program crash?

I do it this way to avoid a crash.

01 GROUP-ITEM.
     05 NUM-ITEM  PIC 9(8).

MOVE "ABCDEFGH" TO GROUP-ITEM
IF NUM-ITEM NOT NUMERIC
    DISPLAY "Bingo"
END-IF

With numeric checking in the runtime system enabled (+F) the program
will crash in the IF-sentence with error 163 (non-numeric data in
numeric field).

That's why I code 

IF GROUP-ITEM NOT NUMERIC 

or

IF NUM-ITEM(1:8) NOT NUMERIC

This way, I can do explicit numeric checking on items that are more
likely to be non-numeric (for example incorrectly filled incoming
interface files) and handle these errors and can leave the runtime
checking enabled so I can see if I have miscoded something.

Best regards


Sven
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric testing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-05T07:35:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A7EBA31.C2DD93FF@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <95cbpn$v7p$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <95g5kf$ear$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <95k86g$8d9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3a7e0125.274686921@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Sven Brueggemann wrote:

> In a MicroFocus environment, NUMERIC testing can only be done on an
> alphanumeric field since the runtime tests a numeric field on numeric
> contents before accessing it (runtime error 163).

This is something which should be fixed in the CoBOL standards.  All
numeric testing should be done against the PIC 9 fields.  The compiler
should be smart enough to use the picture to determine whether the data are
correct.   The following code is ugly:

10   BILL-TOTAL-ALPHA   PIC X(8).
10   BILL-TOTAL       REDEFINES BILL-TOTAL-ALPHA PIC S9(7)V99.

...
IF BILL-TOTAL-ALPHA (1:7) IS NUMERIC

Signed numeric, packed, floating, etc.. numbers can all redefine the same
space, we can't guess correctly which redefines you are really checking for
by checking an alphanumeric field for IF NUMERIC, when what you really want
is to avoid a data exception when you do arithmetic.  So the standard
SHOULD be for a way to test the numeric field for validity.   Some
compilers do this, others do not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric testing

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T03:59:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a821965.44718773@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <95cbpn$v7p$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <95g5kf$ear$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <95k86g$8d9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3a7e0125.274686921@news.btx.dtag.de> <3A7EBA31.C2DD93FF@brazee.net>`

```
And..... IT IS!

The draft standard addresses this with an "incompatible data"
exception.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric testing

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T00:16:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9601v7$fqh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <95cbpn$v7p$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <95g5kf$ear$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <95k86g$8d9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3a7e0125.274686921@news.btx.dtag.de> <3A7EBA31.C2DD93FF@brazee.net> <3a821965.44718773@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Actually, the draft standard has another (more relevant) enhancement.  It
explicitly allows you to do an IF NUMERIC test against non-alphanumeric
fields. The "gotcha" (as far as many people go) is

 01 Group-Item.
  05 NumField  Pic 9 Binary.
      ...
  Move X'FF' to Group-Item.
  If NumField not Numeric
     Display "This is executed - as the data does not match the PICTURE"
  End-If
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric testing

- **From:** piasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-02-05T11:32:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95m30d$hf8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<95astv$ruv$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Try the following definition:
>  01 Customer-code.
>         03 numeric-part         pic x(4).
          03 nemeric-part-9  redefines numeric-part
                                  pic 9(4).
>         03 alpha-part             pic x.

Don't test pic x(4) for numeric, test pic 9(4) for numeric.  I have
seen similar results when testing a social security number.  This will
allow you to move a non numeric into numeric-part and also allow any
numberic calculation on the numeric-part-9.

change the
```

```
I would suggest checking out the comp.software.config-mgmt newsgroup. I wide
range of views on different tools. Also be aware that at least 3 vendors
that I am aware of are moving towards solutions which span the mainframe and
NT platforms:
- Rational (with IBM) : ClearQuest/ClearCase
- Serena : ChangeMan/eChangeMan
- Computer Associates : Harvest/Endevor

The biggest thing is that you need to clearly define what you really need.
There are actually a lot of tools and they have various strengths.
```

```
"Timothy" <timhillock@home.com> wrote:
...
> I don't remember the exact dates, but it was in 1582, September when
> Rome adopted the current calendar.  I think it went from September 12
> (say a Monday) to the next day (Tuesday) of September 23, 1582.
> People had thought that they had lost 10 days of their life. 
...
Depending on what you mean by adopted...
The Papal Bull that decreed it would happen was in February 1582.  The
effective date of the change was the day following October 4, 1582.  The
date 1582-10-04 was followed by 1582-10-15 (in those countries that
accepted the Pope's decision), so day 1 of the Gregorian calendar was
October 15, 1582.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T11:58:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010201065801.14933.00000249@ng-ff1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<53gc6.1826$OP4.79748@news1.atl>`

```

On Fri, 26 Jan 2001 08:44:20 -0600
the ever guiding light
"Judson McClendon", judmc@bellsouth.net 
said
<<
Just because it wasn't *called* "Year 1" at the time, doesn't mean
there wasn't a "Year 1"!  Zero existed since the beginning of the
time, but man didn't recognize and name such it until relatively
recently. ;-)
>>

In Truth it can be argued, and I emphasize, has been argued, that zero never
existed, does not exist and will never exist. And 'though such anti-nihility
seem infinitely silly to us in our culture, as diverse as our intillectual
heritage might be; it was a very serious matter to ancient Greek philosophy.

Most modern text that will get into the issue seem to indicate that the Greeks
in fact had encountered the notion of zero, or nothingness, but they did not
trust it.

Which I suppose should be logged as the greatest of paranoia - to be affraid of
nothing, you see.

The gist of the historical arguement, per the Greeks, was that they could
easily have had a zero-cipher, but felt no particular need for a symbol to
represent that which they did not believe to be real.

All the exploiters of the Semitic alphabet system which led to the Greek
alphabet, actually did have a zero-cipher even though moderns and ancients
would insist otherwise! They used the Semitic Alphabet in a positional
notation. This is not so obvious at first since the set of nine ciphers for
each non-zero value would be different as you increased in magnitude (until the
27 had been exhausted, and then they would recycle the characterset).  

You see, zero would be represented by the absence of any cipher at all in the
position. The blank spot would be closed up (a kind of kerning if you don't
mind the light-hearted treatment). Thus the positional absence of the cipher
_WAS_ the symbolic representation of zero. And it was the same for each
potential position.

It is not so strange, really. Consider modern marketting moniker like
"bellsouth" and "earthlink". Or the easily grokked middle node of this web
address
 www. economictechnology.com
In all of these the characted-nil, or blank is vacumed out of there, and we can
still get it.

Naturally, Judson's, original comments are much broader than my meager focus on
the Greek/Semitic symbol conventions.






So the concept of zero was surely there quite early, as transparent as the
representation might have been.



Robert Rayhawk
rayhawk@alum.calberkeley.org
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T00:37:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Liam Devlin LiamD@optonline.net 
Date: Fri, 02 Feb 2001 06:18:21 GMT
posted
<< 
"This page intentionally left blank"
>>

Well, true this is a manipulation of zeroes too. You see, ... the loggers, pulp
processing corporations, printing companies, book sellers ... all benefit from
the extra zeroes that get attached to the end of the invoices for all the extra
content.

I guess that we are not to belittle such things, afterall there are web pages
elaborated decorated as "Under Construction" and some programmer somewhere got
a fee for that, as did the seller of the icons and generic content that allows
such presentation.

And when it comes to clearing things I confess to have coded explicit move
zeroes and move spaces, rather than using INITIALIZE and surely the ever more
important LOCs went right up ... but I thought I was doing it to make the code
clear. Seems like clearing code should be clear. :-)

Yet you know, we COBOLers need to catchup with the new world; to build the
webserver equivalent of a 404 error we should have an automatic text field that
reads

 "This page UNintentionally left blank",

which field will default to having foreground and background colors equal. And
we could then generalize this to be the quintessential example of zepher cipher
text. We could then claim that COBOL is better.
 











Liam Devlin LiamD@optonline.net 
Date: Fri, 02 Feb 2001 06:18:21 GMT
Robert Rayhawk
rayhawk@alum.calberkeley.org
```

<a id="t-dcdfbe3fb1"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress, CGI, and Environment Variable Question — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### NetExpress, CGI, and Environment Variable Question

- **From:** "Progger" <karturner@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T08:41:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9865AC34C35B30EC.0A509C17C1B0D555.71DD4FFD943AD6FE@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Help please.   Is it possible to set a value to a CGI environment variable?
How about a regular variable or parameter?  If so, how?  Thanks for any help
you can offer.

Karen
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress, CGI, and Environment Variable Question

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T14:24:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Skee6.35999$KP3.9856678@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<9865AC34C35B30EC.0A509C17C1B0D555.71DD4FFD943AD6FE@lp.airnews.net>`

```
You may want to read the reference material on Internet programming that
ships with NetExpress. It's quite thorough.

Normally CGI environment variables are set by the http server and read by
the CGI application.
If you're using environment variables to pass information to subprocesses
then there are more efficient mechanisms you should be considering  However,
if you really want to set environment variables then NetExpress supports the
X/Open extensions.

To set the value of the environment variable my_env_var

DISPLAY "my_env_var" UPON ENVIRONMENT-NAME
DISPLAY my-env-val  UPON ENVIRONMENT-VALUE

To read an environment variable:

DISPLAY "my_env_var" UPON ENVIRONMENT-NAME
ACCEPT my-env-val FROM ENVIRONMENT-VALUE

I have no idea what you mean by "regular variable or parameter". COBOL
variables are set using instructions like MOVE, STRING, UNSTRING, COMPUTE,
ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPY, DIVIDE, ACCEPT, READ, etc. ;-)

- Karl



"Progger" <karturner@aol.com> wrote in message
news:9865AC34C35B30EC.0A509C17C1B0D555.71DD4FFD943AD6FE@lp.airnews.net...
> Help please.   Is it possible to set a value to a CGI environment
variable?
> How about a regular variable or parameter?  If so, how?  Thanks for any
help
> you can offer.
>
> Karen
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress, CGI, and Environment Variable Question

- **From:** "Progger" <turner_karen@mail.chattanooga.gov>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T12:06:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3154FC593316E601.D9A537C4B04431C9.452EAF702A0C2F8C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<9865AC34C35B30EC.0A509C17C1B0D555.71DD4FFD943AD6FE@lp.airnews.net> <Skee6.35999$KP3.9856678@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Thanks.
This is not internet related.  This is the situation that I have (I will try
to be brief)....

In 1997, we migrated from our in house accounting and purchasing
applications from IBM to SCT BANNER application on the Windows 95 (for user
interface) and VMS (for Oracle db and reports and processes).  If not
familiar, SCT BANNER is a outside package.  The user interface is
Developer/Designer 2000 and reports and process are C and Cobol which are
ran from VMS batch files.  Since then we have written a couple hundred in
house Cobol programs.

Now we have migrated Banner to NT.  The only thing left of accounting and
purchasing that is still running on VMS are the couple hundred in house
cobol programs.  The goal it to get them off of VMS.  Management has chosed
Microfocus NetExpress for the Cobol and Perl for the job/batch file.  I
believe Perl was chosen because that is what Banner uses.

ALL of our cobol programs pass data back and forth to the batch files
running them by getting symbols and setting symbols.  Our jobs have to be
integrated with the Banner application (submitted and ran in the same way as
Banner baseline jobs).  To make them work with Banner's submission screens
with little or no modification to Banner programs our in house jobs have to
be able to get and set Perl variables that are available.  I understand that
this is the way that Banner is doing it.

We haven't actually started converting our programs.  I am just gathering
information.  I have an assignment to do the first program, but right now I
have other pressing assignments.  I am sure to have many, many questions as
time goes by.  Any insight that anyone can provide will be greatly
appreciated.
```

<a id="t-c1b5fb8db7"></a>
## Thread: Syntax Question (arrays) — 1 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [2001-01](2001-01.md#t-c1b5fb8db7)._

#### ↳ Re: Syntax Question (arrays)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T07:24:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A797196.3EB3B565@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3a78b93b_2@spamkiller.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Don wrote:

> If I have an array of 50 elements in COBOL, the first element is accessed as
> ARRAY(1) and the 50th element is ARRAY(50).  Can I change the subscripting
> so the first element of the array starts at subscript 8200, and the 50th
> element is ARRAY(8249)?  I could do it in PLI.  Is there a way to do it in
> IBM mainframe COBOL 370?

I've always wanted this ability.  There are times when it fits the business
model well.  The most common way around this is to modify the subscript e.g.
ARRAY(MY-INDEX - 8240).

A couple of times, I have simply wasted some space by starting at 1, but not
using the first x (in your case the first 8199) occurrences.  There is a trade
off here depending on how much space you're wasting, and how such wasted space
effects performance on your system.
```

---

<a id="t-c9fe456d76"></a>
## Thread: ODBC Access with Relativity on NT4.0 — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

_Thread started [2001-01](2001-01.md#t-c9fe456d76)._

#### ↳ Re: ODBC Access with Relativity on NT4.0

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T09:24:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Sdfe6.49$yJ1.51@client>`
- **References:** `<959feb$j92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<atelier_america@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:959feb$j92$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi there,
> I need some help or answer on performance issue
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> users.

Your most likely problem is Access itself.

Access has its own database engine, and it thereby has its own 'thoughts' on
how to do queries.  If a table has a unique index, Access tends to get a
keyset for the entire table before it actually does a query.  The only
mechanism I have found to defeat this is the use of Passthrough queries.

If you have have MS-Query installed (e.g. for use by Excel or Word), try
using it to build and test your queries.  MS-Query is not nearly as
'intelligent' as Access and can be a lot less frustrating.  When you have a
query behaving as you wish, cut the SQL from the SQL window in MS-Query and
paste it into the SQL window in a new Access query, mark the query as
passthrough, and Access should behave better.

Of course, it _is_ possible to construct queries that cannot use the indices
available, but I think this is not your current problem.

(By the way, I am actively working on a new query for our accounting
department that is exhibiting this symptom.  The query even uses our Order
Header table!)

>
> We tried to install both the mapped drive version
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> run ODBC when users are still online.

This makes sense.  The SQL engine is running on the server in C/S, but on
the workstation in the 'mapped drive' version.  The workstation version will
cause a lot more network traffic (thereby four times slower) due to the fact
that it is moving individual disk I/O requests across the network.  On the
C/S version, only the result set is moved over the network.

>
> I suspect there may be something to do with
> locking but I am not sure because I cannot call
> Liant directly since my Relativity is OEM through
> my COBOL application.

Actually, unless your query is updating tables, there is not any locking
going on.  Through private email, please forward the name of the application
vendor, so that we may do some proactive support.

>
> Hope someone can share their experience with me.
> And if somebody in Liant is monitoring this site,
> please help.

A Relativity developer, no less.  :-)

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Access with Relativity on NT4.0

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T09:53:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Ffe6.50$yJ1.356@client>`
- **References:** `<959feb$j92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Sdfe6.49$yJ1.51@client>`

```
Additional info...

Tom Morrison <t.morrison@liant.com> wrote in message
news:Sdfe6.49$yJ1.51@client...
> <atelier_america@my-deja.com> wrote in message
> news:959feb$j92$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
[snip]
>
> If you have have MS-Query installed (e.g. for use by Excel or Word), try
> using it to build and test your queries.  MS-Query is not nearly as
> 'intelligent' as Access and can be a lot less frustrating.  When you have
a
> query behaving as you wish, cut the SQL from the SQL window in MS-Query
and
> paste it into the SQL window in a new Access query, mark the query as
> passthrough, and Access should behave better.

If you really want to test only Relativity and get everything else out of
the way, download the Microsoft Data Access Component SDK (13 Meg) and
install it (choose custom, and eliminate everything except ODBC).  This
contains a program called ODBC Test  (ODBCTE32.EXE) that allows testing
right at the ODBC interface level.  No pretty query-by-example, though.

This download also contains the ODBC documentation.  This might be helpful
if you are doing pass through queries from Access - the ODBC and Access SQL
grammars are not identical.

[snip]

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

### terminfo, linux and rmcobol

- **From:** "Paulo" <paulo@NOSPAM100limite.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T16:24:16
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<981044772.703860@tubarao.ip.pt>`

```
Hi !

Does anyone have a working terminfo file working with linux ? i can't get
special chars from the runtime (specialty, the Portuguese chars), or how can
i do it ... i've read the manual, several experiments, etc ... and it
driving me crazy ...

Help apreciated.

Thank you in advance

A desesperated Cobol programmer :)

Paulo
```

#### ↳ Re: terminfo, linux and rmcobol

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T02:14:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010202211420.27968.00000524@ng-md1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<981044772.703860@tubarao.ip.pt>`

```
Paul wrote a reference to
<<
...  terminfo file ...(specialty, the Portuguese chars),
>>

The advice you received about proving it on the command line first is good
advice. As a small additional hint: consider carefully what you mean by the
"Portuguese chars", If the enye, or non-English "American" puncutation marks
are of interest to you (what we silly folks would call the upside down question
mark and upside down exclamation mark) then these may have a code in Extended
ASCII of the IBM PC universe of 127 or higher (that is, a value of 128 requires
the eight bit to be on). 

Some UNIX software may damage such encodings (although I would be surprised if
that survives into LINIX, and would welcome comment from more knowledgable
folks on this as a general issue).

If there is anywhere the 7-bit ASCII code legacy would be most likely to
surface it would be in the telecommunications functions.

The word American in the title American Standard Code for Information
Interchange is somewhat bigotted, being heavily 'English' oriented. And though
there is both French and Spanish on the Northern continent as well as Portugese
on the Southern continent; the original code always needed workarounds for the
additional non-English ciphers.

The real truth is this simply lead to mispellings which no doubt were insulting
to some.

You may also want to give consideration to what will happen at the other end
(outside of your LINUX box) if the terminfo is in any way to actually be
transmitted. Your LINUX telecom software might soar like the grandest eagle
(once configured correctly) but transmitted ciphers could get masked to 7-bits
on the wires or masked to 7-bits at the receiving end (especially if the remote
site has older hardware or older OSes).

If it is not such sensitive information, could you tell us what fields you are
encoding that leads you to use problematic ciphers in the terminfo file.

Someday we will all go to Unicode and this kind of thing will ease up, but the
"A"SCII text config files will be the last trees to fall.

A minor footnote, in some cases the Extended ASCII ciphers encoded with value
128 through 159 get transformed into the ASCII control characters 0 through 31
by errant 7-bit mask operations. Thus, you have particular ciphers that might
be more than just mangled into other characters, but will instead transform
into telecommunications control characters.






Robert Rayhawk
rayhawk@alum.calberkeley.org
```

## Thread: Another pointer question. — 2 msgs

### Another pointer question.

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T10:48:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rtge6.1283$9r5.206995@news1.primary.net>`

```
In Microfocus cobol how does someone setup a pointer that is return to them
from a C DLL where the pointer points to an array.

Thanks
Tom Wright
```

#### ↳ Re: Another pointer question.

- **From:** "Jacques Vidal" <jvidal@mail.dotcom.fr>
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T19:53:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95evsm$er9$1@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<rtge6.1283$9r5.206995@news1.primary.net>`

```

Tom Wright <twright@@larimore.net> a �crit dans le message :
rtge6.1283$9r5.206995@news1.primary.net...
> In Microfocus cobol how does someone setup a pointer that is return to
them
> from a C DLL where the pointer points to an array.
>
> Thanks
> Tom Wright

If I understand what you mean correctly, it should look like this:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    77    MY-DATA-POINTER    USAGE    POINTER.
    ...
LINKAGE SECTION.
    01    MY-DATA-ARRAY.
            02    MY-DATA    <picture clause> <occurs clause>. // to keep it
simple
    ...
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    ...
    CALL <call convention if needed> "cfunc" USING BY REFERENCE
MY-DATA-POINTER.
    SET ADDRESS OF MY-DATA-ARRAY TO MY-DATA-POINTER.
    ...

From now on,  you can access your array elements with MY-DATA (n). Depending
on what your array contains, you may have to be careful when you define
MY-DATA for alignment reasons. See the  h2cpy utility (it comes packaged
with the mF compiler) if your data is too much complex.

Hope it helps,

Jacques Vidal
```

```
MVS dataset access can be done from server-side programs written in Cobol,
Visual Basic, C++, or even used from VBA applications using C-Cubed products.

directly from the PC.  Check out  www.c-cubed.net
```

```
Hello everybody,

Our shop is considering moving to IBM's Visual Age Cobol product and I
was wondering if any of you had any experience of using it. We
currently develop our code using Microfocus Workbench and sometimes on
the mainframe in TSO. Have any of you used Visual Age Cobol? Is it any
good? Is it better than Microfocus Workbench? On the mainframe we run
our jobs through IMS and access all of our data via a DB2 database.

Thank you.

Jimmy.


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

```
Jimmy,

Before you scrap your investment in Micro Focus, you might take a look at what
C-Cubed has to offer.  They offer transparent dataset , subroutine, IMS, IDMS,
and Datacom access from Animator, using Workbench (v3, v4) MFE and NetExpress.
It's just as if all of your data exists on the PC, but it accesses it from MVS.
No source code changes are necessary, it's very simple to use, and best of all -
it's cheap in price.  They have been doing these kinds of things since 1995, so
they have a proven track record.

Check out www.c-cubed.net for more information ...

Hope it helps - TL
```

- **Date:** 2001-02-02T18:49:17+00:00

```
ghItlh jimroyle@my-deja.com 

>Hello everybody,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy.

I found the VA environment quite hideous. Extremley slow. Chunks
(all?) of it are written in Java and its ever so chuggy at times.
Awful, Awful interface. Hate it.

ISTR IBM recommend at least 128MB to run the compiler/IDE and I have
to agree.

The COBOL compatibility with the OS/390 COBOL is extremley high as you
might expect; This might be important to you.

I like VA COBOL, however. I mainly used Makefiles and called the
commad line tools from there to build projects from within EMACS so I
didn't really notice the IDE frailties beyond my initial shock.
```

## Thread: NetExpress and Form variables — 2 msgs

### NetExpress and Form variables

- **From:** mrtonyr@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T20:22:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95cght$9ec$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am having a little trouble with image submit buttons using NetExpress
2.0. I need to use 3 submit buttons to submit one form and be able to
tell which button was used to submit the form. All my resources say
that you can use multiple submit buttons with different value fields
and the cgi program will be passed the value of the button that is
used. It is supposed to work with both submit buttons and input images.
Everything works ok when I use regular buttons. I need to use inputs of
type image because people will be clicking on logos and whenever I do,
I do not get the form value. Here is the code:

        01 IN-FORM IS EXTERNAL-FORM.
            02 IN-SUBMIT        PIC X(3)    IDENTIFIED BY "DLSUBMIT".

        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            INITIALIZE IN-FORM.
            ACCEPT IN-FORM..
            DISPLAY IN-SUBMIT.
            STOP RUN.

In the html file it looks like this:
     <td> &nbsp <input name=DLSUBMIT type=image value='qif'
           src=../images/download_button.jpg> </td>

The form is being submitted properly because there are other variables
that are being passed. It works fine if I change the above line to read:
     <td> &nbsp <input type=submit name=DLSUBMIT value='qif'></td>

But everything I have read says that either should work. Any
suggestions or is this a NetExpress thing? Is there any way to capture
and display the whole String that the browser sends the program, or are
we stuck with the way NetExpress puts it into the input form?
Thanks,

Tony



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and Form variables

- **From:** "David Sands" <david.sands@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T16:14:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961a3m$l5r$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<95cght$9ec$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi Tony,

I don't think that image buttons actually submit a value to the server. It
submits the x and y coords of where the user clicked in the image.

You could try something like:-

        01 IN-FORM IS EXTERNAL-FORM.
            02 IN-SUBMITX        PIC X(10)    IDENTIFIED BY "DLSUBMIT.x".
            02 IN-SUBMITY        PIC X(10)    IDENTIFIED BY "DLSUBMIT.y".

Rgds
David.
```

```
Following up on Don's suggestion I have, (rarely, but recently) got a file lock
when testing. I resolved it by exiting COBOL. You may find if you try to 'view'
it outside of COBOL you may get an 'access denied' - it's a bugger, but do a
Windows Restart if you are in a Windows environment.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

_Thread started [2001-01](2001-01.md#t-7325882950)._

#### ↳ Re: SIZE ERROR, Truncation, and what the ANSI/ISO Standard says

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T21:51:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cpe6.30030$Ch.6774819@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<956npf$g4d$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, isn't what happens, tho' (from what I've seen) is that (at least when
adding)
you get   1.  (the actual result of the operation).

I think 'undefined' means - the standard does not define the result and the
compiler write is free to do what it wants (just like closing a File with
data in the
01 - some Vendors on other systems leave the rec alone, IBM ensures it is
unusable.

Of course, when EVER the possibility of underflow/overflow (AKA
Truncation/Size error)
a careful COBOL coder, uses the ON SIZE ERROR.


William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:956npf$g4d$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> Given another "ongoing" thread, I thought that I would quote EXACTLY what
> the ANSI/ISO Standard currently says about the fact that TRUNCATION is
*not*
> a part of arithmetic operations when no ON SIZE ERROR phrase is specified
> (or when it is specified - for that matter).  The following is from Page
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the program will continue and the value of any affected resultant
identifier
> is undefined.
>
> This substantive change was introduced by third Standard COBOL; however,
it
> was not identified as a substantive change until amendment 2. Therefore,
it
> has been listed here and as item 45 in paragraph 2.2, Substantive Changes
> Potentially Affecting Existing Programs (Third Standard COBOL).
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> continue execution after performing the statement in paragraph
STATEMENT-1.
> However, some implementations terminated execution during execution of the
> statement in paragraph STATEMENT-2. In third Standard COBOL, the rules
have
> been clarified to insure that the program will continue execution after
both
> of these statements. Furthermore, the rules now explicitly state that the
> value in DIGIT2 will be defined as 10 after executing STATEMENT-1. On the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SIZE ERROR, Truncation, and what the ANSI/ISO Standard says

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T07:40:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95ede0$q20$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<956npf$g4d$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <7cpe6.30030$Ch.6774819@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
What you are talking about is "implementor defined" - which is a different
category (in ANSI-speak) than "undefined".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SIZE ERROR, Truncation, and what the ANSI/ISO Standard says

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T13:24:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95eu7q$2qej$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<956npf$g4d$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <7cpe6.30030$Ch.6774819@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
Keeping in mind that the COBOL standard is the definition of
a programming language, "undefined" means the standard
imposes no requirement on the implementor to do anything
more to conform to the standard. This is not the same as,
"the compiler write is free to do what it wants".

In fact, the rules for what constitutes a conforming
implementation may actually prevent an implementor from
adding rules (to their conforming compiler) where the standard
uses the word "undefined."
------------------
Rick Smith

mangogrower wrote in message
<7cpe6.30030$Ch.6774819@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>...
[...]
>I think 'undefined' means - the standard does not define the result and the
>compiler write is free to do what it wants [...]
>
>William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:956npf$g4d$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
[...]
>> "(8) Size error condition without ON SIZE ERROR phrase (1 NUC). If a size
>> error condition occurs and an ON SIZE ERROR phrase has not been
specified,
>> the program will continue and the value of any affected resultant
>identifier
>> is undefined.
[...]
```

```
> GRPNAME will be good with something like a date.  A GRPNAME of
INVOICE-DATE
> followed by the MM, DD, CCYY fields will allow editing of each individual
piece
> with the attribute assigned to INVOICE-DATE.
>
> However I think the complaint was with the situation of having a line on a
> screen consisting of several different fields repeated multiple times
like:
>
> part number, quantity, price
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the OCCURS cannot be used in this instance because all the fields in the
OCCURS
> must be the same.  If you needed 25 part numbers in contiguous sequence
then
> OCCURS can be used. However you cannot do an OCCURS on the set of 3
different
> fields.
>
> Since GRPNAME and OCCURS are mutually exclusive you are stuck coding each
field
> separately.
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T22:39:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aVpe6.30196$Ch.6809351@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg> <3a5c94d6.53193107@news>`

```
FINALLY, someone pointed out to the newbie/curious that each step that
EXECUTES, gets a RC, either from the program or the system.

Please note, here 'curious',

When the Program Does Not Have Control

For the system to act on the COND parameter, the step must abnormally
terminate while the program has  control. If a step abnormally terminates
during scheduling, due to failures such as JCL errors or the inability to
allocate space, the system bypasses the remaining steps, no matter what the
COND parameter requests.





Kevin J. Hansen <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote in message
news:3a5c94d6.53193107@news...
> On Wed, 10 Jan 2001 22:49:01 +0800, "Curious" <leng1@bigfoot.com>
> wrote:
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.screenio.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-02-01T22:46:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g%pe6.30210$Ch.6813507@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg> <3a5c94d6.53193107@news>`

```
Incomplete Post!

Here's the rest
--------------------------------------------------------------------------
OS/390
MVS JCL Reference
Document Number GC28-1757-04
Program Number
5647-A01
File Number S370/S390-36

After certain types of abnormal termination by the system, remaining job
steps are not executed, regardless  of whether EVEN or ONLY were specified.
The completion codes associated with these types of abnormal termination
are:

122  Operator canceled job
222  Operator or TSO/E user canceled job

You might encounter other system completion codes for which remaining job
steps are not executed,
regardless of whether EVEN or ONLY was specified. See OS/390 MVS System
Codes for further
information about specific system completion codes.


If a step abnormally terminates, the system normally bypasses all following
steps in the job unless the step(s) are part of an IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF
construct that specifies the ABEND, ABENDCC, or �ABEND keywords, described
in Chapter 17,  "IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF Statement Construct" in topic 17.0.
Another way to make the system execute a following step, for instance, to
write a dump, is to code EVEN or ONLY on that step's EXEC statement. The
EVEN or ONLY subparameters are
interpreted first. If they indicate that the step should be executed, then
the return code tests, if specified, are performed. If no return code tests
were coded or if none of the coded tests is satisfied, the system executes
the step.


Kevin J. Hansen <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote in message
news:3a5c94d6.53193107@news...
> On Wed, 10 Jan 2001 22:49:01 +0800, "Curious" <leng1@bigfoot.com>
> wrote:
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.screenio.com
```

## Thread: PowerCOBOL access use of MS Common Dialog — 5 msgs

### PowerCOBOL access use of MS Common Dialog

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T04:00:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010201230021.21405.00001899@nso-mj.aol.com>`

```
I am looking for a sample PowerCOBOL project that makes use of 
the MS Common Dialog control for File Open/Save processing,
Font selection, and Printer Control.
This is not a homework assignment.  I just haven't spent the time with
the GUI side of what FujiCOBOL has to offer.  If there is a site other 
than the adtools.com web-site that I might be able to peruse, please 
point me in that direction.
Thanks to all for your time and assistance.
```

#### ↳ Re: PowerCOBOL access use of MS Common Dialog

- **From:** alain.reymond-nospam@ceia.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T10:29:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a7a8bba.1155151@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<20010201230021.21405.00001899@nso-mj.aol.com>`

```
Have a look at the 'powercobol common dialog 6' thread in this
newsgroup. This was a few days ago and we discussed this problem.

Here is part of an example :

Thank you for your indications. I immediately discovered what you
explain in your second paragraph. I am unable to control if the user
pressed OK or Cancel except using the return-code status.

First activate the CancelError property. 
Then, call the common dialog and immediately after, control the
return-code status. If the user selected Ok, the return-code value is
0, if he selected Cancel, the return-code is not set to 0.

The only problem is that you first receive a warning message :
"CommonDialog. Cancel was selected."
Just press Ok and ignore the message.

It seems to work. Hereunder is a example:

 01  ReturnValue         PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
 01  DisplayReturnValue1  pic +9999999999.
 01  DisplayReturnValue2  pic +9999999999.
 01  STYL    PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  M-TITLE PIC X(15) VALUE "Message".
 01  RET     PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  str     pic x(80).
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.

     COMPUTE STYL = POW-DMOK + POW-DMNOICON
     
     move POW-True to "CancelError" of CommonDialog1
     INVOKE CommonDialog1 "ShowOpen"
     move return-code to DisplayReturnValue2
     move "CancelError" of CommonDialog1 to DisplayReturnValue1
     string 
     	"CancelError : "
     	DisplayReturnValue1
     	" return-code : "
     	DisplayReturnValue2
     	delimited by size
     	into str
     end-string
     INVOKE POW-SELF "DisplayMessage"
         USING str M-TITLE STYL
         RETURNING RET


>I am looking for a sample PowerCOBOL project that makes use of 
>the MS Common Dialog control for File Open/Save processing,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks to all for your time and assistance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PowerCOBOL access use of MS Common Dialog

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T04:17:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rDLe6.635$kz4.80309@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3a7a8bba.1155151@news.skynet.be> <20010202113736.06978.00001413@nso-fd.aol.com>`

```
You really don't need examples.

Once you get the control placed on the form with a name of, say,
"CommonDiaglog1", then type CommonDiaglog1 in your program, highlight
the name, and right-click. You are immediately presented with all
possible methods and properties (after clicking one more time).

But here's an example:

   MOVE POW-TRUE TO 'CancelError' of CommonDialog1.
   INVOKE COMMONDIALOG1 "ShowOpen"
   MOVE "FileName" OF commondialog1 TO FILEID.
   MOVE 'CancelError' OF COMMONDIALOG1 TO RETURN-VALUE.
```

## Thread: MS/MF Cobol 16bit and Windows NT 4.0 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MS/MF Cobol 16bit and Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** "Frank.Saur" <Frank.Saur@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T11:44:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95e2s7$gbd$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi,

i have installed the Microsoft / Micro Focus V 4.5 Cobol Software (16-bit)
on Windows NT 4.0. If I start the compiler i get an error message:

Cobol run time library not installed

If i boot good old DOS (same environment-variables set as in nt) it works.

Can anyone tell me what I should try?

Thanks

Frank
```

#### ↳ Re: MS/MF Cobol 16bit and Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T10:39:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hqBe6.1338$9r5.214917@news1.primary.net>`
- **References:** `<95e2s7$gbd$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Are you sure that your environment variables are getting set int NT.
Microfocus sets the variables according to the user profile.  If you
installed MF under one user and try to run it under another it may not work.
Try moving the environment variables to the system variables that way
they'll be loaded no matter who logs in.  You can check to see if the
environment variables are being set in both by going to a DOS prompt in NT
and typing SET.  If the info scrolls off the screen the type SET |(<--also
known as a pipe)MORE that should list a page at a time.  Look to see that
the variables are being set.

Hope this helps
Tom
"Frank.Saur" <Frank.Saur@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:95e2s7$gbd$07$1@news.t-online.com...
> Hi,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: MS/MF Cobol 16bit and Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T09:05:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A7BC9EC.2A07E039@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95e2s7$gbd$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Frank.Saur wrote:
> i have installed the Microsoft / Micro Focus V 4.5 Cobol Software (16-bit)
> on Windows NT 4.0. If I start the compiler i get an error message:
> 
> Cobol run time library not installed

MS Cobol 4.5 has real mode (DOS) and protected mode (OS/2) run times. 
When started it finds protected mode available and tries to load the
OS/2 version.  This is failing.  One way around this is to use FORCEDOS
to start it ensuring only the real mode is detected.

Isn't this in the FAQ ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MS/MF Cobol 16bit and Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** juergen_ibelgaufts <juergen.ibelgaufts@copewithcytokines.de>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T12:54:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A7BF187.A5FCF1E4@copewithcytokines.de>`
- **References:** `<95e2s7$gbd$07$1@news.t-online.com> <3A7BC9EC.2A07E039@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Hello,

the OS/2 protected mode runs very well on winnt and win2k. You simply
have to add the path to the binp directory inside your cobol
installation for example on the command line (set
os2libpath=%os2libpath%c:\cobol\binp or whatever your directory name
is). Running the OS2 runtime is much faster and more convenient than the
DOS runtime because you will not run into these memory limitations.

Greetings
Juergen Ibelgaufts

---------------------------------

Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Frank.Saur wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Isn't this in the FAQ ?
```

## Thread: Subroutines — 19 msgs

### Subroutines

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T13:11:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have a question about the use of subroutines (hope this is the
correct word).

Following scenario:

Main programm which
- calls a subroutine sub1 (and doesn't cancel after use) the first time
to initialize and set up some file-handling flags.

- calls a subroutine sub2 which should call sub1 as well but with
different file-handling flags.

After the second call to sub1 (from sub2), the original flags are no
more valid but should be valid for the main program.

Question:
How can I start/call a second instance of the same subroutine/program?

Sub2 handles all file accesses to a client file.
Linkage Section:
       01 Kundelib-Linkage.
           03 KL-VerarbeitungFlag  pic x.
               88 KL-Open          value space.
               88 KL-Read          value '1'.
               88 KL-ReadLocked    value '2'.
               88 Kl-Write         value '3'.
               88 Kl-Rewrite       value '4'.
               88 Kl-Close         value '5'.
               88 KL-Unlock        value '6'.
           03 KL-OpenMode          pic x.
               88 KL-Only4Input    value space.
               88 KL-Exclusive     value '1'.
               88 KL-4Update       value '2'.
           03 KL-AccessMode        pic x.
               88 KL-Main          value space.
               88 KL-Liefer        value '1'.
           03 KL-FileStatus        pic x(02).
           03 KL-ErrorMsg          pic x(20).
               88 KL-InvalidAccessMode     value 'Ungueltige Satzart'.
               88 KL-InvalidOpenMode       value 'Oeffen Ungueltig'.
               88 KL-InvalidPerformMode    value 'Operation ungueltig'.
           03 KL-Kdnr      pic 9(06).
           03 KL-Versart   pic x(02).
           03 KL-UserDialogFlag    pic x.
               88 KL-NoUserDialog  value space.
               88 KL-UserDialog    value '1'.

       01 Kunde-Satz Pic x( kdstammlength ).

procedure division using Kundelib-linkage kunde-satz.

Depending on KL-VerarbeitungFlag it performs some actions and gives
results back to the calling program.

My Problem is:
The Main-Program opens the the used file in a different way than the
second program needs the file.

One solution could be:
1. call sub2 to ask for it's status (=> change the sub2-program)
2. Save the status
3. call sub2 to close the file
4. call sub2 with its new status to open the file
4a. work on the file and after work has finished
5. call sub2 to close the file
6. call sub2 to open the file with the saved status

....I don't like this

Environment: MF COBOL 4.1 on Unix


TIA - Stefan Meyer


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

```
indicating "which program/procedure is currently calling sub1" and let sub1
use the datanames appropriate to that circumstance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-02T13:24:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95f1ie$jm8$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <sbBe6.1004$ND2.58928@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <95euv5$dca$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I think you are thinking in non-COBOL terms.

HOWEVER, if you want to do file I/O against the same file in COBOL from
different modules (programs), then the way to do this is to define it with
the EXTERNAL attribute in the FD.  You then define the same file in each
program - and you can do whichever I/O you want in whichever program you
want.  With this "approach" you do NOT pass anything in the USING statement
of the CALL (or Procedure Division Using) statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T08:15:32+00:00

```

Take a look at the "ENTRY" statement.  There is no reason that you cannot
have twenty or thirty different entry points to one subroutine.  Each can
have their own arguments.  Better yet, make it an object, and use different
methods.

"Stefan Meyer" <meyerst@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:95euv5$dca$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

…[111 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T12:52:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95hk11$vr0$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <sbBe6.1004$ND2.58928@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <95euv5$dca$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95hfdo$emj$1@news.igs.net>`

```
My recommendation - STAY AWAY from the ENTRY statement if at all possible.
Not only is this not Standard (and will probably never be), it also can lead
to a variety of "issues" (such as "last used state" of Linkage Section
items).  If you have a single program that has that many "logically"
separate things to do, then I would go with nested programs (which are
Standard) - and in the main part of the program call whichever nested
program does the functionality you are looking for.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T18:25:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95i4d8$f8b$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <sbBe6.1004$ND2.58928@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <95euv5$dca$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95hfdo$emj$1@news.igs.net> <95hk11$vr0$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I use both ENTRY and nested programs. They have separate
uses and using the wrong one can cause problems.

Nested programs work well when a procedure is unique to
the containing program and where the procedure can be
understood independently of the containing program. The
contained program has its own working storage and
access to the containing program's working storage, as
required. When I did a mass file conversion for Y2K, I used
a nested program with 12 contained programs. Each of the
contained programs converted one file. In this case, the
procedures were unique to the containing program, which
converted a system. The procedures could be understood
independently of the containing program. It was a good fit!

Entry programs work well when the procedures are common
to many programs and especially when the entry points share
common routines and working storage within the program.
As part of Y2K remediation, I wrote a program with 38 entry
points and 9 common routines. Each entry point accepted
one format for a date or time and returned another format
using the common routines, as necessary. Only one copy of
the program is loaded into memory and that copy is used by
about 45 separate programs. It was a good fit!

Had the entry point program been written as nested and copied
into the 45 separate programs, the routines and common data
would have been loaded into memory as many as four times.

Had the nested program been written as a program with 12
entry points ... well, that never would have happened!

The bottom line is that the reasons for using entry point programs
and nested programs are fundamentally different.
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <95hk11$vr0$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>...
>My recommendation - STAY AWAY from the ENTRY statement if at all possible.
>Not only is this not Standard (and will probably never be), it also can
lead
>to a variety of "issues" (such as "last used state" of Linkage Section
>items).  If you have a single program that has that many "logically"
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>> methods.
[...]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ [Std]; ENTRY Alternative for Standardization (was: Re: Subroutines)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-04T08:17:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95jl1f$270k$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <sbBe6.1004$ND2.58928@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <95euv5$dca$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95hfdo$emj$1@news.igs.net> <95hk11$vr0$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The reason ENTRY will not become part of the COBOL
standard is because it is considered "unstructured."
This does not mean ENTRY is not the best way to solve
some problems.

There is an alternative to ENTRY that I believe could be
added to the standard.

MODULE-ID. module-name.
environment-division
data-division
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    program-entry ...
    function-entry ...
END-MODULE.

That is, a module consists of one or more programs or
functions were elements of the environment division and
data division are common to all programs and functions
within the module.

This is similar to a class definition where object data is
available to all methods within the class. It is different from
objects because only one instance of the data is present
in the run unit.

Availability of the module is controlled by calling and
canceling the module. After the module is called and until
canceled, the program and function entries are made
available to other elements in the run unit.

This, generally, meets the unwritten requirement for
"structured" while providing nearly the same capabilities
as a program using ENTRY. In fact, I could convert the
program, I discussed in my earlier response, from
ENTRY to MODULE with no changes to the source code
in the 45 programs that use the ENTRY program. The
module program would not be as "clean"; but it would
work.
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <95hk11$vr0$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>...
>My recommendation - STAY AWAY from the ENTRY statement if at all possible.
>Not only is this not Standard (and will probably never be), it also can
lead
>to a variety of "issues" (such as "last used state" of Linkage Section
>items).
[...]
```

```
cannot

Second that. We have subroutines for each file along the lines of:

FILE-OPEN.
     ENTRY 'FI-OP' USING REC-LAYOUT.
     .....
     GO TO QUIT.

FILE-CLOSE.
     ENTRY 'FI-CL' USING REC-LAYOUT.
     .....
     GO TO QUIT.

FILE-READ-NEXT.
     etc
FILE-UPDATE.
     etc
FILE-DELETE.
     etc

QUIT.
     GOBACK.
```

#### ↳ Re: Subroutines

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T05:41:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Stefan Meyer wrote:

> I have a question about the use of subroutines (hope this is the
> correct word).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> How can I start/call a second instance of the same subroutine/program?

This sounds like a good place for a nested program so you can call it &
pass parameters. The nested program program defines the parameters in its
Linkage Section and addresses them via the USING statement. You don't need
a switch to tell it to use a different set of data names. This is a major
reason for coding a nested program or calling one dynamically under CICS.

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T07:45:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net> <95gdnb$jp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You are correct that within the same run-unit you may NOT have both an
external program and a nested program with the same program-id.

From you other post, it sounds (now) like you want a single program to do
all the I/O - but for this to NOT be your main program.  In this case, what
you probably want to do is

1) Define in your main program a WS-area that is a copy of your "record
layout" (probably a copy statement). Also define a field which contains a
"flag" indicating what I/O operation you want performed (e.g. OPEN, READ,
WRITE, CLOSE).

2) Call your subprogram using the record-layout and the i/o-flag.

3) In your subprogram, define your complete file (Select/Assign, FD, etc)
and include the COPY record-layout in the LINKAGE Section.  (Make certain
that your Procedure Division USING statement references all the 01-levels
from your Linkage Section.)

4) Then your subprogram does whatever I/O you want.  Use READ INTO
Linkage-section-layout and WRITE FROM linkage-section-layout statements.

5) Make certain that your main program calls this subprogram "individually"
for each read and write that you want performed - and that you have some way
of determining (in the main program) when you have reached end-of-file (if
you are looping thru reads).

   ***

Now for the big question ...
  WHY do you want to separate the I/O from the main program?  This can be
done, but I don't understand WHY you want to do it.  This (normally?) only
is useful if you have multiple applications using I/O to the same file and
you want a "common" file handler routine.  Is it possible that all you
really wanted (in the beginning) was to use PERFORM statements and isolate
the I/O into separate paragraphs and not separate programs?  Repeatedly
CALLing subprograms has some overhead (depending on your system potentially
SIGNIFICANT overhead).

NOTE:
  If you are using nested programs, look at the GLOBAL attribute and see if
that is of any use to you.
```

```
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:

>

I am not sure of what the original poster intended with the separate I/O
program,
but it goes along with the 3-tier design methodology that separates User
Interface
(screens/reports), Business Logic, and File Handling into separate modules.
The intent being that you could easily change the file system or user interface
without having to re-write the core program.  I am coming from a mainframe 
batch environment and find it difficult to put this to work in my daily
maintenance.
It does provide food for thought when designing new applications.
```

```


Sff5ky wrote:

> In article <95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>, "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> It does provide food for thought when designing new applications.

This is an interesting one, coming at the problem from different perspectives. Now
firstly for Bill  -  recent private comments from Bill to me 'you don't get it,
what direction J4 is going on finalizer'. Now I've tried to explain it to Bill to
the best of my abilities, 'but you still don't get it' when it comes to separate
file handling <G>

Mr. "Sky" you are definitely on the button - referring to three-tier systems, (a)
Business Logic, (b) UI(User Interface) and DBI(Database Interface).

Don Tees has 'hinted' at the answer - use separate objects. There was
correspondence here about a year ago, how in structured, you could have separate
programs for handling files - and there definitely are installations doing this.
Within OO, Don has his own model for handling files, as I also have. In  my case, I
have generic templates for handling relative, sequential, line sequential - and you
can 'throw' any record format at the template 'file' without the latter knowing the
format of the records you are accessing.The record format is of course in your
calling program, preferably as a copy file. (And get this - although he doesn't
buy into the idea - it was Bill who gave me the solution <G>). You use syntax in
your file program, as follows :-

This is part of the template for Sequential :-

       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       End FACTORY.
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT.
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

            Select               Data-File
            assign               Data-filename
            organization         sequential
            access               sequential
            file status          ws-filestatus.

       FILE SECTION.

       FD  Data-File
           record varying from 1 to 1200 characters   *> My arbitrary max
              depending on ws-record-size.
       01  Data-record.
           05                   pic x(1200).

As part of the exercise you have got to pass the record size and full filepathname
to the above. The following is the method for getting at all file VERBS. As Don
indicated, you could use entry points to do the same in structured :-

       *>----------------------------------------------------------
       Method-ID. "access-file".
       *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-result                             pic x(4) comp-5.
       Linkage Section.
       copy "fileactn.cpy".      <----- ( Open/Close/ReadNext/Write etc...)
       01 lnk-result                            pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division Using     file-action
                          returning lnk-result.

          set file-result-ok to true
          initialize ws-Error-Data

          if   dont-initialize
               continue

          else initialize os-record(1:ws-record-size)    *> using reference mod
          End-if

          Evaluate TRUE

            When read-next

                 Read Data-File next record
                   At End
                     Set file-finis to True
                   Not at End
                     Move Data-record(1:ws-record-size)
                       to os-record-data(1:ws-record-size)
                 End-Read

            When write-record

                 Move os-record-data(1:ws-record-size)
                etc.......

I haven't detailed it here, but the os-record referred to above is a copy of the
file record which you are either reading/writing and is passed from sender to file
template, or the reverse. (With some thought, I think I can get rid of the
os-record, which necessitates two method 'calls' - that implies separate methods,
open/closes don't require an 'image' of the record, whereas read/write methods
would - so this would parallel structured entry-points).

Now the tricky one is indexed files - you don't know where the damned PrimeKey is
located. But I do have a template from Pete Dashwood for a generic indexed template
- still to be tried by me in OO, although Pete has confirmed it definitely works in
a structured program. Further, he adds, one can even handle alternate keys,
(possibly in limited form), but a 'bit messy'.

PrimeKey : Don did suggest you could have a fixed Primekey size at the front of the
record, (i.e., in some situations you would have spare characters in the key). Don
did you firm up on that idea ?

Now using OO jargon, I can have say, 10 instances ( 10 file objects ), accessing
that one file template above - not 10 copies of the program, and I can happily
access all 10 files for reading, writing, independently of one another, each
'instance' having its own file-status.
```

#### ↳ Re: Subroutines

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T09:09:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95h3lg$2a71$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Place something like the following In a separate source
file named sub1a.cbl.

copy sub1 replacing
    sub1 by sub1a
    "sub1" by "sub1a".

This creates a copy of sub1 using the same code as
sub1 but named sub1a. It does so by replacing the
identifier (program-name) in the program-id paragraph.
If the program has its name in the source code, as a
literal, the literal replacement will change that.

In the main program, call "sub1". In sub2, call "sub1a".

Each program has its own flags, file position indicator,
etc.; but, with the exception of the copy statement, they
share the same source code.

I tried the copy statement, above. It seems to work fine.
------------------
Rick Smith

Stefan Meyer wrote in message <95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I have a question about the use of subroutines (hope this is the
>correct word).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>How can I start/call a second instance of the same subroutine/program?
[...]
>Environment: MF COBOL 4.1 on Unix
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-03T10:16:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95h7j0$2el3$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95h3lg$2a71$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Additional info:

You will probably need $SET PREPROCESS"CP" on the
first line of sub1a.cbl. This is to take care of nested copy
statements. Another method might be preferred for the
environment in which you are working. Check the manuals.

Using replace, in sub2, with the same substitutions as the
copy replacing in sub1a, could provide rapid testing without
extensive source code modifications.

I wish I would have thought of these earlier!
------------------
Rick Smith
```
