##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T15:12:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95s9to$f0s$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net> <95gdnb$jp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <95lnv6$9fm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95qmnc$k5g$1@news.igs.net> <95r0op$tbl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95rlp2$5nr$1@news.igs.net> <95rt7u$kk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
P.S. to that last.

"Stefan Meyer" <meyerst@my-deja.com> wrote in message

> I should have known that OOP is that easy in COBOL..."Damned".
>

OOP is *very* easy in Cobol, and even if you only use it to replace
subroutines, it has huge advantages.  Wilson Price has published a book on
OOP Cobol(and he was kind enough to send me a free copy)(Elements of Object
Oriented Cobol, www.objectz.com ) that is MF oriented rather than Fujitsu,
and there are some differences.  The new standard is now in final review,
and so that should be cleared up quite soon.

I love it.  The "abort-routine" is very typical of the type of thing it is
almost impossible to do with subroutines.  Not only does it provide a nice
simple way to crash and find out where it happened, but that base object
gives me a "What's this" window for every screen that tells me the source
code library that is currently running.  When projects get huge, that sort
of thing is invaluable to finding your way around in the code.

OOP also provides seamless component based structure; the components can be
written in Cobol or any other language.  PowerCobol will create a COM
object, while Fujitsu also provide a "wrapper" to make regular Cobol code
COM/CORBA compliant.  I have just gotten started on this stuff, and am
starting to get the feel of it.  My next step is to take something like a
customer master system, and wrap it up as a COM object.  In theory, I should
then be able to add a customer database to a system by simply dropping it
into my project.  Certainly, others are using the component model big time,
right now.  Most of those objects can be purchased ... stuff like mailers
and weird-devices.  Peter Dashwood has done a large amount of work using
that approach.  Jimmy Gavan is doing a lot of base module work in MF.  I
would like to see a lot more sharing of code using the OOP approach. I am
convinced that is where the future is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T14:43:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A81C195.85EEF9AD@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net> <95gdnb$jp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <95lnv6$9fm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95qmnc$k5g$1@news.igs.net> <95r0op$tbl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95rlp2$5nr$1@news.igs.net> <95rt7u$kk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95s9to$f0s$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:

>  I would like to see a lot more sharing of code using the OOP approach. I am
> convinced that is where the future is.

I am convinced that that is where "a" future is.   But what we will see are more
than one future for CoBOL.   In enterprise environments sharing code can cause
more cost than benefit.  If you have 75% maintenance, you don't want to unit
test, user test, and system test every thing your module touches.   If you have
75% maintenance, you need to easily and quickly browse all of the code for a
program.    If you have tons of existing non OO code, the thought of a mixed
code environment doesn't look like a good investment.

Also, how much does a good OO shop save in code reusability in real life?   With
GUIs, I bet quite a bit.  With business rules, I bet not much.   Polymorphing
business rules can also obfuscate them.

Different tools are designed for different needs.  OO is not a tool which is
best for all needs.  Neither is CoBOL.
```

```
There is another benefit to OO we have not touched upon.  Refactoring.
I have used this with OO to enahnce performance - reengineering a
process without having to change the underlying objects.  It's pretty
interesting how well it works.  Refactoring an OO system is MUCH
easier that refactoring a structured system.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO : Refactoring WAS : Subroutines

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T05:50:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8CC139.4FD6F5D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net> <95gdnb$jp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <95lnv6$9fm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95qmnc$k5g$1@news.igs.net> <95r0op$tbl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95rlp2$5nr$1@news.igs.net> <95rt7u$kk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95s9to$f0s$1@news.igs.net> <3A81C195.85EEF9AD@brazee.net> <3a86c089.4432262@news1.attglobal.net> <x6Bh6.7844$Nj5.435469@bgtnsc07-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
For : Warren and Howard

Warren Simmons wrote:

> REFACTORING AN OO OBJECT

Ah Ha ! Thane has used one of those $10 words <G> I checked out the few books I
have, plus Net Express on-Line help and Mr. Gates' 'Computer Dictionary' -
zilch. Wanna blame someone - Thane probably picked it up from Ken Foskey, great
OO advocate but spends most of his time currently in C++ - that pays his
mortgage !

It's one of the new buzz phrases, along with Extreme Programming. Refactoring
itself is getting the edge on converting/updating application code, specifically
in an OO environment. One of the gurus is James Fowler. He has a site, starts
"I'll now describe my basic premise, but if you really want the detail, you'll
have to buy my book" GOTCHA !

Go to google.com and search on "refactoring programming" or the other way
around.

I could be way off track but it is another way of saying reusability, and that's
how I interpreted Thane's use of the word. So let's try and keep this KISS.

(1) In an earlier message I illustrated ONE PROGRAM (Class) could have as many
instances as you have files. You subsequently find this File Class program
doesn't do it all for you. You need a new method of access, (a) invent a new
method name which you are going to use from calling programs and add that new
method to the File Class - one change/one compile - the class file, only and
test it with just one program accessing the new method.

Another way would be to create a sub-class containing this specific stuff, so
sub-class B accesses super class A. Now if both Class A and B contain an
identical method name "xyz-do-it", by invoking Class B you are going to get it
actioned as it reads in Class B rather than as it appears in Class A. That's
kind of the reverse order we think in, because Structured Program A tends to
call B, rather than the other way around.

(2) I'll illustrate with GUIs, (although these are not part of the COBOL
standard and never will be - see below about Component Generators). This is
somewhat parallel to what I've mentioned above. In my own design, rather than
laboriously keep on typing source for GUIs, I've put all the GUI actions into a
separate program MyDialog.cbl. (Thane tells me this is not dissimilar to the
approach taken by Flexus with SP2). At the current time MyDialog.cbl handles
only what I need :-

- entry fields
- pushbuttons
- lisboxes/droplists
- radio buttons

and it creates those controls as a result of my using the Merant Dialog Editor
(screen builder tool) or where necessary, dynamically creating controls using
GUI methods.
(Why dynamically ? - the Dialog Editor isn't worth tiddly-squat when I want a
table of 10 x 24 entryfiedls - alternatives being use Activex or Java).

So MyDialog has a whole bunch of method names to do different things,
"hide-object", "show-object", "set-enableEmpty", "set-disable" "get-events"
etc......
and it could grow like Topsy to accommodate anything new I come up with, e.g.,
I could have a new need for a Multiple Line Entry field required for making
notes to attach to a record...

MyDialog.cbl works beautifully. But, getting a bit confused trying to remember
which parameters were required for which method, ( or rather the control being
actioned), I decided I would pass a standard set in a copy file
Dialog-Params.cpy. (Details are irrelevant but it contains x,y,w,h for dynamic
rectangles, and other properties etc.).

So first time I ran MyDialog with the new Dialog-Params it went belly-up !
Here's the original code :-

A Business Logic Class which calls MyDialog
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "display-new-record".
*>-----------------------------------------------------------
Procedure Division.

 set NoStressValues    to true
 set ControlCollection to true
 set field-PrimeKey    to true
 move Field            to Dialog-Field
 invoke os-Dialog "set-disable" using Dialog-Params  *>  in Working-Storage
 set Field-SpecNo to true
 move Field       to Dialog-Field
 invoke os-Dialog "set-nextfocus-empty" using Dialog-Params  <<<<--------

Exit Method
End Method "display-new-record".
*>------------------------------------------------------------

MyDialog.cbl
---------------
Note - the following method is accessed from my Business Program above
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "set-nextfocus-empty".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

*> This method sets the entryfield "setEmpty" for fresh user
*> input.

Local-storage section.
01 ls-object                         object reference.
Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

  invoke os-Collection(2) "at"
         using lnk-field returning ls-object
  set os-CurrentObject to ls-object
  invoke os-CurrentObject "enable"
  invoke os-CurrentObject "setempty"
  invoke os-CurrentObject "setfocus"
  invoke super            "show"

End Method "set-nextfocus-empty".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

The following method in MyDialog is initial 'housekeeping: :-

Method-id. "z-create-Entryfield".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-event                      pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                     object reference.
01 ls-size                       pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-capacity                   pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

 invoke self "getObjectFromId"
        using lnk-ID returning ls-object

 *** etc.... , more stuff, but I've pared down  to illustrate the problem

 if   lnk-length <> zeroes
      invoke ls-object "setLength" using lnk-length
      invoke ls-object "accessSystemEvents"
      move Button1Down to ls-event

      Evaluate true
       when EmptyField
         invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
           ls-event, self, "z-set-nextfocus-empty ", lnk-Field
       when other
         invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
           ls-event, self, "z-set-nextfocus-insert ", lnk-Field
      End-evaluate

*** more stuff................

End Method "z-create-Entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

Now look at the above. My Business Program calling MyDialog for
"set-nextfocus-empty" will give the correct results. But now look at the method
"z-create-entryfield", as originally worded. It will go belly up because of
incorrect parameters. Sod it ! It doesn't work.

Now - revised code to handle the above problem :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "z-create-Entryfield".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-event                      pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                     object reference.
01 ls-size                       pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-capacity                   pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

 invoke self "getObjectFromId"
        using lnk-ID returning ls-object

 *** etc.... ......

 if   lnk-length <> zeroes
      invoke ls-object "setLength" using lnk-length
      invoke ls-object "accessSystemEvents"
      move Button1Down to ls-event

      Evaluate true
       when EmptyField
         invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
           ls-event, self, "z-set-nextfocus-empty ", lnk-Field   <<< new method
name
       when other
         invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
           ls-event, self, "z-set-nextfocus-insert ", lnk-Field     <<< new
method name
      End-evaluate

**** some more code

End Method "z-create-Entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Following new method added - and only occurs in MyDialog.cbl - so you can
consider it to be a 'Private' method.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "z-set-nextfocus-empty".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
copy "dilogprm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ls==.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-field                         pic x(4) comp-5.
Procedure Division using lnk-field.
  initialize ls-Params
  move 2 to ls-number
  move lnk-field to ls-Field
  invoke self "set-nextfocus-empty" using ls-Params

End Method "z-set-nextfocus-empty".
*>------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem solved - and only one compile and one test !

(3) Example 3

Big American Corp Inc., trades only in the Americas. They have a class which
handles all date functions, plus formatting. They now move into Europe, so they
are confronted with German, Dutch, French, Italian etc.( But wait, they've
probably already covered this scenario because in dealing with Canada they had
to allow for French in Quebec, Spanish in Mexico; chances are they are trading
in S. America, so throw in Portuguese as well).

The date problem - no big deal. Having deftly designed a class to handle all
date problems, intrinsic functions, difference between two dates etc., now we
come up against the various 'human' languages - formatting in the 'local' tongue
for display/printing. The various languages are 'sub classed' to Class Date, the
correct terminology being Classes FrenchDates or GermanDates invoke the Class
"super" Dates.

OK. So there's nothing really to stop you paralleling this in Structured. Based
on any given set of tools, it's up to us to become individually innovative.
Warren, like you, I'm much too old in the tooth to be taken in by a modern
gimmick - for me OO COBOL is ELEGANT and an absolute PLEASURE to use. Now the
kicker - the proof of the pudding is in the eating - at the current time you can
only 'prove' it with Fujitsu and Net Express.

Howard's comments

Well I know he's got his violin out to play the 'maintenance' theme, and it is a
genuine concern. I know you wont buy it, but you don't have to goddam test
everything, if you've got it isolated like I've tried to illustrate above. When
was the last time you tested COBOL intrinsic functions against every program in
your installation that uses them.

Savings on reusability - might be figures from other languages, but I don't
accept them as being applicable to COBOL. Until we see a growth in OO COBOL  we
wont know. Yes you are right about the effectiveness with GUIs, as my quotes
above indicate.

Reusability with Business problems - yes and know - I'll go 50% with you that
the business problem is unique. Take Walmart and Montgomery Ward - success and
disaster - it was the application of business(marketing) rules/philosphy that
puts them at the respective ends of the spectrum. Business philosophy and
resulting rules will be the driving force.

But the other 50 % for our two retailers - they have so much in common.

Take a hypothetical example - Payroll. (I know nothing of this topic ensuring we
used an ICL package when I was a Systems Manager <G>). Let's say you are in
Quebec and had a method to calculate overtime which is a standard 1.5. Then the
Quebecers decide that if you work on St. Jean Baptiste day, (whenever that is),
that by law you get double-time, or maybe even triple-time. Ouch ! Your PayCalc
Class doesn't handle it. Solution - add a new method :-

Business Class
-------------
invoke PayCalc "get-overtime" using Rate, Hours returning Dollars - OR -

if StJeanBaptitsteDay
   invoke PayCalc "get-StJean" using Rate, Hours returning Dollars
End-if

Class - PayCalc
----------------
method-id. "get-overtime".
Linkage Rate, Hours, Dollars.
Procedure Division using Rate Hours returning Dollars.
   compute Dollars =  etc....

method-id. "get-StJean".
Linkage Rate, Hours, Dollars2.
Procedure Division using Rate Hours returning Dollars2.
    invoke self "get-overtime" using Rate Hours returning Dollars1
   compute Dollars2 =  etc.......

Maybe a lousy example, but it does illustrate the concept of 'flexibility', and
the same approach is universal in OO to all business rules that break the mode.
OO hardly represents a 'business tool', it is a methodology -  No, a methodical
approach to system design related to business problems, breaking them down into
itsy bitsy methods to cover a variety of possibilities(rules).

Let me stress again - you can parallel it in Structured - I just have the gut
feeling that OO is to an nth degree more flexible. (Well, actually it's more
than a gut feeling - I KNOW it works for me <G>)

> >I am convinced that that is where "a" future is.   But what we will see
>> are more than one future for CoBOL....

Where does the future that you forsee fit in. Here's a sobering thought for the
non-OO'ers. Just heard from Mike Sheehan that Limerick Tech (in Eire) which is
one of the mainstays in the republic for teaching COBOL, is calling it quits in
two years time for our language. If this continues, it wont really matter what
your preference is, whether Structured or OO - unless you are one of the lucky
ones, already retired before COBOL gets buried..

Component Maker

Warren - Knowing your interest in keeping up to snuff, even though in your
twilight years, write to Pete Dashwood who will explain the following more
eloquently than I can. I know he will be delighted to respond to you.

GUIs are not part of COBOL; perhaps they could have been, had they been given
some serious thought 20 years ago when the "Mickey Mouse" Apples, Radio Shacks
and then PCs were first touted. Smalltalk had GUI classes in the '70s. But again
- GUIs designed by a committee !!!!??????  Smalltalk, Jobs, Gates, Java -
single-minded designers/entrepreneurs  pushed the envelope.

It's got to be some four months or more ago that somebody jumped in here
promoting his use of POWERCOBOL - he was such an asshole that most of us ignored
him; ignoring the messenger we also ignored the message. Now Pete is on the same
tack - more diplomatically <G>. The way to go for UI(User Interface) or GUI-ing
is to treat it as being outside of COBOL. (Now being one of the 'originals' in
introducing DB for COBOL, I think you will appreciate the similarity).

Pete's contention is that UI must stand independent of ANY language - and that
includes Java. You use the appropriate tools/languages  to build a 'window',
clicking, dropping and dragging, getting 'shapes', properties AND method code
related to the control. Plus he also adds, drag and drop in a database if
necessary. The finished component then becomes usable with ANY language.. Take
an extension of this UI approach - I doubt any of us would suggest COBOL should
be used for light pens, voice recognition etc. It makes sense to build these
features in using a general purpose Component Generator.

Chances are component generators will come and go as they beat one another out
of the market, but the payola is that we ALL finish up with an even better
designing tool. (Hope our friends at Flexus and Norcom are keeping 'alert').

Jimmy, Calgary AB

>
> Slowly, the concepts of OO creep into my mind. The jargon not withstanding.
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A87F5E8.E1A7C3BB@brazee.net>...
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Please define refactoring.

I'll give it a try.

Refactoring is factoring after the code has been written. :-)

Factoring is accomplished during design.

Factoring is the process of removing common elements
from two or more classes. These common elements are
placed into a superclass. The original classes are then
changed to inherit from that superclass.

The primary reason for (re)factoring is that, if there is a
common element between two classes, there is a
possibility that more new classes might use that common
element.(1)

1. Robert C. Martin, "Designing Object-Oriented C++
    Applications," Prentice-Hall, 1995.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-744dbc7541"></a>
## Thread: cobol student- please help — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: cobol student- please help

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T15:12:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95rok0$g1g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A80BF7B.58067BC5@slic.com>`

```
In a COBOL program it is actually not that common to read just one
file.

When you see the select statement:

Select sysin assign to c:\file1.dat
    access is sequential.(etc)

You are right if you have 2 files they have to have different names.
SYSIN would be then name of a file.   On a PC you would use the file
name.  Example: ASSIGN "C:\cobol\sysin.dat".  On a large computer you
might assign a file a symbolic name such as PE11 for personel file 1.
On a mainfram if you didnt do that, everytime you used a fiel you would
have to identify where the file starts how long the record is how much
space is allocated for the file, etc.  It is a little complicated but
not too bad.  We just copy examples of other programs mostly.

Often a record has to be built from information from 2 or more files.
For instance the person's admin data may be in one file, and the
person's financial data may be in another file.  Another example would
be a batch process where a list of transactions (add, del, change,
payroll, inventory change) is in one file, and the other file is a
master file that is to be changed.  Then you have to match up one
record to the other record by some common field like a social security
number.

It is possible to do this several different ways.  You could read one
file sequentially and read maybe the second file is indexed and you
randomly search the file using the record key.  Another way is to put
the transactions in a table in the same order as the master file and
read the master file till you get a match.  Another way is to read one
file read the second file and compare one field.  Based on the
comparison you would do the transaction when they match.  Then if they
do not match you have to decide, based on a comparison of a field,
whether to read file A or file B.  This is a little harder.  It is much
easier to use a file key. However, you can read a file sequentially
faster than you can randomly access a file using a key.  A lot depends
on the size of the two files, and whether you can use virtual memory on
the computer you are using.

On large computers most files are Indexed by a key field or fields.
You just read a file, store some data in a key and use the key to find
the matching record.  Sometimes when the files are smaller it is easier
to just build a table and search the table. Other times there may be
100,000 records or more.  That is a lot to search!

It seems you just need to see what technique the instructor is deciding
to teach then implement that concept.  So you need to see what he wants
you to do.

In article <3A80BF7B.58067BC5@slic.com>,
  Jim McNamara <mac3@slic.com> wrote:

> Cobol 97
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…

http://www.deja.com/

#### ↳ Re: Setting environment variable from within cobol

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T11:58:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95ruhn$a7u$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<9DAFD3D54DDE70BA.D46F25F7B9C10062.A0910AEA4B133787@lp.airnews.net> <Wx5g6.72884$KP3.21472588@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <28BD62D8C7C45F25.858F353DC195DBC8.52EDDAAD0876DFAE@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Actually, it is starting two new processes.  It starts COMMAND.COM, and
COMMAND.COM starts the EXE.  Each process inherits the environment of the
one that started it.  When you change the environment in your EXE, you are
changing the copy.  When it stops, the new environment area (the one you
changed), is returned to free space.

#### ↳ Re: Setting environment variable from within cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T08:27:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A825859.CF7B6E4@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9DAFD3D54DDE70BA.D46F25F7B9C10062.A0910AEA4B133787@lp.airnews.net> <Wx5g6.72884$KP3.21472588@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <28BD62D8C7C45F25.858F353DC195DBC8.52EDDAAD0876DFAE@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Progger wrote:
> 
> ....so if I execute an exe in a dos bat file it is starting a new process?

Yes.  Whenever a new process starts in DOS a _copy_ of the environment
is made for that process.  When the process completes that environment
is discarded.  If you type SET at the DOS command prompt it will list
the environment.  If you then run a batch program with SET in it the
list will be the same values but is a _copy_ of the environment.  Your
first program gats a new copy, can change it but it is then discarded. 
If you started a child process then that would receive a copy of what
you had changed.

It is likely that environment items are not what you want to do.  For a
start a copy is made only to the size (rounded to 16 bytes) of the
existing items so adding new items, or making existing items larger,
will probably fail.

I usually add into AUTOEXEC.BAT SET ZZZZ=ZZZZZZZ...ZZZZZZZ to fill up
space then put SET ZZZZ= in the batch file to free some up.

What you may want to do is to have a Cobol controlling program that
CALLs your programs passing an interface area:


    prog0:

             WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
                 COPY "interfce.ws".
             PROCEDURE DIVISION.
             Control-Program.
                 MOVE SPACES    TO Interface
                 CALL "Prog1"  USING Interface
                 CALL "Prog2"  USING Interface
                 STOP RUN.

   prog1:
             LINKAGE SECTION.
                COPY "interfce.ws".
             PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Interface.
    
                 blah blah
                 MOVE "I'm happy."   TO Interface-Message
                 EXIT PROGRAM.

   prog2:    as above.

Then you can get a bit sophisticated and have the control program call
the name that is in an area in the Interface:

   prog0:         MOVE "prog1"    TO Program-Name
           
                  PERFORM
                      UNTIL Program-Name = "FINISH"

                      CALL Program-Name
                          USING Interface
                  END-PERFORM

   prog1:         MOVE "prog2"    TO Program-Name
                  
   prog2:         MOVE "FINISH"   TO Program-Name
```

<a id="t-659d74a4d7"></a>
## Thread: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol? — 4 msgs

### Re: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T08:31:52-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A818687.B9E538C5@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<8RZf6.25$D13.51393@nnrp2.sbc.net> <3A80C55B.2527A7F2@slic.com>`

```
Ummm, are you sure about that? Maybe it depends on what language you're using, but
I don't see any necessary relationship between the difference between Do While/Do
Until and the difference between testing at the beginning vs. the end of the loop.

If you test at the end of the loop then the loop will execute at least once
regardless of whether the condition is true or false and regardless of whether
you're testing for While or Until. If you test at the beginning of the loop then
the While loop will not execute at all when the condition is false, nor will the
Until loop execute at all when the condition is true.

As far as I can see, WHILE is simply UNTIL NOT, i.e. WHILE TRUE = UNTIL FALSE.

Jim McNamara wrote:

> The Perform statement includes the Test Before and
> Test After phrases, corresponding to Do While and Do until,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Meesha
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol?

- **From:** Jim McNamara <mac3@slic.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T22:40:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A823F76.BB00E600@slic.com>`
- **References:** `<8RZf6.25$D13.51393@nnrp2.sbc.net> <3A80C55B.2527A7F2@slic.com> <3A818687.B9E538C5@dced.state.ak.us>`

```


Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> Ummm, are you sure about that? Maybe it depends on what language you're using, but
>I don't see any necessary relationship between the difference between Do
>While/Do Until and the difference between testing at the beginning vs. the end of
>the loop. 

>If you test at the beginning of the loop then
>the While loop will not execute at all when the condition is false

Calvin-

  The following is taken verbatim from our text.
  ---------------------------------------------------------------
 

  The perform statement includes the Test Before and Test After
  phrases, corresponing to a do while and do until, respectively.
  Specification of test before tests the condition before perfoming
  the procedure, and corresponds to a do while.  Specification of
  Test After performs the procedure and then tests the condition,
  and corresponds to a do until. Omission of both test before and
  test after defaults to test before.
  ----------------------------------------------------------------
  Cobol from Micro to Mainframe
  3rd edition
  Robert Grauer, Carol Villar, Arthur Buss
  ISBN 0-13-085849-8
  page number 232

  some examples I made below... 
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 CONSTANTS.
 	A			PIC 9 VALUE 3.
	B			PIC 9 VALUE 4.


 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
   PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE UNTIL A < B
    SUBTRACT 1 FROM A 
    DISPLAY 'IS ' A ' LESS THAN ' B ' ?'
    END-PERFORM.
    STOP RUN.

   Lets test it. Test Before tests the condition before executing
   statements. 3 is less than 4 is true and the statements aren't
   executed. If the above constant values are reversed, it loops
   twice until the condition is true (2 < 3).

   In paradox a while loop means repeats a sequence of statements
   as long as a specified condition is True.

while starts by evaluating the logical expression Condition. If
Condition is False,
 the Statements are not executed. If the value is True, the Statements
between the 
 Condition and endWhile are executed in sequence. 
 
	method pushButton(var eventInfo Event)
	  while 4 < 3 
	     msgstop ("Entered","Loop") {displays dialog box}
	    {comment- while condition is false
              msgstop dialog not displayed} 
	  endwhile
	endmethod

 Based on all this, I'm going to go out on a limb and say Test Before
does not 
correspond to a Do While in Cobol and the book is WRONG.  Calvin is
right and Meesha 
is probably confused.  Any comments or suggestions ???   
BTW I'm going into hiding if I'm wrong.
   
  Thanks
   jim
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T08:32:07-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A82D817.1CC72633@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<8RZf6.25$D13.51393@nnrp2.sbc.net> <3A80C55B.2527A7F2@slic.com> <3A818687.B9E538C5@dced.state.ak.us> <3A823F76.BB00E600@slic.com>`

```
Interesting. It's wrong, though. Obviously TEST BEFORE can't correspond to a DO WHILE in
COBOL since COBOL doesn't have a DO WHILE. Since the authors don't specify what language
they're comparing COBOL to I'd take it as generic, i.e. based on rules of logic. Testing
before or after has no intrinsic relationship to whether you perform a loop while a
condition is true or while it is false, i.e. until it is true. Therefore, the authors
are wrong unless they're comparing COBOL to a specific, but unnamed, language in which
the DO WHILE is specifically coded to test BEFORE and the DO UNTIL is specifically coded
to test AFTER performing the loop.

Now I don't want to be a proponent of VB, but it's the only language other than COBOL &
SQL that I've worked with recently. I used to work with C but it's been a number of
years and IIRC loops worked pretty much like they do in VB, i.e. you can test before or
after the loop is performed & you can perform the loop either while a condition is true
or until a condition is true, and the two situations aren't linked. You have a total of
4 possible ways of performing a loop. Perform while a condition is true with test
before, perform until a condition is true with test before, perform while a condition is
true with test after, and perform until a condition is true with test after. You
effectively have those 4 possibilities in any language which allows controlling where
you place your test (and if necessary you can perform the test manually to control
that). I've even done it in EZTrieve.

Of course for taking a test I'd give the answer that's in the book, but I'd also keep in
mind the *true* situation.

Jim McNamara wrote:

> Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> >
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>    jim
```

```

## Thread: Professional Organizations? — 3 msgs

### Re: Professional Organizations?

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T17:49:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a8186bc.13041087@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<9575ic$kav$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A78222E.3A6387E4@brazee.net> <B6A0CFBE.2D3%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A7D0998.BA798915@Azonic.co.nz> <B6A6386B.33B%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A81AB26.BF325028@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Jeff Baynard wrote:
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>operate mouse detracts from an interactive system.

Richard:

You are absolutely correct.  We advocate designing the user interface
according to the task to be performed.  For example:

If the application is an "inquiry-oriented" type of system in which
there will be minimal or no data entry, mouse-based or keyboard-based
navigation is fine.

BUT....if the application is heavily data entry oriented, we recommend
that the programmer consider disabling the mouse if the application is
to run in a Windows GUI environment.  It a very simple process of
setting a switch from the program and can even be done on a "user id
by user id" basis.

The end user learns quickly that to operate the application, the
fingers must stay on the keyboard.  If the end user is operating the
application constantly during their working hours, they may not need
the mouse.

I'm curious if VB or Delphi provide a mouse switch to turn a mouse on
or off for an application or a specific end user.  Anyone know?

It seems to me that this is a fundamental option to allow the
programmer to create an ineffective interactive business data entry
program or an effective one.

Like you said....for graphics design work, desktop publishing or a
program in which dragging and dropping objects can be utilized, a
mouse can be a very nice tool, but for some business-oriented
programs, it can really get in the way.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Professional Organizations?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T08:42:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A825BF5.7D99D12E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9575ic$kav$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A78222E.3A6387E4@brazee.net> <B6A0CFBE.2D3%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A7D0998.BA798915@Azonic.co.nz> <B6A6386B.33B%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A81AB26.BF325028@Azonic.co.nz> <3a8186bc.13041087@news.epix.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:
> 
> It seems to me that this is a fundamental option to allow the
> programmer to create an ineffective interactive business data entry
> program or an effective one.

Unfortunately there are two criteria that must be met by every packaged
application.  It must, of course, meet the needs of the users who will
actually work with it, but first, in order to get to that stage, it must
be bought (or sold).

The person making the buying decision is often not one of the users that
will work with it.  This means that the application must be targetted at
the buyer.  To make it so that he will buy it it must be instantly
usable, ie so 'easy to learn' that "even an accountant can use it"*.

This usually means GUI, mouse, and 'click-around to see what happens' to
make the buyer feel that he could actually work out what is gouing on
and thus feel talented.

In order to meet the criteria of being sold _and_ doing useful work an
application requires _two_ different user interfaces.  However, most
software houses that rely on sales recognise that the only actual
criteria is getting the product sold.  In fact if the application is not
the most effective in use then additional licences will be required plus
more training and more chargable support.

Just tell me if I am being too cynical.


(* Actual quote from a sales brochure)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Professional Organizations?

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T15:10:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a8400af.3381771@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<9575ic$kav$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A78222E.3A6387E4@brazee.net> <B6A0CFBE.2D3%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A7D0998.BA798915@Azonic.co.nz> <B6A6386B.33B%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A81AB26.BF325028@Azonic.co.nz> <3a8186bc.13041087@news.epix.net> <3A825BF5.7D99D12E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>In order to meet the criteria of being sold _and_ doing useful work an
>application requires _two_ different user interfaces.  However, most
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Just tell me if I am being too cynical.

Richard:

I don't think that you are being too cynical.  I agree with you that
the Windows UI is highly overrated when it comes to "ease of use" and
in many cases a poor alternative to a well-designed character mode
user interface.  Too often, the first criteria in the buyers mind has
nothing to do with the quality of the application being purchased.

But, our philosophy is that the user interface should is written so
that mouse clicks on icons/pushbuttons be assigned corresponding
function keys which perform the same control functions.  This makes it
is possible to write a single application which allows an
administrator type person to configure the application to turn the
mouse on or off for an end user.

The marketing guys at the trade show booths can click around to their
hearts content in order to sell the application and the administrator
can shut the mouse off so that the employees can get a reasonable
amount of work accomplished.

 
Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3A8196B6.14B68F34@home.com>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net> <95gdnb$jp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <95lnv6$9fm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95qmnc$k5g$1@news.igs.net> <95r0op$tbl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Stefan Meyer wrote:

> Donald,
> I'll comment inline...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> developping and MF COBOL 4.1 on Unix for production).<snip>

Stefan,

It goes without saying, your aren't going to get anywhere with OOP unless
your compiler acknowledges OO. Not knowing the compilers you quote - I
don't know.

Given your background (as quoted), OOP should be no big deal. Remember, you
don't have to go 'full blown' OO syntax to use it. You can still use
Structured programs that just invoke OO features to get you instances.

The alternative, (Structured approach), as Rick suggested, is to make
copies of file programs and rename them. You could extend to the 'template'
concept for sequential, relative, indexed as I indicated.

Structured - Bill has pointed out entry-points are non-standard - Hell !
there's so much that's non-standard in our compilers anyway, and likely to
remain so, even when J4 arrive at COBOL 2000X  (target date for J4
conclusions, December 2002; then more bureaucracy until it gets passed by
WG4 (ISO Standards) - so even with 'extensions' you are safe until at least
2005, and beyond !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** Jim McNamara <mac3@slic.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A81975B.FD0D87DB@slic.com>`

```
Thanks everyone.
This solution works.

Later
jim

Your problem is in assigning everything to sysin.  Every platform
handles
just how an actual file is linked to the Cobol program a bit
differently.
SYSIN and SYSOUT can be changed to other things.  For example, on a PC,
you
would probably code something like

SELECT FILENAME ASSIGN TO "C:\MYDATA\MYFILE.DAT"

You are probably going to have to hunt down an actual example for your
particular OS to write your code.
```

commetn on it or I bug the crap out of you).

Write if interested and I'll send you a conversion package. It is NOT an

#### ↳ Re: Work problem: cobol signed numerics to SAS

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T19:14:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<964p0s$rs4$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3a81ed77.20647930@news.interramp.com>`

```
Assuming you're on a mainframe, what little I remember about SAS
I don't see why this should be a problem. SAS handles IBM standard
Zoned Decimal format which I think is what you're talking about.
It even handles packed decimal I believe. Defining the fields as
zoned decimal in SAS should allow it to read them.


===================================================================
> Does anyone know how to import numerics with embedded signs into SAS.
> Unfortunately we are working with a file produced with a Cobol program
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Work problem: cobol signed numerics to SAS

- **From:** mel5133928@aol.com (MEl5133928)
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T00:03:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010215190323.04900.00000193@ng-cd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3a81ed77.20647930@news.interramp.com>`

```
For signed packed decimal representation on OS/390, use SAS informat PDw.d
(where w=width of the field and d is a representation of the number of decimal
places to assume). For zoned decimal data, use informat ZDw.d. You can use
BZw.d to convert blanks to zeroes (except leading ones).
```

Now those are (Y|1|J|S) somethings I've wanted a LONG time Bill.

Something Like:

INITIALIZE MY-DATA TO INITIALVALUES. (would init all data items including
FILLER)

or INITIALIZE MY-DATA  WITHFILLER

PLUG, plug,plug.


And for my two pfennigs worth,
The original -Date- routine cited does not work.

when working with CCYYMMDD  (Or any CCYYxxxxx dates)
you MUST divide by 400  FIRST.

Otherwise you get 1900 and 2100 and 2200 as leap years and they are not.

I LOVE 88s/condition names.  I've programmed (professionally) in
COBOL/COBOLII,
Assembler, 'C', Basic/VB, and Delphi (plus other 'packages').
On the IBM Mainframe, VIASOFT (one of the very best COBOL tools) supports
'88' display / mods when used as a filler at the item name.

I PREFER all switches to be 'YES' or 'NO ' because 1) they stand out in
memory
2) I'm not doing math on them.

BTW Peter, why is it easier to 'grep' for WS-SOME-SWITCH-NAME
compared to FATAL-ERROR-UPDATING-SOME-MASTER ?

Quite often you find code like this:

                if .....
                            if .....
                                    MOVE 'Y'       TO
                                          WS-SOME-SWITCH
                            (OR )
                                    IF WS-SOME-SWITCH   =
                                        'Y'


you don't know what is being done unless you request enough 'before/after'
lines to print


PS.  I think if we're coding 'COBOL', we can be certain its a DIGITAL
computer
as opposed to an ANALOG computer - that would be something that understands
+5 Volts!

If everyone always agreed, the world would be pretty boring!

news:93m961$mr2$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net...

```
Hello all,

I'm currently studying the benefits and use of subprograms in COBOL.

One thing I thought was odd was that the variables passed to and from
cannot be group items. Does anyone have the facts or an opinion as to
why I can't pass a subdefined variable in a CALL statement?

Thanks,
Todd
```

#### ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T06:37:57+00:00

TFo wrote:
> 
> Hello all,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Todd

I have only coded USING parameters that are passed by reference.  I
have passed 01 records, group items at lower levels, and elementary
items.  I've done this in COBOL on several platforms, including
mainframe and PC.  The level number of the passed parameter is
unimportant.  The only thing I know you cannot do is try to pass a
literal as a USING parameter in a CALL statement, for the obvious
reasons.

What compiler and platform has this restriction?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** dw_french@NOSPAMyahoo.comNOSPAM (Dave French)
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T10:02:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a826d64.2128256@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A830312.8F8E8217@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Hi all.
I have made a speciality of mixed language programing or "using the
best tool for the job". As long as you abide by the rules for your
compiler, there is not reason why almost anything can be passed from
cobol to, lets say, c++ or c(++) to cobol.
You must have
1. The calling conventions must match
2. The parameter type and size must match
3. The by value/reference/content must match

If you abide by this, the world is your oyster. Ths software I write
uses (on Win32 with fujitsu cobol5 and ms c++6) a mixture of cobol, c,
c++ and a fragment of assembler. all link fine and it runs very well.
The cobol does the stuff cobol is good at, the assembler does its task
and so on.

If you need any advice on this subject, email me or message thru the
group and i will try and advise

Dave French,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A830312.8F8E8217@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Perhaps you meant as a RETURNING parameter.

Most of my COBOL programming has been in a mainframe environment. 
Call by reference seems to be the only way arguments are passed to
subprograms (except for EXTERNAL, but then there is no parameter
list).  Perhaps mainframe programmers aren't adventurous, because I've
never seen arguments passed BY CONTENT or BY VALUE.  They also don't
seem to use RETURNING.   

If you code something like this:

CALL MY-FAVORITE-SUBPROGRAM USING PARM-1, "USERNAME", PARM-3, 3.1415,
PARM-5.

...the compiler will flag each use of a literal as invalid in that
context (from COBOL for MVS & VM version 1.2.2) and the link edit step
will be bypassed.  If the compiler would allow me to do this, then the
subprogram could modify the contents of "USERNAME" or 3.1415 in the
calling program's literal pool.

A colleague of mine once spent a full day debugging someone else's
FORTRAN programm that had the disturbing feature of dividing 4 by 2
and getting an answer of 4.  He finally determined that a subprogram
had received the literal 2 as an argument and had changed it to a 1 in
the calling program.
```

case). Always something new:-)

LiamD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)

Bill Klein (Hi, Bill) writes ...

>Richard,
>   Do you really do
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The OS/390 compiler will not allow you to use "by reference" with a literal.

Related to another poster, there is just a ton of stuff in COBOL that's been
out there for a long time (over 15 years) but no one knows about it. Perhaps
it's as Arnold says, that mainframe programmers aren't adventurous. But I've
seen lots of mainframe programmers dig into the new stuff once they know it's
available. They just need the training (ahem) to know that it's available.

Take a look at this outline from our Beyond COBOL II course to get an idea:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/D204O.HTM  We spend the first half day going over
COBOL II features that many programmers don't know are available.

Don't know what it is right now, but mainframe programmers and managers seem
pretty complacent, assuming that nothing has changed, while totally missing all
the enormous changes that have been going on in COBOL, OS/390, z/OS and other
mainframe areas.

Cheers anyway,



Steve Comstock

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T01:22:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8497F0.6691DAC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A830312.8F8E8217@Azonic.co.nz> <9602t5$jqm$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Richard,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> is another matter - but by REFERENCE does seem "pretty unlikely" to me.

Program-1 _doesn't_ modify that passed parameter.  That is, when a
passed parameter is modified by the called program it should not be
passed as a literal, but there is no reason not to use these if it is
not modified.

for example: in Windows 16 bit API

    CALL "MessageBox"
        USING by value 0 size 2
              by reference "Error" & x"00"
              by reference "Don't do that, please" & x"00"
              by value MB-OK size 2
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T12:33:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961d9p$76m$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A830312.8F8E8217@Azonic.co.nz> <9602t5$jqm$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3A8497F0.6691DAC@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
What you "describe" is the entire difference between BY CONTENT and BY
REFERENCE.  All of you BY REFERENCE "literal" values should be changed to BY
CONTENT - if you know that the "called" program won't modify them.

(Does your compiler actually ACCEPT by REFERENCE in these cases - or are you
saying that it "should").

Again, BY VALUE is also a "different" issue than BY CONTENT/REFERENCE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T11:32:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8526B6.2CAE2C3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A830312.8F8E8217@Azonic.co.nz> <9602t5$jqm$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3A8497F0.6691DAC@Azonic.co.nz> <961d9p$76m$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> > for example: in Windows 16 bit API
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >               by value MB-OK size 2

> What you "describe" is the entire difference between BY CONTENT and BY
> REFERENCE.  All of you BY REFERENCE "literal" values should be changed to BY
> CONTENT - if you know that the "called" program won't modify them.

No. Wrong. Using a by content would create a copy of the original and
pass this copy by reference.  One would only need to use by content if
it were possible that the routine _would_ change the parameter (because
then it will change the copy). If you _know_ (as in this case) that the
routine won't change the parameter then it is perfectly safe to call by
reference and save the copying.

Actually 'by content' is unlikely to be safe where the routine changes
the parameter as it could still overwrite a longer area (unless told the
length) and corrupt other items in the copy area.

> (Does your compiler actually ACCEPT by REFERENCE in these cases - or are you
> saying that it "should").

Yes MF accepts by reference for a literal.

> Again, BY VALUE is also a "different" issue than BY CONTENT/REFERENCE.

Correct. But the issue actually was the claim that one _cannot_ use a
literal in a call using.  By content is a perfectly valid and standard
way of doing so.  By value is X-Open, by reference literal is usable
with MF (and others).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** "Don Nelson" <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T16:03:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<09Cm6.169$dl6.2267@gazette.loc1.tandem.com>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <96o4ms$2vs@netnews.hinet.net>`

```
The reason there was a prohibition against passing a subordinate group is
what is called "major-minor equivalence".  It is primarily involved with
machines that require computational fields to be aligned on word boundaries
or some sort of boundary.  If you pass a group the called program thinks
stuff is aligned correctly but it actually may not be.  This prohibition has
been removed in the proposed standard but it is still a bad idea to do it on
some systems.

Don Nelson

>
>TFo <not@this.time> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> cannot be group items. Does anyone have the facts or an opinion as to ...
```

Just one small point.   Try not to describe files within methods  -
instead do them at the object level.  Files were removed from methods
in the draft standard because they severely broke encapsulation.

#### ↳ Re: PowerCOBOL access use of MS Common Dialog

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T14:36:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95vkdg$dcd$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95ql50$5dg$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <20010207232316.23873.00001916@nso-cp.aol.com>`

```
Some quick comments below...

Sff5ky wrote in message <20010207232316.23873.00001916@nso-cp.aol.com>...


>I too am interested in the use of PowerCOBOL, but from my brief perusal
>of the form/sheet setup was put off by all the extra 'clicking' that seemed
>to be necssary.  Granted I have not been thru the tutorial recently.
>


Yes, it is a bit bewildering at first but that's because we aren't used to
it (well, at least I certainly wasn't...<G>)

Basically, it comes down to dropping controls onto a form in a graphic
environment, deciding what events you want to handle from the controls you
have dropped, and writing the appropriate code in COBOL.

What is happening is that all the code you would normally have to write
yourself (the windows message loop, raising events, creating, controlling,
repainting, and destroying windows) is all abstracted into a standard
template which you never see and don't have to worry about. All that is
visible to you is the COBOL code you write to handle events, and any common
routines (actually, nested sub-programs, but again, that is transparent
too...).

It is a little hard at first for COBOL programmers, used to developing text
with an Editor, compiling and linking it, to suddenly find a Graphic
Development Environment where you can do just about anything by simply
clicking a mouse...

Obviously, we still write specific code with an Editor, but PowerCOBOL takes
care of all the underlying stuff and the compiling and linking (you simply
build the project) and, more importantly, it ensures that the only code you
ever see is what you wrote yourself. This means you can focus on your own
code and remain removed from the vagaries of the Windows environment.


>I plan to have the GSGuide re-printed and push thru the projects
>so that I might get a better feel for the GUI development side of things.
>I've noticed that the POW.... event coding tends to be a bit cryptic.

In the normal COBOL environment we get used to controlling everything and
doing things when WE want to...Open the files, read the data, close the
files, etc.

I believe the single most difficult adjustment for COBOL programmers making
the transition into GUI programming is that GUI is event driven. You can't
tell the user "Hang on a minute! Don't click the mouse yet! I'm not
ready...!"

Once you make this transition and become familiar and comfortable with event
driven programming it is fascinating and rewarding. PowerCOBOL eases it by
allowing event code to written in COBOL. So you can say "Well, if you decide
to click on this control, here's what I want to happen..." If and when the
user does click on it, your code will be activated. You don't have to worry
about the actual activation of it, it is done for you.

It is simply COBOL. Maybe the examples are not the best. If you  (or anyone
here)have problems, mail me the project and I'll give you the background and
try and help.

>Early personal opinion is that I would rather develop the GUI in VB and
>call COBOL97 DLLs for the background processing.   (again I am
>thinking in the batch processing mode and not so much the interactive
>functions that most GUI apps are likely to be directed.)

Ah! I understand your reasons... VB is familiar and comfortable when it
comes to GUI. (And it is also very useful as a "Quick Build" prototyping
tool when we come to RAD projects (another subject very dear to my
heart...<G>)).

Believe me, there are huge similarities between using PowerCOBOL and
developing with VB in the VB/IDE.

It is very possible to develop the GUIs in COBOL (PowerCOBOL) AND call COBOL
97 .dlls from the same environment. There is a Fujitsu supplied module
called "POWEROPENFORM" but I have had problems getting it to work under
Version 5 on Win 98. Fujitsu assure me the problems are sorted in Version 6
which is due for release any time now.


Regards,

Pete.
```

<a id="t-d8ce351bc7"></a>
## Thread: Test Before and Do While — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Test Before and Do While

- **From:** Jim McNamara <mac3@slic.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-07T23:35:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A824C27.5E65DA23@slic.com>`

```
Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> Ummm, are you sure about that? Maybe it depends on what language you're using, but
>I don't see any necessary relationship between the difference between Do
>While/Do Until and the difference between testing at the beginning vs. the end of
>the loop. 

>If you test at the beginning of the loop then
>the While loop will not execute at all when the condition is false

Calvin-

  The following is taken verbatim from our text.
  ---------------------------------------------------------------
 

  The perform statement includes the Test Before and Test After
  phrases, corresponing to a do while and do until, respectively.
  Specification of test before tests the condition before perfoming
  the procedure, and corresponds to a do while.  Specification of
  Test After performs the procedure and then tests the condition,
  and corresponds to a do until. Omission of both test before and
  test after defaults to test before.
  ----------------------------------------------------------------
  Cobol from Micro to Mainframe
  3rd edition
  Robert Grauer, Carol Villar, Arthur Buss
  ISBN 0-13-085849-8
  page number 232

  some examples I made below... 
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 CONSTANTS.
    05    A                       PIC 9 VALUE 3.
    05    B                       PIC 9 VALUE 4.


 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
   PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE UNTIL A < B
    SUBTRACT 1 FROM A 
    DISPLAY 'IS ' A ' LESS THAN ' B ' ?'
    END-PERFORM.
    STOP RUN.

   Lets test it. Test Before tests the condition before executing
   statements. 3 is less than 4 is true and the statements aren't
   executed. If the above constant values are reversed, it loops
   twice until the condition is true (2 < 3).

   In paradox a while loop means repeats a sequence of statements
   as long as a specified condition is True.

while starts by evaluating the logical expression Condition. If
Condition is False,
 the Statements are not executed. If the value is True, the Statements
between the 
 Condition and endWhile are executed in sequence. 
 
        method pushButton(var eventInfo Event)
          while 4 < 3 
             msgstop ("Entered","Loop") {displays dialog box}
            {comment- while condition is false
              msgstop dialog not displayed} 
          endwhile
        endmethod

 Based on all this, I'm going to go out on a limb and say Test Before
does not 
correspond to a Do While in Cobol and the book is WRONG.  Calvin is
right and Meesha 
is probably confused.  Any comments or suggestions ???   
BTW I'm going into hiding if I'm wrong.
   
  Thanks
   jim
```

#### ↳ Re: Test Before and Do While

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T06:27:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A823C26.1C3F96D0@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3A824C27.5E65DA23@slic.com>`

```
Jim McNamara wrote:
> (snip)
>  Based on all this, I'm going to go out on a limb and say Test Before
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

PERFORM UNTIL with TEST BEFORE (either explicitly or as the standard
default), is an initial test loop.  In that sense it is a DO WHILE
loop.  But the standard definition of a structured programming DO
WHILE loop is to execute the iteratated statements WHILE a condition
is true, and exit the loop when the condition is false.  

PERFORM UNTIL with TEST AFTER is a bottom-tested loop, and exits on a
true condition.  So TEST AFTER looks exactly like a structured DO
UNTIL.

The difference is that in COBOL the PERFORM UNTIL always exits on a
TRUE condition, whether it's an initial test loop or a bottom tested
loop.  Structured DO WHILE is an initial test loop that exits on a
FALSE condition.  Structured DO UNTIL is a bottom-tested loop that
exits on a TRUE condition.

COBOL's PERFORM UNTIL with TEST BEFORE is more like "DO WHILE
NOT(condition)".  I would accept it as a DO WHILE because it's easy to
invert TRUE and FALSE, and the most important consideration for me is
whether the exit test is first or last.   

The iterated statements in an initial test loop (DO WHILE) may be
bypassed completely because the exit condition is always tested
first.  The iterated statements in a bottom-tested loop (DO UNTIL)
will always be executed at least once, because the exit test is at the
end of the iterated statements.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Test Before and Do While

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T08:42:56-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A82DAA0.972966F1@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<3A824C27.5E65DA23@slic.com> <3A823C26.1C3F96D0@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> Jim McNamara wrote:
> > (snip)
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://arnold.trembley.home.att.net/

Of interest to me is why you say that a DO WHILE loop is initial test & a
DO UNTIL loop is bottom test. WHILE & UNTIL don't relate to where the test
is performed. In fact in the languages I'm familiar with (that have both)
they're linguistically inverse. Your definition seems to make them
linguistically perverse. (Sorry-couldn't resist.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Test Before and Do While

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T06:53:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8393A6.15CC29C3@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3A824C27.5E65DA23@slic.com> <3A823C26.1C3F96D0@worldnet.att.net> <3A82DAA0.972966F1@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> (snip)
> Of interest to me is why you say that a DO WHILE loop is initial test & a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> linguistically perverse. (Sorry-couldn't resist.)

I learned structured programming concepts in a flowcharting class back
in 1978.  We didn't write any programs in that class, we flowcharted
the solutions.  DO WHILE, when used in pseudocode or a flowchart for
design, was defined as an initial test loop with exit on a false
condition.  DO UNTIL was defined as a bottom tested loop with exit on
a true condition.  

DO WHILE and DO UNTIL are structured programming concepts, and don't
necessarily correspond to any specific programming language syntax.

I can't claim credit for these definitions, they probably belong to
Wirth or Knuth or some other big thinker.  So you don't need to
apologize to me!

With kindest regards,
```

## Thread: Subroutines — 1 msgs

### Re: Subroutines

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T07:55:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A82510B.98FC4FD9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net> <95gdnb$jp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <95lnv6$9fm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95qik6$11ua$1@news.hitter.net> <95qvp3$rp8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Stefan Meyer wrote:
> 
> Sorry no. The is defined as indexed with access dynamic. The main-prog
> opens the file ReadOnly (cause it doesn't make any updates) and the
> subprog needs the file opened I/O (cause it will do some updates).

It is possible to have the file open as I-O and then _not_ do any
updates (gasp, shock, horror).  
The only difference is the locking.  Use MANUAL locking and only WITH
LOCK when a record is required for updating.  Or use WITH NO LOCK and/or
WITH IGNORE LOCK.

Alternately keep track of current open status and CLOSE/OPEN as
appropriate.  That is have a flag 'Open-mode' that is initailly space. 
When the routine is called with a function 'read' then open the file
INPUT and set the flag to, say, 'R'.  If a function (part of linkage) or
'read for update' arrives and the flag is 'R' then CLOSE and OPEN I-O
before reading and set the flag to 'U'.  If the flag is of the correct
mode then there is no need to CLOSE and reOPEN.
 
> > [...]
> > >Sorry if my intention to ask for subroutines was/is not clear.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's what I expected.

Get over it.  You have been obsessed with trying to get two subroutines
instead of actually trying to solve the problem.

In fact how would having two instances of the _same_ subroutine help ? 
They would still need code to OPEN INPUT or OPEN I-O as appropriate, it
would still need to be told which is required (or was it going to be a
psychic routine that _knew_ what was required ?).

Just OPEN the file I-O and use manual locking.
```

<a id="t-4185220c4e"></a>
## Thread: TinyCOBOL release 0.50 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### TinyCOBOL release 0.50

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T05:45:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8278BF.94B90178@arvotek.net>`

```
The TinyCOBOL compiler version 0.50 is available at the TinyCOBOL home
page (1) and iBiblio (2).

This release contains bugs fixes, and code enhancements, including
updates to the math verbs, the EVALUATE statement, file OPEN/CLOSE
statements, file LINE SEQUENTIAL IO.

The CALL BY CONTENT, JUSTIFIED RIGHT, and REPORT SECTION (preliminary)
statements have been added to the COBOL grammar.

The DISPLAY/ACCEPT LINE/POSITION statements have been added, as an
extension to the standard COBOL grammar.

Sample programs with CGI, SQL, and GUI interfaces have been added to the
source distribution. 


Please report any bugs and/or problems to the TinyCOBOL mailing list. 


1) TinyCOBOL Home Page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/

2) iBiblio (formerly Metalab)
   http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

yes!

004958
004959 D-BEHANDLA-FEL-TTYP SECTION.
004960
004961     MOVE 'X'    TO FELPOSTER
004962
004963     MOVE C35R010-TTYP TO FEL-TTYP
004964
004965     DOWHILE IN1-POSTER-KVAR
004966       AND C35R010-FORVNR   = FORVNR IN C35CFORV
004967       AND C35R010-RSKPAR   = AKT-RSKPAR
004968       AND C35R010-REDOVDAT =  AKT-REDOVDAT
004969       AND C35R010-TTYP     = FEL-TTYP
004970           PERFORM XB-SKRIV-FELPOST
004971           PERFORM XA-NESTA-INPOST
004972     DOEND
004973
004974
004975 D-END.
004976/PAGE

that is, if you the PRECOSP precompiler.

<a id="t-944d8ce3d2"></a>
## Thread: Returning a variable to a batch file (DOS) — 3 msgs

### Returning a variable to a batch file (DOS)

- **From:** "Gary Susott" <susott@atta-boy.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T08:26:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bTyg6.23763$iN1.170565@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com>`

```
I am using NetExpress 3.1 with all flavors of windows and DOS on a Novell
Network. We have a batch file which includes running a menu program (Jobctl)
which was created in the DOS version on Merant MF.  As part of this program,
it creates a unique directory to run the selected job for each user.  The
ending path would look like F:\users\%user%\jobctl.999 (Where 999 is
replaced by a number from 1 to 999).  The current directory is then changed
to this new path and a temporary batch file created to run the selected job.
The program exits.  The origional batch file then runs the temporary batch
file.

With NetExpress, when the JOBCTL program exits, although the new path and
batch file have been created (and the current directory changed) the current
directory reverts back to the current directory of the batch file.  All my
research says that the environment that NetExpress runs in disappears (and
doesn't affect any other environments) when the program terminates; with
possibly the exception of the returning option (as in "exit program
returning xxxx") .   I am looking to return the extension to the jobctl
directory to the batch file.

Where does the returning variable reside in memory and is there any way that
DOS can access it?  Might there be another method to pass this 3 digit
variable back (in addition to creating yet another file to be read?
```

#### ↳ Re: Returning a variable to a batch file (DOS)

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T22:04:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PJEg6.86515$KP3.24447078@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<bTyg6.23763$iN1.170565@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com>`

```
You can probably use the RETURN-CODE register to do this. The batch file
would have to test ERRORLEVEL which would make for a 1000 lines of 'IF'
statements. You might be better off having the main batch file provide the
name of the temporary file to your COBOL program rather than the other way
around. Then all the batch file needs to do is test for it's existence after
the COBOL program completes.

<a id="t-3a646e33d6"></a>
## Thread: EvalIt32 is a 32-bit library that allows programs to evaluate math expressions defined at run time. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### EvalIt32 is a 32-bit library that allows programs to evaluate math expressions defined at run time.

- **From:** "Igor Evsikov" <ievsikov@flowpath.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T15:14:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6Dg6.454$HX5.69416025@radon.golden.net>`

```
EvalIt32 is a 32-bit library that allows programs to evaluate math
expressions defined at run time. EvalIt32 includes functions etEvalIt and
etCompEval for evaluate math expression, etEvalFile to evaluate math
expressions from input files, and writes results to output files. This .dll
supports common math operators and functions for real and complex numbers,
as well as user variables and functions. This version includes direct
support for Visual Basic, C++ Builder (Borland) and Delphi. Other compilers
can use modified versions of a headers.Requires Win9x/NT/2000.

Download:
http://www.zdnet.com/downloads/stories/info/0,,001EU4,.html

Features:

      - Arithmetic and logical operators and functions
      - Common functions such as exp, ln, sqrt, sqr, bnml etc.
      - Common, trigonometric, hyperbolic complex functions
      - Trigonometric, Hyperbolic  functions
      - Numerical Integration
      - Equations can be solved
      - Special functions (Gamma, Bessel's, Si, Ci, erf, erfc, Fresnel's)
      - Statistic functions (Average, Standard deviation, Sum, Random,
        Gauss random, statistical variance, etc )
      - FOR-type loop
      - if (...) then (...) else (...) function
      - Error position fixed
      - Print results, graphics and print preview
      - Save graphics to BMP, WMF, EMF formats
      - Radian and Degree modes for trigonometric functions
      - Precision: 10-12 significant digits.
      - Range: _(3.4E-4392 to 1.1E+4392)
      - User define functions(only profesional edition)
      - User defined constants

Special requirements: None.

Shareware.  Uploaded by the author.

Igor Evsikov
ievsikov@flowpath.com
```
