## Thread: Use of SECTION — 90 this month / 91 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Use of SECTION

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T22:35:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I was wondering if you are using sections in the procedure division?

I noticed an information message telling me that some commands are not
in a section.  This is from an old program, and I have never used
sections in the procedure division.  I was wondering if you could give
me a pointer on how to use them?  do you start the first line after
procedure division and call it a section or what?

I couldn't find much in my IBM Language Reference on this topic. It
seems they just have one section declared, so I didn't see the point to
using it.

I program in COBOL LE on an IBM VSE Mainframe.


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T15:58:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com wrote:

> I was wondering if you are using sections in the procedure division?

They are common in Europe, and used to be required for CoBOL SORT
procedures.   In my environment, we have IDMS COPY commands which use
sections, so to avoid warning messages, I put a dummy section name at the
top of my procedure division.

I dislike any drop through code, and thus shun perform thru and sections as
much as possible.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T13:57:33+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net>`

```
I believe there is still a good case for using sections in COBOL.

With the advent of "Go To" less programming, I see more and more students
trying to put EVERYTHING in line (No, I am NOT advocating that we all go
back to using Go To...<G>) with a plethora of scope delimiters at the bottom
of the code.

Time and again students send me code, unable to find an obscure logic bug
that becomes visible as soon as the program is structured properly into
functions.

Most of them are unaware of the availability of SECTION and so they perform
paragraphs. While you can get away with this for relatively simple batch
processing, it complicates things when this code is maintained (new code is
added) and/or there are time constraints on its execution (as there are in
an on-line environment).

Using SECTIONs and thinking in terms of SECTIONing code, I believe makes the
program much easier to maintain.

I encourage people to make a SECTION for each box on their structure or
Block diagram. In this way the thinking is "compartmented" into functions
and it is very quick and easy to know where to go when problems arise. It
also helps to remove duplicated logic which is another cause of errors in
in-line code.

For programs which are time critical, I DO advocate using a Go To to get out
of the SECTION via a common EXIT.

Here's my case: (This is an extract from the advice I give people who ask
for it).

=================== begin quote ===============================

You are probably wondering why I have emphasised the use of SECTIONs, when
there seems to be little difference between a single section and a single
paragraph.

A SECTION can contain multiple paragraphs and this has implications.

Consider the following:
*----------------------------------------------------------
Xample section.
X000.
    Some codeï¿½
     If <some condition> [we want to bypass all the remaining code and get
out of the section.]
         Go to x999
     End-if
     More codeï¿½
     .
X999.
     Exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------
Nextbit  section.
N000.
And so onï¿½

I am sure you are being taught NOT to use GO TO, but the above is one of the
cases where its use is really justified. The important thing is to make sure
that your GO TO does NOT branch outside the section it is defined in.
(ï¿½GO TO N000ï¿½ in the above would be disastrousï¿½)
In most cases there is no need to exit the current section, but in some
time-critical processes, it is important NOT to execute any more
instructions than is absolutely necessary, so this GO TO gets us off the
hookï¿½

When you execute the Xample section you do so as follows:

Perform Xample  [Note that we perform the SECTION name. This will execute
all paragraphs within the section]

Whether you exited prematurely via the GO TO, or you went right through to
the exit, control will return to the statement following your Perform in
exactly the same way, just as you expect it to.

You could not achieve this effect by using a single paragraph rather than
using a section.

[ASIDE:
 You COULD achieve the same effect by using PERFORMï¿½THRU. For example ï¿½

Perform X000 thru x999

The problem with this (apart from it being ugly <G>) is that there are
implications for maintenance.

Suppose another paragraph is added AFTER x999? Maybe a common process that
MUST be done, whether we took the GO TO or not. We must now remember to
amend the PERFORM to change its range. If we had used a section, this would
be one thing less to have to think about.

It may sound a bit trivial but in the real world it might be necessary to
add several paragraphs at different points in the process flow and the logic
might be complex.

PERFORMï¿½THRU is best avoided. Use sections.

END ASIDE]

===================== end quote ================

I realise that many here will disagree, and I also respect the rights of
individual programmers to use whatever is comfortable for them.

My experience in working with students over the last few years has shown me
that SECTIONing code, drastically cuts the error rate.

Pete.

PS. I really don't have time to debate this publicly here and cannot
guarantee to respond to posts on it. I only look at CLC occasionally. If you
are genuinely concerned about it or have questions or want examples and
clarification, please mail me privately at: dashwood@enternet.co.nz

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net>...

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

> Time and again students send me code, unable to find an obscure logic bug
> that becomes visible as soon as the program is structured properly into
> functions.

Not wishing to start a flame war or even disagreeing with your thoughts on
Sections but... the use of term 'properly' (as in correctly) stikes me as a
little 'subjective'. Structuring into function is only one way to solve the
problem of organization - is it proven to be more correct than others?

And, further on the use of Sections - there used to be (probably still is)
hardware architectures that page memory using variable length segments and
if I recall correctly - on some Unisys (was Burroughs then) systems the
compiler used to use the program Sections to determine these.

Brian Stanley
```

```
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

I have seen errors with this logic.  Someone puts a paragraph after a section,
now becoming implicitly part of the perform.   I don't see any difference
between using PERFORM THRU or PERFORM section.  They're one and the same (now
that we don't have memory segmentation), with the same problems.

I want my compiler to generate a warning whenever a drop through is possible -
especially when EXIT PARAGRAPH is available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T14:04:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A854A8A.8E47C432@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A83FD31.F80FF640@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I have seen errors with this logic.  Someone puts a paragraph after a section,
> now becoming implicitly part of the perform.   

In some cases this may not be detected by testing.  The extra paragraph
that is perfromed by being an unwanted part of the section may be quite
innocuous, until one day at 2am ....

> I don't see any difference
> between using PERFORM THRU or PERFORM section.  They're one and the same (now
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> especially when EXIT PARAGRAPH is available.

I wrote an informal proposal to the standards committe that would add
label qualifiers to section and paragraph labels.  These would set the
valid uses that the label could be referrenced and thus provide warnings
and/or compiler errors to avoid the potential bugs that exist with
performed sections.

(see, I am not an avid anti-perform-section advocate, or even an
anti-goto advocate, I am only an anti-bug advocate. With label
qualifiers these areas exposed by perform section could be eliminated).

In Cobol there is only one label type (well two if sections are counted
as different, but they are the same 'type' of label).  These labels may
be referenced in various ways: PERFORM, THRU, GO TO, drop through,
SORT/etc procedures, ..

In other languages different _types_ of labels are available, each for a
specific type of reference.  In C there are procedures that can only be
called, labels that can only be goto'ed.

The flexability of Cobol (or inability to specify the type of label)
leads to many classes of potential bugs that do not (or cannot) occur in
other languages.  One way to eliminate these bugs is to restrict labels
so that they can _only_ be used in specific ways.  One _provable_ way of
doing this is to eliminate GO TO (hence gotoless programming) and to
eliminate sections in PD (actually it would be equally valid to
eliminate paragraphs and only have sections but that is far harder to
get right and to check for).

Another way is to add restrictions to each label, by way of qualifiers,
such that their misuse is detected by the compiler.

The Qualifiers were (from memory):

    LOCAL -reference from outside current section invalid
    PERFORMED - may only be performed, not dropped into or go to'ed
    FROM - may only be subject of GO TO
    INTO - may be dropped into.

These may be used in combination.
    
one might add NULL for a commentary or structure label that may not be
referenced.  This could be useful to add section headers for overlaying
that may not be performed.

This means that the code might be:

    Process-something PERFORMED SECTION.
    PS-Start NULL.                         (or LOCAL)

        IF (condition)
            GO TO PS-exit
            .
        more code
        .
     PS-exit LOCAL FROM.
        EXIT.

     Another-pargraph PERFORMED.
     ....

We can see here that several bugs can now be detected by the compiler. 
For example a GO TO wrong-exit would be detected because it would be
non-local.  A perform of PS-Start (mistakenly done instead of the
section) would give an error.  It should find that both PS-exit and
Another-paragraph will be dropped into (a GOTO PS-exit would be
_required_ at the end of the previous paragraph).  Note that EXIT does
not exit, many newbies make the mistake of thinking that EXIT is like
C's return leading to nasty bugs.

These changes would help to eliminate the potential for bugs that
performed sections inherently have.  With these changes to the language,
performed sections could be as safe as performed single paragraphs and I
would have no objection to this being taught.  Without these changes the
students are being misled into bad practices (IMHO).

Note that the problem with programs that have GO TO's is _not_ at the GO
TO, the problem is at the label (I have another thesis on this).
```

```
Jerry P wrote:

> > >one might add NULL for a commentary or structure label that may not
> be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >

> Wasn't this the "NOTE" paragraph that was declared "Archaic,"
> "Obsolete," and a "Public Outlaw" all in the same year (back in '55)?
> I think the banishment had something to do with the Air Force
> accidentally ordering 16,000,000 tins of anchovies (the yellow kind,
> not the round ones).

No.

And it wouldn't have been 1955.

The NOTE paragraph used the word 'NOTE' and made all the content in the
paragraph a comment.

This proposal was for label qualifiers.  Having a 'NULL' label would
make that label unable to be referenced but would have no effect on the
content of the pargraph.  This would make a 'PERFORM PS-Start' an error,
but the code could still be executed with 'PERFORM Process-Something'.

Some like to have paragraph labels after section headers to make it
'tidy' but the label should not be referenced. (where 'referenced' means
used in a GO TO or PERFORM etc).
```

```
it!
```

```
I am aware of no other language that has as many devices
for dividing problems and for controlling and hiding complexity
than COBOL. I am aware of no other language where the
use of such devices is avoided "like the plague."

Yes! Use sections to divide the problem.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T12:42:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961dm4$f1i$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <9616ce$2fj7$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
(all prior discussion cut)

One thing that still "surprises" me is the GENERAL (not true in ALL cases)
division between US and Europe on Paragraphs vs Sections (in the Procedure
Division).

 - It is unusual (not totally unheard of) to find SECTIONS in "new" code in
the US
 - It is "common" (but not universal) to find SECTIONS in "new" code in
Europe

(or at least that was my experience with both Micro Focus and IBM mainframe
users).

Personally, given the introduction (in some compilers now - and in the next
Standard) of EXIT PARAGRAPH,  I still "recommend" (for maintainability):

1) Never use Sections (in the Procedure Division <G>)
2) Never use PERFORM para1 THRU para2 (even if para2 is "just" an EXIT)
3) Never use GO TO

There is plenty of evidence that ALL programming "algorithms" can be coded
this way.  Obviously, if you are "maintaining" a program that uses one or
more of these constructs, it might be useful to "maintain" the current
style.  However, for "new" code, I just don't see where/why using any of
these really "helps" - and they CAN all lead to programming errors in
maintenance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T01:48:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96816j$19m7$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <9616ce$2fj7$1@news.hitter.net> <961dm4$f1i$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think it only fair to include the information from Micro Focus
as given in their COBOL User Guide, 3.2, April 1994,
page 4-12 - 4-13.

# Perform sections, not paragraphs. Put EXIT PROGRAM
   and STOP RUN statements at the end of the first (main)
   section.
# Only use GO TO to paragraphs within the same section.
# Do not use PERFORM ... THRU statements.

I like the above because it allows the occasional GO TO
in a controlled manner.

Steve McConnell, in "Code Complete," page 780, wrote:
"Maintenance programmers spend 50 to 60 percent of their
time trying to understand the code they have to maintain ... ."

Anything, and I do mean anything, that tells the maintainer,
clearly and unmistakably, what the code does, is better
than adherence to dogma. My biggest complaint is that
many flags are lies and flags that hide GO TO are big lies.

Using structuring methods that are too restrictive means
the programmer will need, occasionally, to lie to the
maintainer. When that happens the code will be more
difficult to maintain. When the methods are too loose,
the programmer may fail to create maintainable code.

The Micro Focus list seems about right, to me.
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <961dm4$f1i$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>...
[...]
>Personally, given the introduction (in some compilers now - and in the next
>Standard) of EXIT PARAGRAPH,  I still "recommend" (for maintainability):
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>maintenance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T18:33:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> I believe there is still a good case for using sections in COBOL.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the SECTION via a common EXIT.

So, you are saying that with 'GO TO less' programming, here is a way to
avoid being 'GO TO less' and to use GO TO.  Hmmmm.

> You are probably wondering why I have emphasised the use of SECTIONs, when
> there seems to be little difference between a single section and a single
> paragraph.
> 
> A SECTION can contain multiple paragraphs and this has implications.

While SECTION headers may provide a commentary, and is useful for
overlaying, the _only_ reason to PERFORM a SECTION rather than a single
paragraph is to allow for a GO TO.  There is no other reason.

PERFORMing single paragraphs (even if they are grouped in SECTIONs for,
say, overlaying) disallows GO TO.  This is the single most important
reason for only performing single paragraphs.  

By allowing the PERFORM of a SECTION (or the use of THRU) you allow GO
TOs and also allow the introduction of several classes of bugs which
cannot exist when only single paragraphs can be PERFORMed.

> Consider the following:
> *----------------------------------------------------------
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> cases where its use is really justified. 

There are __NO__ cases where it is justified.  You are simply making the
code more complex than necessary, introducing an unnecessary overhead
for each 'function' and then claiming that a 'short cut' GO TO is
'really justified', but this is only because of all the extraneous
overhead that you clutter the code with.

The overhead is:
	adding SECTION header, 
	checking after function that another SECTION exists,
	adding paragraph-exit,
	checking that GO TOs go to correct exit.

The simple way of doing this is:

        X000.
             some code
             IF <some condition>
                 PERFORM X000-condition-true
             ELSE
                 PERFORM X000-condition-false
             END-IF
             .
        X000-condition-true.
             code
        X000-condition-false.
             code
        N000.
             etc
	
When we cut out all the overhead and stop thinking about taking
short-cuts to by-pass it, we create new paragraphs as required _without_
ever having to worry about drop-through, or wrong exit, or making sure
that there is a SECTION to terminate the PERFORM.		


> The important thing is to make sure
> that your GO TO does NOT branch outside the section it is defined in.
> (ï¿½GO TO N000ï¿½ in the above would be disastrousï¿½)

as would leaving off the NextBit SECTION header, or extracting some code
as a performed paragraph and putting it any near this code.

Forget SECTIONs and you will have many fewer opportunities for bugs.

> In most cases there is no need to exit the current section, but in some
> time-critical processes, it is important NOT to execute any more
> instructions than is absolutely necessary, so this GO TO gets us off the
> hookï¿½

Writing hard real-time systems in Cobol are we ?

I have written interactive applications running as .INT interpreted byte
code on a 5MHz 8085 as a MULTI-USER system under MP/M with CIS Cobol. 
It wasn't the speed of the code that made it slow it was disk access.

On Windows with a reasonable CPU the redraw speed of the screen is far
longer than any code short cuts would save.

As there are other, more effective, ways of avoiding code execution that
would make _zero_ difference to any interactive system then you have no
justification for creating bug traps.
 
> When you execute the Xample section you do so as follows:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Whether you exited prematurely via the GO TO, or you went right through to

> the exit, control will return to the statement following your Perform in
> exactly the same way, just as you expect it to.

Except when it does strange things because it hasn't been done quite
carefully enough.

> You could not achieve this effect by using a single paragraph rather than
> using a section.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Perform X000 thru x999

Even worse, don't get me started on THRU.
 
> The problem with this (apart from it being ugly <G>) is that there are
> implications for maintenance.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> be one thing less to have to think about.

And if we only ever PERFORMed single paragraphs that gives about half a
dozen less things to think about, all of them potential bugs.
 
> It may sound a bit trivial but in the real world it might be necessary to
> add several paragraphs at different points in the process flow and the logic
> might be complex.

The __ONLY__ reason for having several paragraphs in a SECTION is to
cater for spagetti code.  While a paragraph at the start (actually
unnecessary) and an exit-paragraph caters for a short-cut exit, such as
may be achieved EXIT PARAGRAPH in the next standard, the additional
paragraphs can only be useful as targets for GO TO spagetti code, or
perhaps PERFORM paragraph and GO TO over it.

You are inventing 'GO TO less'-less programming, ie completely
unstructured spagetti.

> My experience in working with students over the last few years has shown me
> that SECTIONing code, drastically cuts the error rate.

Compared to what ?

> clarification, please mail me privately at: dashwood@enternet.co.nz
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T02:25:09+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard,

as you posted this privately as well as here I will quickly respond.

1. You are quoting me out of context in order to support your own viewpoint.

2. I really don't care how you personally do it or whether or not you
personally use sections or paragraphs; and I am certainly not out to convert
the GO TO Less Evangelists.

My concern is to see that people who have not yet acquired the experience to
make their own decisions are aware of the arguments for using sectioned
code. And also, in passing, to make the point that there are some exceptions
to many "rules". I am NOT advocating spaghetti code, and it is unworthy of
you to try and push my argument to that extreme...<G>

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz>...
>Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

>> For programs which are time critical, I DO advocate using a Go To to get
out
>> of the SECTION via a common EXIT.
>
>So, you are saying that with 'GO TO less' programming, here is a way to
>avoid being 'GO TO less' and to use GO TO.  Hmmmm.


NO! I am saying that  there ARE some cases where GO TO is justified; like,
processes which are TIME CRITICAL...



>While SECTION headers may provide a commentary, and is useful for
>overlaying, the _only_ reason to PERFORM a SECTION rather than a single
>paragraph is to allow for a GO TO.  There is no other reason.

You just gave TWO reasons for doing it, then said there is ONLY one...!
What about simply documenting code as functions and relating back to a
structure chart? I wouldn't mind betting there are many more paragraphs in a
program than are documented on the appropriate structure chart or Block
diagram. SECTIONing it gives a "Top Down" view towards isolating functions
and can be tied back to the original structure. For students this eliminates
many logic errors and shows them immediately where to look.


>PERFORMing single paragraphs (even if they are grouped in SECTIONs for,
>say, overlaying) disallows GO TO.  This is the single most important
>reason for only performing single paragraphs.

If you believe in the TRUTH of GO TO less programming. Us Atheists have a
different view... I use whatever works.

>
>By allowing the PERFORM of a SECTION (or the use of THRU) you allow GO
>TOs and also allow the introduction of several classes of bugs which
>cannot exist when only single paragraphs can be PERFORMed.

What makes you think I can't code a GO TO in a program that has no
sections...???
(I wouldn't do it, but the compiler certainly won't stop me...)


>
>> Consider the following:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>      If <some condition> [we want to bypass all the remaining code and
get
>> out of the section.]
>>          Go to x999
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I am sure you are being taught NOT to use GO TO, but the above is one of
the
>> cases where its use is really justified.



>There are __NO__ cases where it is justified.

BOLLOCKS! I have given an example where it IS justified...

>You are simply making the
>code more complex than necessary, introducing an unnecessary overhead
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There is absoutely NO  (object code) overhead in adding a SECTION Header
(but I suspect you know that...)


>The overhead is:
> adding SECTION header,
> checking after function that another SECTION exists,
> adding paragraph-exit,
> checking that GO TOs go to correct exit.


Ah! It's a CODING overhead you're talking about...

If you NORMALLY write SECTIONed code you won't perceive it as an overhead.
You would only have a GO TO under exceptional circumstances and therefore it
is NOT a regular overhead (coding or otherwise...). It all comes down to the
way you code.


>
>The simple way of doing this is:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>that there is a SECTION to terminate the PERFORM.

OK, that's fair. So instead of SECTIONs we have a plethora of paragraphs...

It comes down to personal preference. I have expressed mine; you have
expressed yours. I don't expect ever to change your mind, and on this
occasion, you certainly haven't changed mine.

>
>Forget SECTIONs and you will have many fewer opportunities for bugs.


Forget computer programming and you will NEVER have any bugs...

>Writing hard real-time systems in Cobol are we ?

Have been known to...<G>


>I have written interactive applications running as .INT interpreted byte
>code on a 5MHz 8085 as a MULTI-USER system under MP/M with CIS Cobol.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>longer than any code short cuts would save.


Wow! Am I supposed to be impressed?! Fail to see the "point" of your
argument.

Try writing a routine to operate the valves in an on-line oil refinery (or
any real time process) and see what happens when you close the valve 30
milliseconds too late because you were executing code irrelevant to the
established condition, just because you wouldn't code a GO TO...

(While I have great respect for your technical prowess, Richard, you are
talking to someone whose been around a long time and done most of
it....don't try and blind me with science...) Your bullshit argument re
Windows is nothing to do with a time critical process and you know it...Oh,
and BTW, NOBODY writes time critical code in .INT the time to interpret it
renders it non-viable.


>Except when it does strange things because it hasn't been done quite
>carefully enough.


If you write computer programs without doing things "quite carefully enough"
they will fail to work, IRRESPECTIVE of whether you use SECTIONs or not...

>
>Even worse, don't get me started on THRU.


OK, we can agree on this one. Hey! Everybody! Don't use PERFORM...THRU

Can we get it removed from the forthcoming standard...sorry Bill...<G>


While this is fun, it is kind of pointless and I've run out of time...
>
>> My experience in working with students over the last few years has shown
me
>> that SECTIONing code, drastically cuts the error rate.
>
>Compared to what ?
>
Compared to coding everything in-line with a heap of scope delimiters at the
bottom of the code. This is a logical extension from using
paragraphs...("Hey, I can avoid having paragraphs at all if I code it all in
line..." And that neat END-PERFORM can go at the bottom of everything)

(I did say that, but you obviously only see what you want to...)

That's it from me... I really don't care how any of you write your code.
Actually, I respect totally your right to do it however you think best. I
posted originally because the subject was raised, and I have seen it causing
problems with the people I tutor.
I use SECTIONs. My programs work. Both structured and Object Oriented. That
is not to imply that yours won't or don't; merely to demonstrate the
futility of this argument.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T19:11:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961fde$es6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Well said, Pete.

As you pointed out, there is no single "RIGHT" way to code a COBOL
program. We all develop our preferred methods for writing code and
structuring programs. I've always thought that one of COBOL's great
strengths was that it provided more than one way to achieve your
objective. As users of the tool (COBOL), it is up to us to learn its
power and how and when to apply it effectively to solve a given
problem. So many people fall into the trap of limiting the power of the
tool by adamantly avoiding certain features.

When it comes to structuring a program and deciding what functions to
use, it is based on personal preferences and shop standards. There is
no right or wrong way, only a "Preferred" way. What I prefer is not
necessarily the same as what you prefer, and there is nothing wrong
with that.

Flame wars, or "Crusades against the Infidels", as I call them, are so
ridiculous and such a waste of time. we should be sharing ideas,
techniques and experiences openly without trying to push our way
as "THE ONLY WAY". I joined this forum to learn, and, on occasion, help
someone else learn. Except for the "Keepers of the Grail", I have
thoroughly enjoyed and benefited from the exchange between my fellow
forum members.

I've been writing COBOL since 1964 and consider myself to be
proficient, yet I continue to learn new and better ways to write code
in COBOL. The way I write a program today is not the same as it was a
year ago, much less 10 years ago. I look for ways to improve and become
more efficient. When I find them, I incorporate them. To do otherwise,
is to become obsolete. We all know what happened to the dinosaurs,
don't we?

Don't let the narrow/small/shallow-mindedness of some forum members
keep you from contributing. It would be a great loss for the rest of us.

John Chafin
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T20:19:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A845265.3468126D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <961fde$es6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


John Chafin wrote:

> Well said, Pete.<snip>
>
> Don't let the narrow/small/shallow-mindedness of some forum members
> keep you from contributing. It would be a great loss for the rest of us.
>

He is such a 'modest' bugger, (but he is so often so right !) - he will love
your last comment <G>

Back to Sections. When I was first stumbling and 'teaching myself' COBOL
using a Radio Shack and RM/COBOL '74 - yes I used Sections. I had to;
limited to 64K I had to prioritize and use sections as overlays ( 50 upwards
as I recall ). Since switching to a PC environment I have neither used nor
seen the necessity for sections. OO-wise - sections are irrelevant.

When the new Standard arrives, giving both OO and UDF(User Defined
Functions), we may well see Structured programs moving over to UDF - kind of
replaces all those copy file routines and calling of sub-programs.. Even
without going whole hog on OO, you could judiciously use customized OO
classes with Structured.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T10:58:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> 1. You are quoting me out of context in order to support your own viewpoint.

What context do you think I was out of ?

> 2. I really don't care how you personally do it or whether or not you
> personally use sections or paragraphs; and I am certainly not out to convert
> the GO TO Less Evangelists.

Yes you were.  You posted the original to try to convince people that
perform section and GO TO were a viable option for use.

> My concern is to see that people who have not yet acquired the experience to
> make their own decisions are aware of the arguments for using sectioned
> code. 

Then you do them a dis-service.

In order to _safely_ use performed sections there is a need for checking
the code that is beyond the compiler.  This can be done using fixed
conventions and a program checker such as Judson does.  Without that the
code will be far too error prone and labourious.  This may be acceptable
in tiny noddy programs, but quickly becomes an unnecessary burden in
production programs.

> And also, in passing, to make the point that there are some exceptions
> to many "rules". I am NOT advocating spaghetti code, and it is unworthy of
> you to try and push my argument to that extreme...<G>

You advocated performing sections and then stated one may need several
paragraphs.  What purpose _other_ than spagetti code would these
paragraph labels serve ?

Actually they _may_ be performed and jumped-over.  This, however, leads
to both performs of sections and of paragraphs and thus to
inconsistency.  This gives whole new classes of bugs awaiting the
unweary (or inexperienced, which is who you are aimed at).

> NO! I am saying that  there ARE some cases where GO TO is justified; like,
> processes which are TIME CRITICAL...

Name one such case in the context of teaching students.

In fact name any case where this can be justified at all, ever, in the
context of Cobol applications.

One of the reasons that you may feel it is justified is that you
advocate _heavyweight_ procedures.  To perform sections there are
several coding overheads that must be done (checking headers, giving it
a number, placing it in the code at some point, adding exit).  This cost
(compared to other strategies) leads to large unwieldy sections that are
difficult to exit from.  This is because it is always easier to 'add a
bit more here' than to create a new procedure to perform.  Once you have
large unwieldy structures then GO TO is a 'quick way out'.

However, using lightweight procedures (performed paragraphs), the cost
of breaking the code down into simple structures is very low.  GO TO is
not needed to 'jump over the mess', there is _NO_TIME_PENALTY_.

case1:  A SECTION.
        A1.
           IF ( condition )
               GO TO A1-exit.
           some code
           .
        A1-exit.
           exit.
        B SECTION.
        ...


case2   A.
            IF NOT (condition)
                some code
                .

Which is faster to execute ?  Which is less bug-prone ?


> >While SECTION headers may provide a commentary, and is useful for
> >overlaying, the _only_ reason to PERFORM a SECTION rather than a single
> >paragraph is to allow for a GO TO.  There is no other reason.
> 
> You just gave TWO reasons for doing it, then said there is ONLY one...!

There are reasons for _having_ section headers (without _performing_
them).  ie the paragraphs may be performed but these are collected into
sections for the purpose of overlaying.  There is only one reason for
_PERFORMING_ a section.

> What about simply documenting code as functions and relating back to a
> structure chart? I wouldn't mind betting there are many more paragraphs in a
> program than are documented on the appropriate structure chart or Block
> diagram. 

Duh.  Do you put all the xxxx-exits on your structure diagram ?  What
about the 'several labels in a section' that you said may be necessary
?  It is most likely that you have 'many more' (twice) the number of
paragraphs than the structure diagram shows.

But of course using lightweight procedures leads to more paragraphs. 
This requires a more detailed structure diagram (or subsets thereof). 
With heavyweight procedures they can cram more into one structure box
without having to think about the design: one perform, one box.

> SECTIONing it gives a "Top Down" view towards isolating functions
> and can be tied back to the original structure. 

> For students this eliminates many logic errors 

But opens up many classes of coding errors.

> and shows them immediately where to look.

in a large heavyweight procedure where they have to spend time working
out the logic that is just one box on the structure.

Given more paragraphs and a more detailed structure diagram(s) when they
do find the correct 'box' it makes them look at the exact code.

> >PERFORMing single paragraphs (even if they are grouped in SECTIONs for,
> >say, overlaying) disallows GO TO.  This is the single most important
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> different view... I use whatever works.

I used to do that a couple of decades ago, then I found what worked
_better_.

It isn't a matter of religion, it is a matter of progress.  Using
Cobol-61 gave few opportunities to build effective programs without
these short-cuts.  SECTIONs were required for overlaying (on my machine)
and these were all batch programs anyway.

Using the features of Cobol-85 we no longer need to continue with the
past.  Interactive programs are not just batch programs with a few
ACCEPT/DISPLAY in them (except for CICS which are just batch, but are
only marginally interactive).

You're not an atheist, you are a Flat-Earther.     ;-)

> >By allowing the PERFORM of a SECTION (or the use of THRU) you allow GO
> >TOs and also allow the introduction of several classes of bugs which
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (I wouldn't do it, but the compiler certainly won't stop me...)

If you code a GO TO in a performed paragraph (no THRU) then it is either
back to its own label for repitition or it leads to loss of control (or
makes the program spagetti - same thing).

> >> I am sure you are being taught NOT to use GO TO, but the above is one of
>>> the cases where its use is really justified.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> BOLLOCKS! I have given an example where it IS justified...

Give us the justification then.  Claiming that it is faster to execute
than coding it a different way is just nonsense.  It is simple laziness
in not creating a proper structured solution.

It may be acceptable in an existing unweildy, badly constructed and
poorly maintained program where this already exists, but you are
advocating constructing _new_ programs and teaching students to do this.

Why are you advocating the equivalent of the biplane fighter ?

> Ah! It's a CODING overhead you're talking about...

Exactly.  It was coding you were teaching wasn't it ?

> If you NORMALLY write SECTIONed code you won't perceive it as an overhead.

Exactly. If you don't know better you will think this is normal.  As you
seem to have always written performed sections then you, yourself, don't
think of it as overhead.  You probably also think of tracking down 'loss
of control' bugs as 'normal'.

> You would only have a GO TO under exceptional circumstances and therefore it
> is NOT a regular overhead (coding or otherwise...). 

What are you saying here ?  You will only sometimes have the overhead of
section header and exit paragraph and sometimes not ?  You will
sometimes perform a paragraph (accidently or deliberately) ? and
sometimes check that a section is terminated by a following section (and
not just a paragraph) and sometimes you don't bother ?

If you don't do the overhead of making the checks and applying the
standards then you have potential bugs that are very difficult to find.  

These overheads are sufficiently onerous and bug infested that some
(such as Judson) have written programs that impose standards to ensure
the bugs are detected.

> It all comes down to the way you code.

Actually, given that you are teaching students, I think that they
deserve better than coding dragged up from the 60s.

> OK, that's fair. So instead of SECTIONs we have a plethora of paragraphs...

Exactly.  Now, instead of strict 'top down' Jackson from the 60s we can
start doing some 'bottom up' and create _re-usable_ code in paragraphs.

'Top-down', Jackson, structure diagrams, batch programming and
heavyweight performed sections leads (generally) to re-use of code
through copy-and-amend (leading to bugs of several types).  This is
because it is too expensive (in coding terms) to create a new section
just to have it available for other procedures to use (in any case it
wouldn't fit on the structure diagram).

Doing 'bottom up' creates small re-usable procedures that can be
assembled in various combinations to create solutions.

Both 'top down' and 'bottom up' are needed to create effective
applications where the middle becomes a way of tieing the functionality
into the required structure.

With interactive systems (which I have been doing for a couple of
decades) where the user is in control of the progress through the
program (rather than the structure diagram) the 'bottom up' approach is
far more appropriate.  This leads to lots of small, easy to debug,
re-usable procedures that can be assembled in different ways to do the
users bidding.

In this sense CICS is not an interactive system, it is a batch system
with screen input and output.

OO is a 'bottom up' process.

> It comes down to personal preference. I have expressed mine; you have
> expressed yours. 

No. It comes down to progress.  You prefer not to make any.

> I don't expect ever to change your mind, 

What, back to what I was doing in the 60s and 70s ?

> and on this occasion, you certainly haven't changed mine.

No, I didn't expect to change your mind, I could tell that I wouldn't,
it seems to have resisted for too long to be possible now.  I did,
however, expect to disavow other readers of the group of following your
rather archaic advise. 


> Try writing a routine to operate the valves in an on-line oil refinery (or
> any real time process) and see what happens when you close the valve 30
> milliseconds too late because you were executing code irrelevant to the
> established condition, just because you wouldn't code a GO TO...

Actually, there are many hard real-time programs that manage to do the
job without having to resort to GO TO. In some cases the language
disallows it.

But I suspect that you know little of process control because those
programs are interrupt driven and your description just doesn't fit.

Have you ever written an interrupt driven program ?  Or a hard real-time
one (such as operating values on an oil refinery) ? Or are you just
trying to bullshit us with 'science'.


> (While I have great respect for your technical prowess, Richard, you are
> talking to someone whose been around a long time and done most of
> it....don't try and blind me with science...) Your bullshit argument re
> Windows is nothing to do with a time critical process and you know it...Oh,

No, it was related to interactive systems as specified.

> and BTW, NOBODY writes time critical code in .INT the time to interpret it
> renders it non-viable.

Actually you are quite wrong yet again.  Oak, the precursor of Java, was
designed exactly as a real-time language executing in byte-code (which
is what .INT is).

> If you write computer programs without doing things "quite carefully enough"
> they will fail to work, IRRESPECTIVE of whether you use SECTIONs or not...

But with sections there are _far_more_ things that need to be done more
carefully, and thus are more opportunities to make it fail to work.

> While this is fun, it is kind of pointless and I've run out of time...

It is not pointless if we are talking about the quality of the next
generation of programmers.

> Compared to coding everything in-line with a heap of scope delimiters at the
> bottom of the code. This is a logical extension from using
> paragraphs...("Hey, I can avoid having paragraphs at all if I code it all in
> line..." And that neat END-PERFORM can go at the bottom of everything)

Well, exactly.  You compared an initial write in one bad coding
technique to a rewrite in another bad coding technique and then
concluded that it drastically cut the error rate.  However, simply
having them rewrite the code will reduce the error rate as it gives them
a second chance to design and implement.

Yes, rewriting any program a second time will result in fewer errors. 
This does not mean that the coding technique is the reason.

> (I did say that, but you obviously only see what you want to...)

Oh, I saw it, I just wanted to draw you out somewhat.   ;-)

> That's it from me... I really don't care how any of you write your code.

While I _do_ care how others, especially students, write their code (or
specifically write my code if they worked for me).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jeff Baynard <jeffbaynard@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T19:06:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6AAFB80.61E%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I agree.  95% of CPU time is spent in 5% of the code i.e. loops, searches,
etc.  The number of times something is executed is much more important than
the amount of time it takes to execute that code once.  Coding sections to
save execution time is ironically a waste of time.  Coding more or less
paragraphs to save execution time is a waste of time.  The execution time of
GO TO xxx-EXIT versus dropping through the code is basically irrelevant.
This isn't 1962, ok?

Jeff


in article 3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz, Richard Plinston at
riplin@Azonic.co.nz wrote on 2/10/01 5:58 AM:


> Give us the justification then.  Claiming that it is faster to execute
> than coding it a different way is just nonsense.  It is simple laziness
> in not creating a proper structured solution.
```

```
<jeffbaynard@home.com> writes:

>save execution time is ironically a waste of time.  Coding more or less
>paragraphs to save execution time is a waste of time.  The execution time of
>GO TO xxx-EXIT versus dropping through the code is basically irrelevant.
>This isn't 1962, ok?
>

This depends on how much data you expect your software to be able to 
process.  I work in an environment where EVERY microsecond saved is
important.  By the end of the night the amount of time saved could make 
the difference between making deliveries on schedule or paying 'express'
delivery rates.
There are also applications where timing is critical to determine the routing
of a document or some electronic switching device.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <3A863E82.865466DE@home.com>, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
writes:

>
>I'm not really bothered about the discussion about whether or not you use
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This has branched off the main thread.  Sorry for the side trip.
Yes, I have ventured thru many variations of coding styles in my
persnal development.   Relative to the applications that I currently 
support, there is a place for 'structured/perform thru' coding and there 
are others where use of top-down/goto is better.
Based on the application, different coding styles are used.
I have recently grown into the use of the COBOL85/9x syntax extensions,
but when supporting code developed under COBOL68 or COBOL74 rules
you have to play within the guidelines that are available.
My side trip was prompted by an earlier post related to the use of 
SECTIONs and letting a 'condition' fall thru the remainder of the 
section code hitting all remaining tests eating away at processor cycles
when this 'condition' should have branched out and moved on to 
another record or section of code.
I operate under a mandate of efficiency in code/memory/time/processor
utilization.  Even though the concept of the day is 'disk/memory is cheap',
it does not necessarily mean that the user is going to be able/willing to 
run out and add more memory/disk just to run my application.  There
is enough bloat to software in the industry.
```

```
no SECTION is not where you look for performance improvement other than that
of interest to academics and Real Dweebs.

consultant to look at the system; and when I say "outside" I mean someone
who has never seen the code before and can truly add a "new set of eyes" to
the problem; and "system," someone to whom you will need to define the
business rules which the application must service.
```

```
In article <96642s$upj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> writes:

>Do you have statistics which allow you to determine the
>average processor and elapsed time per transaction? - number of files
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It's been a long time since getting that deep into the timing of my programs.
I now work mostly from experience.  The experience of knowing that a 
'select' decision has to be returned to a device within 7 microseconds 
of issuing a 'read'  and seeing the difference when using fall thru/goto
coding versus perform loops that push/pop entries onto stacks for tracking
where the program needs to go.
The used to be a progam we ran (FLAME) that monitored performance
based on I/O wait, memory paging, and CPU cycle utilization.  Out of 
those analysis tools came guidelines for optimizing program performance.
Further, we investigated the use of buffering to cause the system to
'read-ahead'
and use logic record access over physical record access.
Such things are not considered in the PC environment as in 'most' cases
you don't have the volume of data to crunch that is encountered on the PC.

In todays world, with PCs being able to directly access mainframe files,
things are likely to start changing.  Memory consumption, physical media
access, storage methods, storage allocation all come into play when designing
something that is to hammer thru millions of records in a finite period of time

and still produce reliable, usable results.


ensure that a 'select' is
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T07:57:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A87F9BE.80EC9B1F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<96642s$upj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20010211133059.08348.00002257@nso-da.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:

> It's been a long time since getting that deep into the timing of my programs.
> I now work mostly from experience.  The experience of knowing that a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> where the program needs to go.

Every time your OS and/or compiler gets updated, you should re-do these tests.
Different optimization routines can make significant changes to your results.

And there is one thing I have learned on this forum.  Some optimizers will look for
a GO TO anywhere in the code.  If one is there, they don't optimize any of the
performs, regardless of whether you only use GO TO to go to the abort routine.
Knowing this, I now do the illogical PERFORM 9999-ABORT.   Keep this in mind while
doing the tests.

Of course, the best thing for loop efficiency is to decide what to leave out of the
loop!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T14:01:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A87ECA6.7EC3D922@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<B6AAFB80.61E%jeffbaynard@home.com> <20010210233125.21405.00002465@nso-mj.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> This depends on how much data you expect your software to be able to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> delivery rates.

It is far more likely that minutes or hours could be saved in optimising
disk access than seconds would be saved by using sections or paragraphs.

Of course, given an existing lump of a program that is badly constructed
and uneconomical to rewrite then it may well be useful to shave a few
microseconds without worrying about its elegance.

However, this does not imply that one design strategy is inherently
faster to execute than another.

> There are also applications where timing is critical to determine the routing
> of a document or some electronic switching device.

And how many of these are in Cobol ? How many of them 'perform
sections'?, how many use goto in whatever language they are written in ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T13:24:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Example where using SECTION and GO TO was faster
than paragraph.

Initial code was in the form:

para.
    *> setup a table of 32 conditions
    perform varying xi2 from 1 by 1 until xi2 > 32
        if condition (xi2)
            *> body
        end-if
    end-perform
    *> save results

Faster code was in the form:

sect section.
    *> setup a table of 32 conditions
    move 0 to xi2.
head-of-loop.
    add 1 to xi2
    if xi2 > 32
        go to end-of-loop
    end-if
    if condition (xi2)
        *> body
    end-if
    go to head-of-loop.
end-of-loop.
    *> save results

Notice that the only difference is the replacement of the
PERFORM VARYING with the equivalent lower-level
code. After this change, the program ran 30% faster.
The improvement occurred because the lower-level
loop control was slightly faster than the PERFORM.
The magnitude of the improvement occurred because
the body was executed less than 10% of the time. Loop
overhead, in this loop, was an important factor in overall
performance of the program. More than 99% of CPU,
for the program, was consumed by the loop.

While this example is not the same as the one on which
you commented, it is an example where using SECTION
and GO TO is faster than using paragraphs only.

Of course, there is a paragraph-only solution that is
faster -- un-roll the loop to create one really big
paragraph! If you would choose this solution to avoid
SECTION, then we can debate the meaning of
"'proper' structured solution." ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T14:36:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<966t3j$d1k$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
In my earlier note about avoiding SECTIONS, I did mention that I think this
is advisable ESPECIALLY when the new EXIT PARAGRAPH  statement is available.
I think that almost everyone will agree that there is a "performance
advantage" in "heavily used" loops in being able to "jump out" of it - when
the appropriate occasion arises.  It is "possible" to do that today with
various constructs (including - but not limited to - SECTIONS).  However, I
do see this construct (already available as an extension with some
compilers) as one of the more "significant" *little* enhancements in the
draft Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T23:09:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Another example where using SECTION and GO TO is
faster than paragraph.

Code in the form:

sect section.
    do something.
para-1.
    evaluate a-variable
        when my-special-value
            do something more
            go to para-1
        several when phrases
            doing other things
    end-evaluate.

It is possible to re-write this by using perform and a flag;
but it should be clear that adding the flag and loop
overhead will slow things down.

I must admit that this is not a "'proper' structured solution"
as it is; but, given the choice between this and a slower
solution with flags, I would prefer this. I find, repeatedly,
that strict adherence to the precepts of structured
methodology prevent some fast, simple, even elegant
solutions; such as the above.

Incidently, code in the above form was supplied by Micro
Focus, in the sample preprocessor, in the demo\preproc
sub directory for the MF WB 3.2 system. It is very likely
it was supplied for other systems, as well.
------------------
Rick Smith

Rick Smith wrote in message <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net>...
>Example where using SECTION and GO TO was faster
>than paragraph.
[...]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T23:34:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
What am I missing.  How does making "sect section" a section instead of a
paragraph change ANYTHING in this program, i.e. wouldn't

 para-0.
     do something.
 para-1.
     evaluate a-variable
         when my-special-value
             do something more
             go to para-1
         several when phrases
             doing other things
     end-evaluate.

Do EXACTLY the same thing?  If your problem is that somewhere else in your
code you had a
   PERFORM SECT
statement, then this could "easily" (without introducing any other changes)
become
  Perform Para-0 thru Para-1

This *still* isn't "structured" and I would MUCH prefer a variety of other
structures for this - but I just don't see how this example shows any
advantage of SECTIONs over PARAGRAPHs - even though it does show a "common"
use of GO TO.

P.S.  The way that I would code this in Micro Focus COBOL would be:

Change any existing PERFORM SECT to Perform New-Para, then change the
existing code to:

 New-para.
     do something
     Set Para-End to False
     Perform para-1
         Until Para-End
             .
 para-1.
     evaluate a-variable
         when my-special-value
             do something more
             Exit Paragraph
         several when phrases
             doing other things

     end-evaluate
     Set Para-End to True
           .

I would need some pretty  clear evidence that this was "significantly"
slower - before I believed it.  (There is another structure available in
Micro Focus - but *not* included in the draft Standard that might also be
considered.  It uses a PERFORM forever construct - and you could use the
EXIT PERFORM statement to get out of the loop - with no flag at all.
However, I find that not very "self-documenting" - and wouldn't use it
myself)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T03:47:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The code and speed estimate is based on perform section
versus a "proper" structured solution using perform paragraph.

As for using perform ... thru, yuck, never!

Your proposed solution introduces overhead as I suggested
such a solution must; therefore it will be slower. Whether there
is and what constitutes "significantly slower" is subjective and
not wholly relevant to the thread.

The primary consideration is whether it is justified to have
GO TO. (You could not have known this because of "snipping"
of the thread.) I provided justification (well ... opinion) elsewhere
that using a flag to hide a GO TO is a big lie that makes the
code unclear or mistakable. (You could not have known that
because your response, in this part of the thread was posted
before I provided the justification, in another part.)

---- This is the snipped part of the thread ----
>> >> I am sure you are being taught NOT to use GO TO, but the above is one
of
>>>> the cases where its use is really justified.
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>in not creating a proper structured solution.
---- Through here ----

If I have given proper consideration and not misunderstood,
too much, and referencing three of my other posts to this
thread, then:

1. If an implementor treats perform of section and perform of
    paragraph, equally, the perform of a section will take no
    longer than the perform of a paragraph, where the section
    and the paragpraph contain the same instructions.

2. Using sections and GO TO paragraph, within the section,
    provides opportunities for speed improvements that are
    not available with paragraphs only.

3. Using sections and GO TO paragraph, within the section,
    provides opportunities for a programmer to communicate
    honestly to a maintainer.

Therefore, programmers should prefer sections as the
building block for code, using paragraph labels and GO TO
only when necessary.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I had promised myself not to add any further fuel to this fire (especially
as I started it <G>) but on reading Rick's post below I was blown away...

It states exactly what my original post (and the single response I made to
Richard's counter) was trying to say, but Rick has expressed it more
eloquently and succinctly than I did.

I suggested that there could be a case for using GO TO  (strictly within a
SECTION)where TIME CRITICAL  processes are involved;Rick has shown that
there may well be other occasions where it is justified also.

Why does such an innocuous observation bring down such an outpouring of
wrath?

No one is desecrating Holy Writ here, just suggesting that sometimes there
may be exceptions to some rules. It doesn't invalidate the general
applicability of the rule, and I'm sure Rick doesn't write spaghetti code
any more than I do (that's "never").

On the basis of what's been written here, I don't think either Rick or
myself have advocated persistent or continual use of GO TOs  (except  under
some very carefully defined circumstances which are perceived to be "rare")
or the overturning of Structured Programming (whatever you perceive that to
be) and no-one is trying to tell you how to code; write it how you like.

I have been accused of being stuck in the past, trying to turn the clock
back, giving archaic advice, and generally suffered the contumely of my
peers (Awww...poor flower...<G>)

And yet I am using COBOL in cutting edge applications. Self modifying
heuristic code, platform independent components (ActiveX/COM) to run in HTML
and Java Servlets and VB and C++ applications, Windows applications (GUI
event driven programming), OO COBOL.

Not bad for an anachronistic old codger.... Just think what I might have
done if I hadn't held myself back by using Sectioned code...<G>

Thanks for the succinct summation, Rick. I for one appreciate and endorse
it. (especially the last paragraph <G>)

Pete.
```

```
Pleas explain why you think that, of the two following pieces of code,
one would be faster than the other and which would this be:

A)  this SECTION.
       IF ( X >= Y )
           GO TO this-exit
       END-IF
       rest of code
       .
    this-exit.
       EXIT.
    

B)  this.
       IF ( X < Y )
           rest of code
       END-IF
       .
```

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> If I have given proper consideration and not misunderstood,
> too much, and referencing three of my other posts to this
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> only when necessary.

If those 3 elements were the only 3 elements we care about, you are justified
with your conclusion.  (although I don't agree with #3 - communicating honestly
with the programmer is facilitated by avoiding GO TO - of course, you may be
referring to the obfuscating effect possible with switches - which is not at all
necessary)

But you left out some considerations:

4.  Using sections and/or perform thru logic increases the opportunity for
programmer error.  I HAVE seen such errors.  Sometimes they can be subtle and
missed during unit testing.

5.  Using GO TO commands can adversely effect optimization compilers.  Where
possible, allow the optimizer to achieve speed improvements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T14:43:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<969em9$2ubo$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net>...
[...]
>But you left out some considerations:
>
>4.  Using sections and/or perform thru logic increases the opportunity for
>programmer error.  I HAVE seen such errors.  Sometimes they can be subtle
and
>missed during unit testing.
>
>5.  Using GO TO commands can adversely effect optimization compilers.
Where
>possible, allow the optimizer to achieve speed improvements.

I need to address these items, separately. Item 4 will take
some time to answer, so I will answer only item 5 at this time.

The primary effect on optimization, of GO TO, is "in-lining";
that is inserting instructions in-line, or replacing a PERFORM
with the instructions performed to eliminate call / return
overhead.

When a program consists entirely of paragraphs, a single
GO TO statement must prevent in-line optimization because
it is not possible for the compiler to determine flow of control.

When a program consists of sections, a single GO TO
to a paragraph name within a section, need not prevent
in-line optimization. This is because the compiler can
determine flow of control. Therefore, the perform of a section
containing a GO TO with a destination within the section may
be "in-lined". A GO TO outside the section must inhibit
in-line optimization.

One of the examples I provided showed a 30% increase
in performance. The compiler directive OPTSPEED was
used both before and after the change. However, I believe
this compiler did not have in-line optimization.

Sometimes the programmer can do better than the compiler
when optimization is required. We do this often when we
select a faster algorithm. We sometimes make an algorithm
faster with a well-placed GO TO.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T20:00:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net>...
[...]
>But you left out some considerations:
>
>4.  Using sections and/or perform thru logic increases the opportunity for
>programmer error.  I HAVE seen such errors.  Sometimes they can be subtle
and
>missed during unit testing.
>
>5.  Using GO TO commands can adversely effect optimization compilers.
Where
>possible, allow the optimizer to achieve speed improvements.

Having addressed item 5, separately, I will now respond
to item 4.

Using only paragraphs increases the opportunity for
programmer error. The programmer may insert a section
name which, when performed, will cause the program to
misbehave. ;-)

This is the peanut butter - chocolate analogy. By extension,
those who like paragraphs will see sections as distasteful.
Those who like sections will see paragraphs as distasteful.
Unlike peanut butter and chocolate, paragraphs and sections
should not be combined.

It is probably true that adding a section to a program built
with paragraphs will show up as an error earlier.

The bottom-line is that, whatever style is selected, it is
still incumbent upon the programmer to ensure the program
meets the requirements of that style. This does not preclude
the use of tools to do style checking.

I did not give this a great deal of consideration earlier
because I recognized there was an essential truth that
both styles are widely and successfully used. I needed only
to confirm that both, when used consistently, could prevent
code from "falling through". I found this to be true.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
donald tees wrote in message <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net>...
>
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >programmer error.  I HAVE seen such errors.  Sometimes they can be
subtle
>> and
>> >missed during unit testing.
[...]
>> Using only paragraphs increases the opportunity for
>> programmer error. The programmer may insert a section
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>;<)

Total contradiction? I think not!

For example, I create a copybook with a procedure that
is in a section and use that in a program that has only
sections. The routine works well.

I decide to use the same routine in a program that
has only paragraphs; but I forget to replace SECTION
by nothing. The COPY statement is placed immediately
before an abort routine. Everytime I perform the new
routine, the program aborts with no message.

Had the second program not used paragraphs, the
error would not have occurred. Therefore, the use of
paragraphs increased the opportunity for programmer
error!
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T23:36:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
I *totally* agree that "mixing" programs (or parts of programs) with
PARAGRAPHS and with SECTIONS is *bound* to lead to problems.  I fully
support using one or the other as a "shop standard" (even in a "shop of one"
<G>).  However, I have yet to see why using all paragraphs would EVER be any
more error prone than using ALL sections.  As stated elsewhere, all of this
"assumes" that you avoid THRU (of either paragraphs or sections) which I
feel *does* lead to errors.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T23:47:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96ahrk$38k6$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net> <96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I agree, Bill.  Over the 25 years I've been programming, I've dealt with
quite a few programs with paragraphs only and quite a few with sections
only.  I really have no preference of one over the other, but in my
experience, mixing the 2 is probably the worst thing you can do from a
maintenance point of view.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T01:48:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96alk2$18uq$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net> <96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I never said, previously, that one style was more error
prone than the other. I do believe that either can lead
to errors under some conditions. Errors are more likely
to occur when a programmer using one style, most of
the time, modifys a program using the other style.

I do not have any hard numbers, so I will make up some
numbers to illustrate my point.

Assume group A works mostly with paragraphs, group B
works mostly with sections, and group C works equally
with both. Each works with programs 1 - 99 (paragraphs)
and 101 - 199 (sections).

I think we might see numbers such as the following:

Paragraph/Section naming errors per 1000 procedures.

      P    S
A   0    20
B   5     0
C   1     1

That is, A is about 4 times more likely to omit SECTION,
when SECTION is required, than B is likely to include
SECTION, when it should not be included. While C is likely
to error on both; but do so less frequently.

I think that an error of omitting SECTION, when it is required,
will, more likely, make it through testing and into production.

Remember, I made up these numbers for illustration only;
but I would expect the relationship to hold; were it actually
tested.
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>...
>I *totally* agree that "mixing" programs (or parts of programs) with
>PARAGRAPHS and with SECTIONS is *bound* to lead to problems.  I fully
>support using one or the other as a "shop standard" (even in a "shop of
one"
><G>).  However, I have yet to see why using all paragraphs would EVER be
any
>more error prone than using ALL sections.  As stated elsewhere, all of this
>"assumes" that you avoid THRU (of either paragraphs or sections) which I
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >> >4.  Using sections and/or perform thru logic increases the
opportunity
>> >for
>> >> >programmer error.  I HAVE seen such errors.  Sometimes they can be
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Rick Smith
```

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
