- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net> <96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <96alk2$18uq$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> I never said, previously, that one style was more error
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the time, modifys a program using the other style.

Quite correct.  Keeping a single style helps because of the
familiarity.  However, I suspect some have been doing this since the
60s   ;-)
 
 
> Paragraph/Section naming errors per 1000 procedures.
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> to error on both; but do so less frequently.

There is one essential difference - I can run grep or editor searches to
show that there are no 'section' and thus eliminate that error easily
(and also for GO TO).  In order to show that the section has been used
correctly it is necessary to process the source is a far more
sophisticated way, and this probably requires certain standards to be
followed to cater for the checker (see Judson).
 
> I think that an error of omitting SECTION, when it is required,
> will, more likely, make it through testing and into production.

Certainly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T16:22:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96cc2l$esl$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net> <96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <96alk2$18uq$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8A3452.82286D8E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
OK - now for the "really important" <G> questions:

1) Has *anyone* ever been convinced to change from using SECTIONS to
PARAGRAPHS or vice versa by this or any other "electronic" or "in person"
discussion?  (As opposed to changes based on "thou shalt" rules from a
manager or shop standard)

2) Are any of the readers of this NG who are new (or relatively new) to
COBOL really interested in this "prolonged" discussion?  Is there some
chance that you may adopt one style or another based on the "input"
(evidence?) provided here.

  ***

If people are not convinced to either change or adopt a "different" way of
programming based on this (or similar) discussions - I suspect that this
entire thread is just about as useful as "we all" thought it would be when
it first started.

P.S.  This applies "equally" to discussions of editors, ENTRY statements,
88-levels, etc.  I don't mind such discussions; I usually monitor them; and
often contribute to them.  HOWEVER, I do want to make certain that all
"participants" are aware of their "usual" impact on the rest of the world -
i.e. NONE <G>.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T16:40:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96cd77$g9nk$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net> <96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <96alk2$18uq$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8A3452.82286D8E@Azonic.co.nz> <96cc2l$esl$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Like I said, I really have no preferences of one over the other, but one of
the first things I do before modifying any program is to find all "SECTION."
since adding a paragraph to a SECTIONed program can be much more critical
than adding one to a PARAGRAPHed one.  As you mentioned, I usually try to
follow the conventions of the existing program or shop standards.
```

```
Good points, Bill.   I've saved most of these messages to try to condense
them.
Best guess on result; local rules.

As an aside: Sections for "overlay" had a purpose. It seems to me that this
purpose has disappeared because of cost factors.

I'm not sure that limited to control of limited memory by the source program
is not a useful thing to have or might be again.  Changes in hardware design
and thus the operating software might make the original intention disappear
completely.  However, I see we are trying to run on the net with phones and
wonder just how stable our current ideas of needs are.

Warren Simmons
```

- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net> <96ah1s$gon$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <96alk2$18uq$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8A3452.82286D8E@Azonic.co.nz> <96cc2l$esl$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <mYji6.22873$JN5.183021@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com> <3A8A9790.30330018@brazee.net>`

```
Working within the "guidlines" set out by my company, If I can see how a
change in style will make my life easier, either in maintaining or in
creating code, I will adopt the practice.  If the suggestion is outside of
our "guidlines", I try to get those changed to the new procedure.  As a side
note, this is my only programming position (which I started the same date I
took my first COBOL course in college) and the news group is in fact my
tutor.  It has been amazing what college doesn't include in their courses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T19:09:03-05:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T08:01:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A894C2D.4D7CDCD9@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net> <96a6m2$n1$1@news.igs.net> <96ac6l$via$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> Had the second program not used paragraphs, the
> error would not have occurred. Therefore, the use of
> paragraphs increased the opportunity for programmer
> error!

Having a mixed environment increased the opportunity for programmer error.

But sections are only meaningful anymore when it includes paragraphs.
Paragraphs do not need sections though.   If we get rid of GOTO EXIT commands,
everything can be structured.

That said, I work in an environment where we use IDMS COPY statements which
include sections.   These are at the end of the program, so their only effect
on a paragraph based environment is that we get a warning message if we don't
stick a dummy section at the start of the program.

Back in the days that CoBOL sorts required sections, I would put a dummy
section at the end of the sort procedures:
REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM SECTION.
DUMMY-PARAGRAPH.
     EXIT.

This was a safeguard to make it harder for a programmer to stick in a
performed paragraph at the end of the program - and have it executed by
dropping through.

Mixing paragraphs and sections increases programmer errors, as does performing
THRU, or GO TO above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T07:45:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A89487A.3A597F6D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A8437FD.2A6AD4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <960u5j$foq$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A851ED8.34A7E369@Azonic.co.nz> <966lki$2pgu$1@news.hitter.net> <967o0h$106c$1@news.hitter.net> <967sk6$q7o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <968891$1hul$1@news.hitter.net> <3A880494.1E3C3AB4@brazee.net> <96a164$ik7$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Not quite.   A program designed around sections (in the era when memory
segmentation is not a concern) will have paragraphs.   A program designed
around paragraphs doesn't need sections.  Either way can be confused with copy
members.

The errors occur when we mix both styles.

Now if your sections are an other way of doing a PERFORM THRU EXIT with one
paragraph followed by an exit paragraph, then the two styles are equally error
prone.   If you like this style (I don't), you may find that EXIT PARAGRAPH
will allow you to eliminate drop through code altogether.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A89487A.3A597F6D@brazee.net>...
>Not quite.   A program designed around sections (in the era when memory
>segmentation is not a concern) will have paragraphs.   A program designed
>around paragraphs doesn't need sections.  Either way can be confused with
copy
>members.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>paragraph followed by an exit paragraph, then the two styles are equally
error
>prone.   If you like this style (I don't), you may find that EXIT PARAGRAPH
>will allow you to eliminate drop through code altogether.

Hmmm! Pronouns in second person singular!
Now it's personal. :-)

My sections are not another way of doing PERFORM
THRU EXIT!

I have had EXIT PARAGRAPH, EXIT SECTION, EXIT
PERFORM, and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE for nearly
ten years and have used them when appropriate!
(Since Micro Focus COBOL/2 v2.5)

My code with sections does not "drop through!"

Sections do not cause "drop through," "fall through,"
"trickle," or any other error. Only programmers can
create that problem!

Well over 99% of my procedures consist of a section
header followed by one unnamed paragraph. While this
may not conform to COBOL 85 or 74, it does conform
to the draft COBOL standard! I have been using unnamed
paragraphs in sections for more than 16 years.
(Since Micro Focus Professional COBOL v1.1.)

In the procedure division, I could
replace == section.== by ==.==
    ==exit section== by ==exit paragraph==
    ==go to== by ==mary==
and all, except 2 or 3 programs, would compile and run
with no errors or other anomalies! Those 2 or 3 programs
use about 4 or 5 GO TO statements for optimization.

I do not design around sections or paragraphs, I use
procedures! For code I post to the newsgroup, I use
paragraphs when section is not required; otherwise I use
sections.

If it is still available on your server, look at the code I posted
in "Re: Using MOVE to Truncate is Counter-Intuitive ...".
Add the word "section" to each paragraph-name and you
will have a clearer image of how code in sections need not
be different than code in paragraphs.

I use sections because it gives me an option not available
with paragraphs -- the occasional use of GO TO when
needed.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

- **From:** pknudsen@gw.total-web.net (Paul Knudsen)
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T01:54:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a947bb3.21694373@news.gw.total-web.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
You overlooked the best reason off all to use sections, at least in
the MVS world.

The IBM compiler puts performed procedures inline by default.  I
believe it will do this four or more times before deciding to use a
branch.

But if you code a section with a priority-number, that section is not
placed inline when it is performed.  Hence, a routine called only
once, or only on error would not be inline, thus conserving memory, or
if you prefer, "locality of reference".

<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>

>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T20:01:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96pund$bn5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3a947bb3.21694373@news.gw.total-web.net>`

```
FYI - the use of "priority numbers" is an OBSOLETE feature of the '85
Standard.  (If compiling under IBM MVS, OS/390 or z/OS - use the
FLAGSTD(H,O) compiler option to see what IBM tells you about its use).

This feature is already "removed" from the draft Standard (along with all
the remaining parts of "segmentation"). Therefore, if we are talking about
making something ARCHAIC in this draft Standard, reference to "priority
numbers" (or anything else related to ANSI/ISO segmentation) is
"irrelevant".
```

```
works for me.

insert performed code inline x times and there's nothing we can do
about it?

<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Standard.  (If compiling under IBM MVS, OS/390 or z/OS - use the
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T18:43:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96seit$88m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3a947bb3.21694373@news.gw.total-web.net> <96pund$bn5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3a92b2bf.1011384@news.gw.total-web.net>`

```
"inserting performed code" is NOT something that relates to the ANSI or ISO
Standard.  What you are seeing is a "result" of the IBM "OPT" compiler
option.  Today, you could get rid of your segment numbers and use the NOOPT
compiler option (available with VS COBOL II and later).  No code will be
"inserted" inline.

OK - now for my "confusion"
  What do you think is the "problem" with IBM "inserting" performed code
"inline" (so it is MORE local to other procedural logic)?  You are aware (I
assume) that if the IBM "optimizer" can detect (at compile-time) that one or
more of the PERFORMS cannot "logically" ever be reached - it is NOT copied
in line?

In some operating systems, I can see "load module size" as an issue.
However, in IBM's OS/390 and z/OS, I just don't understand the "problem"
with copying code into multiple places in the object module.

If there were a "good" reason for having IBM do other optimization but NOT
do this, I think that you should submit this to IBM as a SHARE requirement
or via their support "REQUEST" channel.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** pknudsen@charter.net (Paul Knudsen)
- **Date:** 2001-02-24T19:19:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a9806d1.3002051@news.gw.total-web.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3a947bb3.21694373@news.gw.total-web.net> <96pund$bn5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3a92b2bf.1011384@news.gw.total-web.net> <96seit$88m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 19 Feb 2001 18:43:55 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"inserting performed code" is NOT something that relates to the ANSI or ISO
>Standard.  What you are seeing is a "result" of the IBM "OPT" compiler
>option.  Today, you could get rid of your segment numbers and use the NOOPT
>compiler option (available with VS COBOL II and later).  No code will be
>"inserted" inline.
Then why did the committee eliminate priority numbers?  

No, using NOPT won't do because most performed should be placed
inline.  But not all.  And it should be up to the programmer what is
and what is not.

>OK - now for my "confusion"
>  What do you think is the "problem" with IBM "inserting" performed code
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>in line?
The "problem" is a seldom-entered routine should not be in the middle
of the code.  Of course unreachable code should not be there either.

>In some operating systems, I can see "load module size" as an issue.
>However, in IBM's OS/390 and z/OS, I just don't understand the "problem"
>with copying code into multiple places in the object module.
Then why did IBM implement the option to override this in COBOL II?
They must have thought it was a problem.

>If there were a "good" reason for having IBM do other optimization but NOT
>do this, I think that you should submit this to IBM as a SHARE requirement
>or via their support "REQUEST" channel.
I think efficiency is a good enough reason.  Don't you think code
should be efficient?  Even if the gain is quite small?  And especially
if it can be done with little effort--which it can--provided the
programmer uses Sections.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-25T20:22:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97cehn$l9q$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83247D.6A73202C@brazee.net> <95vipt$d9t$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3a947bb3.21694373@news.gw.total-web.net> <96pund$bn5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3a92b2bf.1011384@news.gw.total-web.net> <96seit$88m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3a9806d1.3002051@news.gw.total-web.net>`

```
I guess that I am still missing something.  I just do NOT believe that
putting "infrequently used" PERFORMed code "inline" adds any performance
degradation.  If you have some evidence of this, can you share this (and
have you shared it with IBM)?

Personally, I am STRONGLY opposed to COBOL programmers controlling compiler
optimizations.  Having worked with and for both PC and mainframe compiler
vendors, I just do NOT think that this is a "good" thing to be under
programmer control.  If you have a specific need, that your vendor is not
meeting, then let them know about it - otherwise, I think that COBOL
programmers should "stick" to the COBOL code level and *not* fiddle around
with object code algorithms.  This doesn't mean that you can't or shouldn't
"test" different COBOL algorithms for "performance" implications - but I do
NOT think that code generation is a good place for COBOL programmers to
work.

P.S.  If you have a specific set of "code" that is INFREQUENTLY used - and
that you do not want "copied inline" - have you tried making it a nested
program?  The overhead of a nested program (unlike an external program) is
minimal - and it certainly is NEVER "copied" inline.  I wouldn't
(personally) use it for a "heavily" used set of code - but I believe the
entire "thrust" of this was to not "copy inline" infrequently used code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

```
> >If there were a "good" reason for having IBM do other optimization but NOT

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

I can see a use of being able to define what type of efficiency is desired.   For
instance, in some environments size vs speed is a trade off.   Sometimes an
analyst should be able to choose.   Certainly optimizing a SQL query in a client
server environment is dependent upon many variables.
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T00:39:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R_Gg6.39$uw5.7868@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The best pointer to their use is - wait for it now - don't.

Sections can be thought of as a super-set of paragraphs. For example,
instead of PERFORM AAA THRU ZZZ you could PERFORM XXX (a SECTION
containing paragraphs AAA thru ZZZ).

In addition to the older sort syntax requiring section names, there
was a technique allowing the operating system to page sections in and
out of memory.

Anyway, they've mostly outlived their usefulness.
```

```
"Jeff Baynard" <jeffbaynard@home.com> wrote in message
news:B6AAF756.61D%jeffbaynard@home.com...
> It's good to see that at least some people realize that things change over
> time.  What we did 10-20 years ago may be outdated today, and we have to
> realize this.  Storage constraints for instructions is basically
irrelevant
> these days.
>
> Jeff
>
I was rather amused at a conversion I did recently.  The old system was
Isam, the new is a sequential file and a list box.  The new version contains
the old system as a DLL. I call the old system subroutines to convert the
files on their first access after install.  It is only an extra meg or so of
memory ... I learnt this language on a 16k H-200.  A mainframe. We had sixty
megabytes of storage on three removable disk drives. This is like coding on
a machine provided by God.  There are no restraints, only in our
imaginations.

In between then and now, I have coded in eight or nine assemblers, nine or
ten variations of basic, six or seven Cobols, two Pascals, one PL1, two
Fortrans, one C, one C++, one Java, and a whole bunch of silly little
languages that I have forgotten.  Of all that, the Cobol code is the only
stuff still running, and *all* of that still works, right back to the days
of the stuff I wrote for the H-200.  That says a lot for the language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T22:05:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96a8gc$qt2$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <960pse$rmq$1@news.igs.net> <B6AAF756.61D%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A87F854.DFA5D2EA@brazee.net>`

```
Segmentation was made obsolete in the 85 standard
and was removed from the draft COBOL standard. While
there may be support in some older compilers, the
direction should be to treat segment-numbers and the
SEGMENT-LIMIT clause as documentary.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
Who knows. It probably doesn't make any difference on today's machines.

As an aside, I retract my statement that "Storage constraints for
instructions is basically irrelevant these days."  Today, my project lead
found out one of our GUIs hit the maximum size of all the scripts in any one
object (event scripts, user events, functions, etc.) in PowerBuilder.  212
kilobytes to be exact.  Strange, this.  We can make changes to the
descendants, just not the ancestor.  It causes WinNT to GPF when saving.

So I guess you never know.  If we had been a little more conservative and
resourceful in that object, it may have payed off now...

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

- **From:** "Raymond Schelp" <rschelp@linkline.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T18:06:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t8ha8ick9cdh5d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I used to exclusively code programs using the "section". It cuts down on the
need for "thru" being coded everywhere and thus a bit more readable.

The rule to remember that execution of a performed section continues until
another section is encountered.  I did NOT use "thru" with sections.

My code would look something like:

1000-Mainline section.
1000-Main.
        Perform 2000-Init
        Perform 3000-Loop
        Perform 4000-End
1000-exit. exit

2000-Init section.
2000-Initialize.
         blah blah
2000-exit. exit.

3000-Loop section.
3000-Looping.
        blah blah
3000-exit. exit.

etc etc etc

If I'd coded additional paragraphs within the 2000- or 3000-  sections ...
then execution would have continued thru to the last paragraph of each
section.

news:95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I was wondering if you are using sections in the procedure division?
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.deja.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SECTION

- **From:** "charles" <fjlk@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-14T01:25:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n8li6.121718$P5.2688487@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`
- **References:** `<95v6vk$i9u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <t8ha8ick9cdh5d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Thanks for all of the input on the use of section in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
I guess I could just comment that line out and see what happens.  I don't
see it being used in a cross reference.  The program works even though it
uses
GO TO statements.

Where I work we have programs from one extreme to the other.  Some programs
use the GO TO statement and a top-down approach and some use almost no GO TO
statements.  We do not consider there to be anything wrong with using a GO
TO statement.

How I approach a program almost always depends on the complexity of the
task.  The more complex the task the more structured the program gets.  When
I was in school we usually had one in-file and one out-file.  At that time a
structured program was easy, however; I found that where I work they have
many programs with many GO TO statements.  When I first started working I
couldn't even understand what they were doing.  If you want to confuse a
structured programmer, just throw in some GO TO statements.  It was like
where did all these GO TO statements come from?

I got over it after a while and learned to live with it. It is not wrong to
use a GO TO statement.  A person can get just as carried away with too many
sub paragraphs.  You see the same thing in object oriented programming where
they attempt to use the maximum number of functions to make a simple
greeting like "Hello World"!

the
```

<a id="t-d20830537c"></a>
## Thread: Subprograms and the CALL statement... — 4 msgs

### Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T00:55:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<meHg6.93$uw5.11129@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A82A451.4F8FCF0@mutualwheel.com>`

```

"TFo" <todd@mutualwheel.com> wrote in message
news:3A82A451.4F8FCF0@mutualwheel.com...
> I haven't tested this. I've only read my school book. It states that
all
> parameters can be at any level, but  must be elementary items. I
think I'll
> give it a try tonight, and see if the book is right or wrong.

Your book is wrong. Passed parameters in the calling program may be at
any level; in the called subroutine they must be 01 level. There is no
restriction on group/elementary in either place.

In CALL BY REFERENCE (the most common), when passing to a subroutine,
what is actually passed is the address of the first byte of the passed
parameter. Whether the (logically) passed item is a single byte,
10,000 bytes, elementary item, group item, or a sack of nuts, is
irrelevant; The compiler doesn't care - the linker doesn't care. Only
the address of the beginning byte of the logical parameter is
furnished to the called program. What the called program does with
that address is up to it.

Here's one that does a body good:

05  MYBYTE  PIC X.
...
CALL 'MYSUB' USING MYBYTE.


(MYSUB)

LINKAGE SECTION.

01  MYBYTE  PIC X(10000).

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MYBYTE.

MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO MYBYTE.
GOBACK.

This is an exaggeration, but much sweat is sometimes expended in
tracing bizarre symptoms to a mis-match in calling/called parameters.
But note that the above silliness IS possible.
```

#### ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T00:31:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9602td$oqs$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A82A451.4F8FCF0@mutualwheel.com> <meHg6.93$uw5.11129@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Actually ...
  There is an obscure (and not enforced by many compilers) rule that says
that a passed parameter *must* be defined as an 01-level, a 77-level, or an
elementary item.  "intermediate" level group items may NOT be passed in an
ANSI'85 conforming compiler (unless they have an extension - which most
compilers do).  This "rule" will be removed in the next Standard.

For example, given:

 01 Group-1.
     05  Group-2.
          10 Elem1  Pic X.
          10 Elem2  Pic X.
     05 Elem3   Pic X.

ANSI rules allow you to pass Group-1, Elem1, Elem2, and Elem3 but *not*
Group-2.

(The historical reasons have to do with slack bytes and synchronousation -
but most compilers have not enforced this rule for YEARS)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T01:01:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_p0h6.3565$ZJ5.297487@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A82A451.4F8FCF0@mutualwheel.com> <meHg6.93$uw5.11129@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <9602td$oqs$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <

> For example, given:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANSI rules allow you to pass Group-1, Elem1, Elem2, and Elem3 but
*not*
> Group-2.
>
> (The historical reasons have to do with slack bytes and
synchronousation -
> but most compilers have not enforced this rule for YEARS)

Must have been historical reasons (nee 'grandpa's way'), certainly not
boundary requirements (unless some brain-damaged compiler is inserting
slack-bytes in my record definition);  Assuming "Group-1" (above) is
on a double-word boundary, "Elem2" is on a byte boundary and "Elem3"
is on a half-word boundary.

But, then again, 77-levels still abound (memory savers, don't you
know).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subprograms and the CALL statement...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T19:10:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9624dv$6dp$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<qb448t0qnnfth95kksdt04n3utgqj5t0pk@4ax.com> <3A823E94.BCA76CCD@worldnet.att.net> <3A82A451.4F8FCF0@mutualwheel.com> <meHg6.93$uw5.11129@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <9602td$oqs$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <_p0h6.3565$ZJ5.297487@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Ok, the *actual" problem case (still not worried about by any compilers) is:

  01  Group1.
       05 Elem1  Pic X.
       05  Group2.
             10 Synced-Field  Pic S9 Binary Sync.
       05  Elem3  Pic X.

 Call "whatever" using Group2
        vs
  Call "whatever" using Synced-Field

In *some* implementation any required "slack" bytes to put synced-field on
an "appropriate" boundary are included in Group2 - while in other
implementations they are put BEFORE Group2.  (both are standard conforming)

The called program may or may not expect its "received" item to be on an
appropriate boundary - and "other" peculiar things can happen
(theoretically).  Anyway, this rule is being removed from the Standard - and
isn't even enforced by any existing compiler (that I know of - but there may
be some somewhere that does)

An even "uglier" example is:

01 GroupA.
    05 Tabl occurs 5 times.
          10   ElemA Pic S9(01) sync
          10   ElemB Pic X.

Call "whatever" using Tabl (2)
```

---

## Thread: need help. tables — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: need help. tables

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T07:56:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Stevens wrote:
> --
> And, while straying from the original question, the next step is what I
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmer that used this technique.

Some of us don't want to waste 30,000 bytes on a 9 byte table.  I
started on mainframes with 16k words and 20 years ago was using 8bit
micros where the availble program space was around 40Kb.

You do realise that the compiler will allocate a fixed maximum size for
the table don't you ?  While your calculation will establish the logical
size of the table this mechanism is not dynamic and the physical size
won't vary from the maximum.

I just add a dummy entry to my predefined tables and process until I see
that:

> 01 xxx-list.
>    05 pic x(3) value "AAA".
>    05 pic x(3) value "AAA".
>    05 pic x(3) value "AAA".
     05 pic x(3) value high-values.
> 01 xxx-table
>    redefines xxx-list.
>    05 xxx-entry
        occurs 4
>       indexed by ndx.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     varying ndx from 1 by 1
      until xxx-entry(ndx) = high-values . . .

Yes, I have to adjust the OCCURS, but I could use 78s to do that.  I
don't waste resources.
```

#### ↳ Re: need help. tables

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T15:01:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95vljj$del$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz>...

>Some of us don't want to waste 30,000 bytes on a 9 byte table.

Amen! I was shocked at this...not surprising he's only found one other
programmer who does it that way...<G> (mind you, that's one too many...<G>)
  I
>I just add a dummy entry to my predefined tables and process until I see
>that:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't waste resources.

It is interesting, Richard, that for many years I used the same technique as
you describe. (Maybe it's a Kiwi thing..<G>?)

Then I went through a phase of moving the thing I was looking for to the
last entry in the table (where your high-values are) and doing the perform
until I got a match. If the matching index was equal to the number of
entries in the table, then it was not found... Describing it now seems kinda
weird...

Today I use Search...

01 table-search-flag   pic x value space.
     88 found-it      value '1'.
     88 not-found   value '0'.

set ndx to 1
search xxx-entry
      at end
           set not-found to TRUE
           < ...or, do whatever you want for NOT FOUND>
      when  xxx-entry (ndx) = whatever
           set found-it to TRUE
           <...or, do whatever you want for FOUND>
end-search
if not-found.....etc.
else
      move xxx-entry (ndx) to wherever....etc.
end-if

It makes it easier to locate specific table lookups in the code (look for
SEARCH rather than PERFORM (not that you need to do that with MY code of
course..<G>)), and it LOOKS more like COBOL...

For large tables I implement SEARCH...ALL and use the compiler's binary
chop. For exceptionally large or weird tables,  go right back to basics and
implement my own tertiary or skewed chop using PERFORM...<G>

I would recommend SEARCH to the users of this forum.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: need help. tables

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-08T22:39:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95vsd9$uns$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz> <95vljj$del$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
    I would add one feature to all the methods of searching so far.  Before
doing any of these types of searches, compare your previous search argument
and search result with your current search argument.  You oftentimes can
bypass the overhead of searching if what you're looking for is the same
thing you just got done looking for last time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need help. tables

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T07:11:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A83FA81.E481EDA6@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz> <95vljj$del$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <95vsd9$uns$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:

>     I would add one feature to all the methods of searching so far.  Before
> doing any of these types of searches, compare your previous search argument
> and search result with your current search argument.  You oftentimes can
> bypass the overhead of searching if what you're looking for is the same
> thing you just got done looking for last time.

Also, for a small table, sometimes it is more efficient to do a linear search
than a binary search just walking the table can be the best solution - but as
you say, not as efficient as no search.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: need help. tables

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T07:07:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A83F9B1.CAF3A870@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz> <95vljj$del$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Large variable length tables can often be re-engineered.   Sometimes the program
could be designed with match/merge logic instead.   Or keyed files or database
records...

Such tables can either waste tremendous amounts of space, can be in danger of
overflow, or both.

Aborts 2 years after you wrote the program due to overflow can do weird and
costly things.
```

#### ↳ Re: need help. tables

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T09:11:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Fri, 09 Feb 2001 07:56:30 +0000, Richard Plinston wrote:

>Ed Stevens wrote:
>> --
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>that:

I don't agree with you here. The space allocated will be the total space of
the first 01 level, in case of the example 9 bytes. The compiler will give a
warning that the size of the redefine is not equal to the original size and
that's it. You add new 05 levels under the first 01 level and without
changing anything else you're up and running. Not one byte of data storage
wasted, just a little overhead when calculating the tablesize.
>
>> 01 xxx-list.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't waste resources.

Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

```
Pete.
```

```
One final note. Sometimes, when using tables, it's advantagous to know the hir
frequency expected for each element in the table. For example, a retail company
may have seasonal items whose sales activity occurs in a 1 or 2 month window.

In this case, IF statements referencing those months before doing the table
search could drasticly reudce the CPU time spent searching the table.

Jack.   





In article <96bcfk$vu4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>, Peter E. C. Dashwood says...

…[38 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: need help. tables

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T01:51:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A849EB2.734AD5C4@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g8hu2r0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
Willem Clements wrote:

> >> refer to as the "self-adjusting table."
> >> =======
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> wasted, just a little overhead when calculating the tablesize.

First of all your code is invalid according to -85 rules where
"..neither the original definition nor the redefinition can include an
item whose size is variable as defined in the OCCURS clause.".

MF extensions allow this though, as may others.

The -85 rules also includes the note: "It is important to observe that
the REDEFINES specifies the redefinition of a storage area, not of the
data items occupying the area."

In all compilers that I have used a redfinition at the record (01)
level, whether implicit (FD) or explicit makes the storage area the size
of the largest record.

Running a compile right now of the above code allocated a fixed area of
30,000 bytes.  Changing the logical size of the area by setting the
depending on does not change the size of the area dynamically.

What confirmation, by such means as memory maps, dumps, etc have you
that shows that your compiler does not generate a 30k area ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need help. tables

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T17:44:07+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g8ihtj2.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g8hu2r0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <3A849EB2.734AD5C4@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Sat, 10 Feb 2001 01:51:46 +0000, Richard Plinston wrote:

>Willem Clements wrote:
>
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>that shows that your compiler does not generate a 30k area ?

I agree with you on the implicit define, but I'm pretty sure that the IBM VSE
Cobol compiler allocates the storage based on the original define, not on the
redefine, of course other compilers may behave differently.
I haven't got any proof, because I don't have access to a mainframe anymore
(it took up too much space in the living room)

Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

```
redefinition (at the 01-level)
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

```
> I don't remember what happens with the old DOS/VS COBOL compiler - but with
> VS COBOL II and "later" - I think you will find that you get a compile-time
> error if you try and redefine a smaller (original) item with a larger
> redefinition (at the 01-level)

I don't know how serious that error is, but going the other way I have this
with CoBOL for MVS.  The error is in a vendor supplied copy member:

3200  IGYDS1073-I   "BL-LINE-ITEM" redefined a larger item.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need help. tables

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T16:07:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961plq$di5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95m7c4$kia$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7FE2F1.57469509@Azonic.co.nz> <95p8mf$abu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A803836.5DBF074E@home.com> <95u8vh$lm1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A83A2AE.DFD8CB38@Azonic.co.nz> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g8hu2r0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <3A849EB2.734AD5C4@Azonic.co.nz> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g8ihtj2.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <961cvi$vnp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3A843C96.46075268@brazee.net>`

```
Redefining a larger by a smaller item is fully ANSI conforming - and your
compiler *must* support it.  It is only redefining a smaller by a larger
that is a problem.
```

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz

> don't waste resources.

On the other hand, if you have a resource available to you - in this
case memory - and you DON'T use it, you have wasted an opportunity
that can never be reclaimed.

"Use it or lose it" is sometimes a good rule.
```

<a id="t-faa2679d4a"></a>
## Thread: LIKE clause??? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: LIKE clause???

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T00:08:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9601ih$fd7$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<JD%f6.29141$k8.6996229@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
IBM's COBOL/400 (or ILE COBOL) has a "like" clause.  It does work like
(similar to?) the PL/I clause - and the TYPE and TYPEDEF clauses in the
draft of the next Standard or more accurately it is really closer to the
SAME AS clause in the draft Standard.
```

<a id="t-d68d69d1d1"></a>
## Thread: Subroutines — 1 msgs

### Re: Subroutines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T00:40:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9603em$jjh$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<95ebmb$qr6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A7B99ED.AD0ADAF@optonline.net> <95gdnb$jp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95h24b$ltn$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <95lnv6$9fm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <95qmnc$k5g$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Recursive programs (not OO) also makes it possible to have multiple
instances of the same program "active".  Neither OO nor recursive programs
are Standard or particularly portable (yet).  I don't remember (if we ever
knew) which compiler is being used.  Depending on the answer to this, either
OO or recursive programs or both may already be available.
```

<a id="t-9b55870a79"></a>
## Thread: Error 9/13 when printing through a network with Net Express / Win95 — 3 msgs

### Error 9/13 when printing through a network with Net Express / Win95

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T12:00:37+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<960f17$plf$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
Hi all,

I hope someone has come accross the following problem :

We are using Merant's Net Express 3.0 to develop some character based
applications that are running on a Windows NT server network. The clients
are both Win 95b and Win 98 PCs. We are using the Windows runtime to run
this applications (runw.exe).

The problem is that when we print from a Windows 95 PC to a Network printer,
we occasionally receive a 9/13 error (File not found) and the application
hungs! There is no rule under which this problem occurs,  which means that
the same PC can issue any number of prints before the problem occurs.

The same problem never appears from a Windows 98 client, or when we use the
character runtime (run.exe) from a Windows 95 application! (but
unfortunately we have to use run.exe to overcome some difficulties with our
local character set).

Has anyone come accross this situation ?

Thanks in advance for your concern.

Regards

Panos Zotos
```

#### ↳ Re: Error 9/13 when printing through a network with Net Express / Win95

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T19:00:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-CNVdz44D0Gb8@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<960f17$plf$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
On Fri, 9 Feb 2001 10:00:37, "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr> wrote:

> Hi all,
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Windows 95 caused me many, many, many problems with its decrepit
buggy printing system.

Try applying every fix in sight that may vaguely relate to the 
printing system and/or update or replace all printer drivers...
```

#### ↳ Re: Error 9/13 when printing through a network with Net Express / Win95

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T14:52:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a86a64f.497736101@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<960f17$plf$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
"Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr> schrieb:

>The problem is that when we print from a Windows 95 PC to a Network printer,
>we occasionally receive a 9/13 error (File not found) and the application
>hungs! There is no rule under which this problem occurs,  which means that
>the same PC can issue any number of prints before the problem occurs.

I assume that your are printing to an LPT device (LPT1:, LPT2:). Try
to change the device name to (\LPT1, \LPT2) - Windows sometimes can't
find it's devices if your current directory has a long name.

Best regards

Sven
```

<a id="t-44866a0bc3"></a>
## Thread: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol? — 3 msgs

### Re: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol?

- **From:** "pwmeister" <peter.meister@tietoenator-nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T13:31:18+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<960npi$hh5$1@news1.enator.se>`
- **References:** `<8RZf6.25$D13.51393@nnrp2.sbc.net> <95u1d2$kma$1@news1.enator.se> <3A82AB67.2831A481@brazee.net>`

```
Mr Brazee

why what? I'm not quite sure if I understand your question?

1) Do you mean, why anyone would use a precompiler to code DOWHILE today? 2)
or, why I actually did send a code snippette on DOWHILE? 3) or if I use
DOWHILE? 4)

1) We recently won an outsource contract for a couple of system
(accounting/finance/etc) wich runs under MVS.  Most of the code has been
developed using this precompiler together with an old (ANS 68?) cobol
compiler. I would guess that I have at least  900 programs (batch and IMS
online) which requires the precompiler to generate code.
The contract does not include any work on renewing.  It is there and I have
to live with it (poor me..).
2) well, it is better than replaying "do your own homework", although my
example on DOWHILE does not belong to COBOL.
3) beside the responibilities in 1) I also develop in COBOL II (ANS -86) on
MVS and AS/400 (not ILE) for another system. I have been using Cobol for the
last 18 years and I use PERFORM and inline PERFORM as anybody else in this
n-g. However, for the first system I'll have to use "DOWHILE" because of the
circumstances.

Peter Meister, utvecklare, TietoEnator, Sweden


Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A82AB67.2831A481@brazee.net>...
>So the question is "why"?
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T07:01:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A83F82F.606BB606@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8RZf6.25$D13.51393@nnrp2.sbc.net> <95u1d2$kma$1@news1.enator.se> <3A82AB67.2831A481@brazee.net> <960npi$hh5$1@news1.enator.se>`

```


pwmeister wrote:

> Mr Brazee
>
> why what? I'm not quite sure if I understand your question?
>
> 1) Do you mean, why anyone would use a precompiler to code DOWHILE today?

Yes.   I don't see that the gain is worth the cost (moving away from standard
CoBOL is certainly a cost when you hire maintenance programmers).   Your answer
is that you use it today because your customer used it in the past.  So why did
they use it in the past?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a "while statement " in Cobol?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T11:12:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A85220D.E9B4D831@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8RZf6.25$D13.51393@nnrp2.sbc.net> <95u1d2$kma$1@news1.enator.se> <3A82AB67.2831A481@brazee.net> <960npi$hh5$1@news1.enator.se> <3A83F82F.606BB606@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Yes.   I don't see that the gain is worth the cost (moving away from standard
> CoBOL is certainly a cost when you hire maintenance programmers).   Your answer
> is that you use it today because your customer used it in the past.  So why did
> they use it in the past?

I would assume because it gave them an in-line perform not available in
standard Cobol until -85.

I recall there were many extension packages in the 70s that offered
features that later became avialable in -85 cobol.  Decision Tables, for
example, would now be coded with EVALUATE.
```

<a id="t-801245d87d"></a>
## Thread: CANCEL statament question (Microfocus Cobol ocobol 4.1) — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### CANCEL statament question (Microfocus Cobol ocobol 4.1)

- **From:** Roberto Wagner <rwagner@arnaldocastro.com.uy>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T15:48:48+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A83E72F.C10D1E4C@arnaldocastro.com.uy>`

```
This is an extract from the microfocus cobol reference manual about
CANCEL statement

    "The CANCEL statement determines whether or not a subprogram will be

    re-entered in initial or last-used state. It does not necessarily
free any memory
    used by the subprogram. However, in many operating systems, if the
    subprogram has been dynamically loaded, this statement will also
free the
    memory originally used by the subprogram. EXTERNAL files and data
areas of
    the subprogram are never freed by a CANCEL statement. "


Question: On AIX Operating System, this statement free memory of
subprograms called ?

Thanks in advance.
Roberto Wagner
```

#### ↳ Re: CANCEL statament question (Microfocus Cobol ocobol 4.1)

- **From:** "Bill Yarnall" <Bill.Yarnall@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T13:58:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96774t$9f1$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3A83E72F.C10D1E4C@arnaldocastro.com.uy>`

```
The CANCEL statement does in fact cause a free() operation - However because
of the way that UNIX (including AIX) performs garbage collection the freed
memory does not actually return to the OS, instead it is available for
re-use by the same process. This memory re-use is only possible if you also
utilize the -l0 setting for COBSW.
What this means in practical terms is:1) You must set COBSW=-l0 and then the
CANCEL statement will allow memory to be re-used,  and improve memory
utilization and efficiency. If you do not use CANCEL no memory segment will
not be re-used. Memory doesn't actually get returned to the OS until the
process terminates.
"Roberto Wagner" <rwagner@arnaldocastro.com.uy> wrote in message
news:3A83E72F.C10D1E4C@arnaldocastro.com.uy...
> This is an extract from the microfocus cobol reference manual about
> CANCEL statement
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Error handling question — 6 msgs

### Error handling question

- **From:** "Progger" <turner_karen@mail.chattanooga.gov>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T08:52:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8046FCF931A0CD9D.646ABEB1643F7093.5FAD57D335A77FF9@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Is there a way in Microfocus cobol/net express to force a pgm to exit with a
fatal error? ....the point being to single to the calling job (perl script)
not to continue and send email to the user that the job cancelled and why.

So far the only way that I can see to do it is on an error write to a file,
then when the pgm terminates check for its existance....surely there is a
better way!
```

```
100-MAIN.
        IF ERROR
            PERFORM 998-ERROR-ROUTINE

998-ERROR-ROUTINE.

       CALL 'CORP-POST-ERROR'.
       GO TO 999-EXIT-ROUTINE.

999-EXIT-ROUTINE.
       STOP RUN STOP-RUN-VALUE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error handling question

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T21:15:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A845F8D.F38BCDB8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8046FCF931A0CD9D.646ABEB1643F7093.5FAD57D335A77FF9@lp.airnews.net>`

```


Progger wrote:

> Is there a way in Microfocus cobol/net express to force a pgm to exit with a
> fatal error? ....the point being to single to the calling job (perl script)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> better way!

Not an immediate answer to your problem, but it may give you some ideas.

When using RM/COBOL I had a standard Declaratives Section checking for
file-status errors, checking just like you are, and errors made the program go
belly-up. (Lousy I know, but that's how I started). Perhaps there's a dab hand
here knowledgeable about Declaratives who can illustrate an extension checking
for things other than file-status.

Using NetExpress, solely OO classes - I ran into a different problem. Separate
classes for files, I can signal back to the Business Logic that a file error has
occurred and take remedial action as appropriate.

Now rather like you are finding with your structured programs - just telling
something an error occurred isn't sufficient - you need a mechanism to break out
of the program, (i.e. 'neatly' return to your Main Menu without an ABORT).. As
an example, in Class X,  Method A finds an error and I want to exit the program.
It wont do that. It will happily trundle on attempting to invoke Methods B, C, D
etc..giving some weirdo results until it goes belly-up. As a parallel this is
your structured Program X performing Para A, (finding an error) and then still
attempting to perform Paras. B, C, D etc...

This is by no means the only solution, but this is how I do it in OO, (which you
could adapt to structured ) :-

Working-storage seciton.

01 ws-ErrorCode                     pic x(4) comp-5.
   88 ContinueProgram               value 0.
   88 CancelProgram                 value 99.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "begin".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 ....
Procedure Division using .....
 .... initial stuff......
set ContinueProgram to true
invoke self "get-default-info"

if ContinueProgram
   invoke self "X10-open-facility-files"

  if ContinueProgram
     invoke self "X20-MaterialCollection"

    if ContinueProgram
       invoke self "X30-open-descriptions"

     if ContinueProgram
        invoke self "X40-PipeSizeCollection"

      if ContinueProgram
         invoke etc.......

          if ContinueProgram
             invoke self "exit-program"
             .                                           <--- The All important
'period' goes here
End Method "begin".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "error-exit".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Procedure Division.
  set CancelProgram to true
  invoke self "exit-program"
End Method "error-exit".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "exit-program".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Procedure division.
   invoke os-Dialog "hide"
   invoke self "close-files"
   invoke self "finalize-objects"
End Method "exit-program".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------

and ..... a method which might either cause or receive the result of an error :-

       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "item-and-point-classes".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-result                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       Procedure Division.
        invoke ItemNames "new" returning os-ItemNames
        invoke os-ItemNames "begin"
               using os-Desktop os-DescripsDBI returning ls-result

         if   ls-result = zeroes
              invoke Points "new" returning os-PointNames
              invoke os-PointNames "begin"
                     using os-Desktop os-DescripsDBI returning ls-result
         End-if

         if ls-result <> zeroes                  *> somewhere prior I have
already issued
            invoke self "error-exit"            *> a windows messagebox for the
error
            EXIT METHOD
         End-if

       *> There could be some more code here but it gets bypassed by the
        *> EXIT METHOD above, if appropriate.

       End Method "item-and-point-classes".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

AND........ before Bill jumps in - the new COBOL 200X  has a list (approx 122)
of Exception Conditions, functioning much like the Declarative/file status
checking.

HTH.

Jimmy, Calgary, AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Error handling question

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T13:10:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<963ekh$vo1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8046FCF931A0CD9D.646ABEB1643F7093.5FAD57D335A77FF9@lp.airnews.net>`

```
In article
<8046FCF931A0CD9D.646ABEB1643F7093.5FAD57D335A77FF9@lp.airnews.net>,
  "Progger" <turner_karen@mail.chattanooga.gov> wrote:
> Is there a way in Microfocus cobol/net express to force a pgm to exit
with a
> fatal error? ....the point being to single to the calling job (perl
script)
> not to continue and send email to the user that the job cancelled and
why.
>
> So far the only way that I can see to do it is on an error write to a
file,
> then when the pgm terminates check for its existance....surely there
is a
> better way!
>

Hi:

Yes, there certainly is a better way than the despicable method
suggested above.

Presumably, the user gets some form of output from the job, yes?
Instead code your COBOL program to produce a meaningful message telling
them exactly what the problem was and the steps required to remedy
the error. That error message could easily appear on the output
or, if no output was produced because of the error, be the output
itself. Since the user is presumably expecting output, he would be
happier to find out that his output was in error rather than wondering
where the hell it was.  Expecting the user to check his email to see
if his job ran is a bit much, no? Why is a COBOL job is being run from
a Perl script anyway?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Error handling question

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T07:53:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8644EB.70FF327C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8046FCF931A0CD9D.646ABEB1643F7093.5FAD57D335A77FF9@lp.airnews.net> <963ekh$vo1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Yes, there certainly is a better way than the despicable method
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> a Perl script anyway?

Some of us automate processes for the users.  Instead of producing a
schedule for the user to run certain programs asking then to start the
program, enter parameters etc and inspect the result, we build a
schedule for the _computer_.  The computer runs the program, if all goes
well then the user need not know that his data has been updated,
however, if there is an exception the user needs to be involved.

For example orders may be arriving by ftp in EDI or XML format.  These
can be processed through to printing a picking slip in the warehouse
without involving the sales dept - unless an error occurs. (I do this).

For example in a shipping system, a process may be asking the port
company's web server for status of a number of containers to update the
shipping system and send EMails to carriers as they become available on
the wharf and cleared by customs and MAF.

Now this _could_ be done (and was being) by having a user connect to the
internet and bring up the enquiry form, key in the container number, see
the status and key this into the shipping system.  But now they only
need to be involved if there is an exception.

Do your clients have to do everything manually ?

But I suppose some are happier with a manual telephone patch panel than
an automatic exchange.
```

<a id="t-1c9783d1a6"></a>
## Thread: Press Release on COBOL Public Review — 4 msgs

### Press Release on COBOL Public Review

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T12:53:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961ecp$889$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those interested in the future of COBOL, the following is the complete
Press Release "requesting" Public Review Comments on the draft COBOL
Standard,

(a web copy of this can be found at:
  http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/01-0074.htm

The method described below is for submitting *US* public review comments.
If you are "outside" the US, please contact your national Standards body -
as documented at:
  http://www.iso.ch/addresse/membodies.html)

"IT/01-0162
J4/01-0074

National Committee for Information Technology Standards (NCITS)
NCITS Secretariat, Information Technology Industry Council (ITIC)
1250 Eye St. NW, Suite 200, Washington, DC 20005
Telephone 202-737-8888; Fax 202-638-4922;
Email: ncits@itic.org

Date: January 31, 2001

NCITS (National Committee for Information Technology Standards is announcing
the public comment period for ISO/IEC FCD 1989, Information Technology-
Programming languages, their environments and system software interfaces-
Programming Language COBOL. The public review extends from February23, 2001
to April 9, 2001, the U.S. TAG (NCITS/J4) will accept comments immediately.
ISO/IEC FCD 1989 can be downloaded from the NCITS/J4home page at:
http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

COBOL is the most widely used and most accepted of programming languages.
First developed in the late 1950's, COBOL has grown and evolved over time
with the release of international standards. Now in the wake of twin "40
Years of COBOL" celebrations in Japan and the US, a new draft international
standard is available for review.

The features of the new standard continue the COBOL tradition of being the
vanguard of international programming languages. The new standard continues
the tradition of the original COBOL specification, created to provide an
easy, natural syntax that is easy to use and maintain.

Key features of this draft include greater internationalization of the
language, better interlanguage communication and object orientation. In
addition, numerous updates to the syntax allows COBOL to continue as the
pre-eminent language for processing and manipulating data.  The introduction
of the new Validate feature is just one example. The new standard also
implements common exception handling.  Free format source will free the
programmer from the traditional 80 column restraints of the original COBOL.
Inline comments will make it easier to comment specific features of a
program to ease future maintenance.  New TYPEDEFS and new data types will
make it easier for the COBOL programmer to communicate with other languages
as well as making it easier for these other languages to make use of COBOL
programs that contain key business rules.

New COBOL development using Object Orientation will now be possible. The
COBOL OO syntax is feature rich and robust.  Object oriented programmers
will find the syntax familiar, while traditional COBOL programmers will be
pleased with the continued tradition of an English like syntax that makes
programs easy to read and understand.

COBOL is the language of the new millennium and NCITS/J4 is very pleased to
present this update for public review.

Public Review Comment Instructions

To facilitate speedy consideration of comments, please submit them via email
to Deborah Donovan at ddonovan@itic.org,  with a copy to:  PSA Department
(psa@ansi.org) ANSI, 11 West  42nd Street, New York, NY  10036.  In
addition, all public review comments must include name, company name,
address, telephone number, and email address if applicable.

Any public review comments received after April 9, 2001 will be addressed
and considered for future balloting and/or revision.

[Note to Commentors]:  Due to their meeting schedule for ISO/IECFCD 1989,
the U.S. TAG (NCITS/J4) does not plan to be able to issue responses to
commentors until May 2002.

Call for Patents

A call for possible patents and pertinent issues (copyrights, trademarks) is
also being issued. Please submit information on these issues to the NCITS
Secretariat at 1250 Eye Street NW, Suite 200, Washington DC 20005.Email:
NCITS @itic.org

NCITS issues this call for patents in accordance with ANSI Procedures for
the Development and Coordination of American National Standards."
```

#### ↳ Re: Press Release on COBOL Public Review

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T20:23:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A84534F.24BE835C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<961ecp$889$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
<snip>

> Call for Patents
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Development and Coordination of American National Standards."

Bill,

I was curious about the implications of the above when I first read Don
Schricker's message. Given that COBOL is a 'freebie' - what are the implications
of the above statement ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Press Release on COBOL Public Review

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T16:14:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961q6a$46d$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<961ecp$889$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3A84534F.24BE835C@home.com>`

```
The final COBOL Standard may or MAY NOT be a freebie.  If you look at the
draft FCD, it states,

"This ISO document is a draft International Standard and is
copyright-protected by ISO. Except as permitted under the applicable laws of
the user's country, neither this ISO draft nor any extract from it may be
reproduced, stored in a retrieval system or transmitted in any form or by
any means, electronic, photocopying, recording or otherwise, without prior
written permission being secured.

Requests for permission to reproduce should be addressed to ISO at the
address below or ISO's member body in the country of the requester:
 Copyright Manager
 ISO Central Secretariat
 1 rue de Varemb�
 1211 Geneva 20 Switzerland
 tel: +41 22 749 0111
 fax +41 22 734 0179
  e-mail central@iso.ch

Reproduction may be subject to royalty payments or a licensing agreement.
Violators may be prosecuted."

Later on (in the same document) it states,

"Any organization interested in reproducing the COBOL standard and
specifications in whole or in part, using ideas from this document as the
basis for an instruction manual or for any other purpose, is free to do so."

Given this (apparent) contradiction, if you do NOT follow up with the
indicated people, I do *not* think that you should assume that the final
(approved) ANSI or ISO Standard will be "a freebie".  It may be - but it
also may not be.

Contact your national Standards Body (as well as the listed ISO and ANSI
sources) if this is an issue for you (or if you have any access to ANY
existing Language Reference Manual that is copyrighted and includes text
that also appears in the draft Standard).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Press Release on COBOL Public Review

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T14:27:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dch6.702$vd7.85494@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<961ecp$889$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3A84534F.24BE835C@home.com> <961q6a$46d$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
It's not that unusual to copyright-protect intellectual property while
simultanenously granting a limited-use license to the public-at-large.

I do that with much of the software I provide as "freebies." (e.g., my VSAM
space calculator, COBOL data tutorials, etc).

The reason I do it is simple: while I am more than willing to allow anyone
to use the software to solve a problem for themself or an employer/client, I
am not willing to allow that work to become a component of a a product which
is mass-marketed. (Not willing without some additional consideration, that
is).

When an author explicitly places intellectual property in the public domain,
he loses ALL control over its future use and distribution.
```

<a id="t-2ee44ddc45"></a>
## Thread: CICS Garbage in WS - LE vs. COBOLII? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS Garbage in WS - LE vs. COBOLII?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T19:11:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ohXg6.15847$Sl.694504@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

This isn't strictly a matter of applications coding... but there have been
some discussions of this which have floated o'er the cubicle wall and I
thought I'd mention it.

I am kinda sketchy on the details but... it seems that when testing a new
version of CICS folks have found that programs which have previously been
well-behaved are now showing garbage in unitialised WS fields.  This
happens to either LE programs or COBOLII (I *said* I was sketchy).

Has anyone else run across this phenomenon or one similar?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS Garbage in WS - LE vs. COBOLII?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-09T16:04:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<961plt$qq6$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ohXg6.15847$Sl.694504@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
What you are talking about is a "well known" issue with post-OS/VS COBOL.

If you are using the VS COBOL II run-time library, then make certain that
you use the WSCLEAR run-time option (to initialize fields without VALUE
clauses to binary zero).

If you are using the LE run-time library, then use the STORAGE (00, ...)
run-time option.  (You will want to look at the other parts of the STORAGE
option - as they SHOULD be different under CICS and non-CICS.

There are no compile-time options to "impact" this - but using VALUE clauses
will "solve" the problem as well.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS Garbage in WS - LE vs. COBOLII?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T00:56:27+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<%k0h6.15886$Sl.697441@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
(00, ...)
> run-time option.  (You will want to look at the other parts of the
STORAGE
> option - as they SHOULD be different under CICS and non-CICS.
>
> There are no compile-time options to "impact" this - but using VALUE
clauses
> will "solve" the problem as well.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > This isn't strictly a matter of applications coding... but there
have been
> > some discussions of this which have floated o'er the cubicle wall
and I
> > thought I'd mention it.
> >
> > I am kinda sketchy on the details but... it seems that when testing
a new
> > version of CICS folks have found that programs which have
previously been
> > well-behaved are now showing garbage in unitialised WS fields.  This
> > happens to either LE programs or COBOLII (I *said* I was sketchy).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

Although these options WILL work in both the LE and COBOL2
environments, they are overhead. How substantial ? It depends on the
workload.

I totally agree. Going forward, new programs should use either the
VALUE clause or issue initialisations during HOUSEKEEPING.

Cheers,

Bill


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS Garbage in WS - LE vs. COBOLII?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2001-02-10T21:48:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A85B65B.B1BE4CC1@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<ohXg6.15847$Sl.694504@iad-read.news.verio.net> <961plt$qq6$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I am enough of a programming purist that I have always argued that code
that depends on a initial value in a variable that is not explicitly
initialized is "incorrect code".  I suspect the COBOL standard would
describe such values as "undefined" and leave behavior of such a program
up to the implementation.  To write code whose ultimate behavior is
determined by factors outside the programmer's control is not goodness.

Unfortunately in the real world you have programmers that have gotten
away with poor coding practices, and removing operating system support
that allows such code to run with some degree of success becomes a
political issue.  When we converted to COBOL II many years ago, we tried
running without WSCLEAR in the CICS environment to improve performance. 
There was an initial attempt to clean up bad code as part of the
migration to COBOL II, but there was just too much bad code out there
and the "it used to run" argument won the battle.  We have been running
with WSCLEAR and the LE STORAGE equivalent ever since.

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> What you are talking about is a "well known" issue with post-OS/VS COBOL.
> If you are using the VS COBOL II run-time library, then make certain that
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
...
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS Garbage in WS - LE vs. COBOLII?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T21:46:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fuhg8toc97nvt7uim81gbgi87ujiqlouhd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ohXg6.15847$Sl.694504@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
On Fri, 09 Feb 2001 19:11:16 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:

>
>This isn't strictly a matter of applications coding... but there have been
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD
Doc,

In older releases of CICS (up to Version 2) CICS would initialize the working storage of a
COBOL program (OS/VS COBOL or VS/COBOL II) to binaty zeroes before the COBOL runtime would
insert the initial values.

Since CICS Version 3 this initialization by CICS no longer happens.  While one might argue
that this is a bug, it is actually a fix to a bug, because IBM always has stated that the
contents of initialized variables in Working-Storage is unpredictable.

How do you get out of the mess?

option1: Always initialize variables in your program.  Most important, at the beginning of
your program, do MOVE LOW-VALUES to mapnameO for all the maps you are working with.  But
initializing must be done for all other variables as well

option2: Let the runtime system do it for you.  WSCLEAR is the option for VS/COBOL,
STORAGE is the option for LE.  No changes to the program (good news).  Because of higher
overhad - longer responsetimes (bad news). Because of higher processor speed and faster
I/O system - less responsetime (good news)

Programmer time being more expensive than computer time, I would opt for option2 (and
still would ask all the programmers to work according to option 1)
 
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         What does CICS mean? - Crash IBM Computer Swiftly
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS Garbage in WS - LE vs. COBOLII? - Thanks to all!

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T20:57:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K6Yh6.16326$Sl.721116@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<ohXg6.15847$Sl.694504@iad-read.news.verio.net> <fuhg8toc97nvt7uim81gbgi87ujiqlouhd@4ax.com>`

```

To all - many thanks for you kind and rapid responses; they have been
relayed to the Appropriate Personnel and the response was:

'Well... we kinda figured that out but it's nice to see that we aren't the
only ones who came up with that problem.'

DD
```

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
