- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a86a46e.497254579@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<95en6t$320k$1@stargate1.inet.it> <95puo7$qgs$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
"Bill Yarnall" <Bill.Yarnall@merant.com> schrieb:

>There is a 2Gbyte limit on data files. If you require files larger than
>2Gbytes then you on Object COBOL V4.1 you must use 'File Striping'  in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>monolithic large files - Non Indexed files may also exceed the 2GB boundry.

The system reference for Object COBOL reads "File-Sizes: no limit" and
this seems to be true for c-isam files. On some Unixes there is a
system-dependant limit of 2GB - on Linux, 64 Bit adressing was just
recently introduced.

IDXFORMAT(3) has a limit of 1GB.

Best regards

Sven
```

RFr

#### ↳ Re: Sorting Tables with sort-merge ??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T10:11:55-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<966dhc$7at$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The ability to SORT a table is in the draft of the next Standard - but is
not currently ANSI/ISO conforming.  Micro Focus (MERANT) has had this
capability for several years - I do not know which other vendors already
support it as an extension.

Which compiler are you using?
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

Thanks. For as long as I've been writing, using the SORT verb to sort tables
that way (with INPUT/OUTPUT procedures) never occured to me. (Duh).

Kinda easier than writing your own bubble/insertion/shell/quick sort every
time, isn't it?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting Tables with sort-merge ??

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T07:29:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A87F352.4370127A@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3A86ABA0.20923005@netcologne.de> <cvhd8tsq7ndd1opj5igm5mueqmihdrpcge@4ax.com>`

```
I rarely have need to sort a table that can't be handled by an external sort
nor a standard CoBOL sort.

Real small tables can be accessed just as fast with a linear search than a
sorted search -  where "real small" means in one page of memory.

But last year I did have such an occasion and coded in a simple comb sort.
A simple perform worked.    If you want to code your own sort, look at comb
sort in the following web page.


http://www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm#comparisons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting Tables with sort-merge ??

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T06:54:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A88DA0E.D0727BDF@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3A86ABA0.20923005@netcologne.de> <cvhd8tsq7ndd1opj5igm5mueqmihdrpcge@4ax.com> <3A87F352.4370127A@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I rarely have need to sort a table that can't be handled by an external sort
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm#comparisons

A couple of years ago we had a performance problem in a CICS
application, where a large table of time dependent values had to be
sorted before it could be redisplayed.  The nature of the application
was that the sequence of values changed frequently (but not the sort
key).  And since it was CICS, we could not use the SORT verb.  We used
the comb sort from the ETK web page, and we were very pleased with the
results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting Tables with sort-merge ??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T09:02:06+01:00

#### ↳ Re: Sorting Tables with sort-merge ??

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T21:46:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hcig8toiq6qtnft6o80lhcr4nqg61c5ak7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3A86ABA0.20923005@netcologne.de>`

```
On Sun, 11 Feb 2001 16:11:28 +0100, "R.Freitag" <r.freitag@netcologne.de> wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>RFr

Sure,

and your compiler doesn't even have to know the new syntax of sorting tables.  Try
something like

SORT Sortfile INPUT PROCEDURE read-table OUTPUT PROCEDURE write-table

Read-Table.

    PERFORM VARYING Row-number FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL Row-number > Max-rows
       RELEASE Myrow(row-number)
    END-PERFORM
    .

Write-table
    MOVE 0 TO Row-number
    PERFORM UNTIL End-of-table
       RETURN Sort-file
          AT END
             Set End-of-table TO TRUE
          NOT AT END
             ADD 1 TO Row-number 
             MOVE Sort-rec TO Myrow(row-number
       END-RETURN
    END-PERFORM
    .




     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         What does CICS mean? - Crash IBM Computer Swiftly
```

## Thread: ISPF--SMR question — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### ISPF--SMR question

- **From:** "Kevin Solomon" <kjsolo@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T20:16:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KqCh6.130284$V22.26738659@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
This isn't really a Cobol question but, I believe many of you work or have
worked in an IBM 390 environment.

I'm using Fileaid in my JCL to extract information from the SMR syslog so
that I can then manipulate print job information in a statistics program.  I
only want to extract print jobs that have successfully completed.

I'm coding an IF statement that extracts the required job names for 1 Julian
Day.  Since the job names will not be unique entries (one entry might be a
purge, one might be ended etc.) I need at least one more AND condition that
will exclude multiple references to the same print job.  What string or
strings should I look for in this AND condition or following conditions?
Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: ISPF--SMR question

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T00:29:22+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ih3e8t0q66o0k4v0hgpqr0fc5aoarepdmf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<KqCh6.130284$V22.26738659@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
On Sun, 11 Feb 2001 20:16:42 GMT "Kevin Solomon" <kjsolo@home.com> wrote:

:>This isn't really a Cobol question but, I believe many of you work or have
:>worked in an IBM 390 environment.

:>I'm using Fileaid in my JCL to extract information from the SMR syslog so
:>that I can then manipulate print job information in a statistics program.  I
:>only want to extract print jobs that have successfully completed.

:>I'm coding an IF statement that extracts the required job names for 1 Julian
:>Day.  Since the job names will not be unique entries (one entry might be a
:>purge, one might be ended etc.) I need at least one more AND condition that
:>will exclude multiple references to the same print job.  What string or
:>strings should I look for in this AND condition or following conditions?
:>Thanks.

I would expect that a jobname/jobid combination would be unique.
```

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-11T22:57:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A871885.108C68A5@neo.rr.com>`

Kevin Solomon wrote:
<snip>
> I'm coding an IF statement that extracts the required job names for 1 Julian
> Day.  Since the job names will not be unique entries (one entry might be a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

I would think you will want to look for the $HASP message that
shows when the job comes into the system.  It is at that point
that a unique job number is assigned to that job.

If I understand what you are doing correctly, your AND condition
is just the start of your headaches.  But, then, I'm not aware of
SMR.  What is it?
```

<a id="t-f8a9dc6fc2"></a>
## Thread: StdTmplt — 2 msgs

### StdTmplt

- **From:** Jose <cobol@myrealbox.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T05:39:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<967sul$aqh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
How i can add a new .ppj into \cobol\StdTmplt folder to have it when
i "open new project..." under powerCOBOL? i want to have my own
ABM "model" file like a StandardForm, StandrdDialog, ActiveX and
ProjectOnly. It's possible? if yes.. how?
Thanks

Jose


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: StdTmplt

- **From:** "Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T10:50:35
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<968fb1$853$1@venus.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<967sul$aqh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am sure you can copy a template of your own to the specified directory. A
more interesting way a doing what you want, is to crate a directory of your
own, populate it with your own templates, and with a project opened, select
"Tools", then "Options...", and fill the "Template Folder" box with the full
name of the newly created directory. When you create a new project, all the
templates (either those supplied by Fujitsu, as your own) will be presented.

best regards,
Paulo Vieira
http://www.emporsoft.pt

"Jose" <cobol@myrealbox.com> wrote in message
news:967sul$aqh$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

> i "open new project..." under powerCOBOL? i want to have my own

first week of October in Anaheim - if not check out cobolreport.com.

COBOL get together without BIG BLUE ! If you mainframers particularly
had any intent of making it to Anaheim, wouldn't you want to see IBM's
latest COBOL offerings, or chew the fat with IBM techies.

If you truly care about COBOL which gives you your bread and pays your
mortgage, may I suggest you do something about it - start pestering your
local IBM people as to why the MAJOR player in COBOL wont be there.

I have no personal (hidden) interest in this. If I do make it to Anaheim
be assured I can quite happily occupy my time 'bending the ears' of the
Merant (Micro Focus) people <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB

```
Hi folks,
    how do i compare to a field defined as PIC +9(3)???
Im using mf-cobol, and the coding looks like

move 100 to f001.
if f001 = 100
   display 'true1'.
if f001 = +100
   display 'true2'.
if f002 = '+100'
   display 'true3'.

all 3 variations produce a false result.

Thanks for reading,
Wolf
```

```
What is the definition for f002?
It seems that if you test f001, which I assume is PIC +9(3), you will get a
true.
On the other hand, it is possible that what you are looking for, is an
unedited numeric field, something like PIC S9(3).
regards,
Paulo Vieira

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T08:19:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<968nii$6a6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<968ecg$bhn$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at>`

```
Compare them to numbers.  (+1, -1, etc.).  As long as you are comparing a
number to an ASCII string, then you will have maintenance problems.

"Wolf Grossi" <wg@*NoEMail*PleaseRespond*NG*ID*magro-soft.com> wrote in
message news:968ecg$bhn$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at...
> Hi folks,
>     how do i compare to a field defined as PIC +9(3)???
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: number check...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T07:14:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A87EFCE.3D2F803F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<968ecg$bhn$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at>`

```
PIC +9(3) is only used for output, although there is a function
available to convert it to the type of number the computer uses.
What you want is PIC S9(3), in which case all three of your comparisons
will work.   You were actually comparing a number with an alphanumeric
display.

Oh, in real life, we like to use CoBOL's self documenting feature -
maintenance is a big, big part of CoBOL.  Variable names such as f001
are almost always counter to this.

Wolf Grossi wrote:

> Hi folks,
>     how do i compare to a field defined as PIC +9(3)???
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Wolf
```

#### ↳ Re: number check...over and out

- **From:** "Wolf Grossi" <wg@*NoEMail*PleaseRespond*NG*ID*magro-soft.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T21:27:24+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<969gvr$4kn$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at>`

```

command inside the COBOL source (for example CALL WINAPI "xxxx" ....).

If you have a free LIB is appreciated.

Thanks in advance.
Roberto

<a id="t-211e7d0e1b"></a>
## Thread: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic — 46 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T13:29:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
During the few years I have been following this group,
I have seen no one provide justification for the continued
availablility of PERFORM ... THRU in the COBOL language.

The use of PERFORM ... THRU should be made archaic.
It is a poor programming practice that leads to and causes
problems in maintaining source code.

Removing any item from the COBOL standard takes many
years. First, the item must be made archaic or obsolete in
the next revision of the standard. Second, if the item was
made archaic, then it must be made obsolete in some
subsequent revision. Third, the item is removed in the
following revision.

Making the item archaic, initially, is a more conservative
approach to its eventual removal and gives more time
for conversion of those programs which still use
PERFORM ... THRU.

Those who wish to have PERFORM ... THRU removed
from the language may request such removal by commenting
on the Final Committee Draft (FCD) for the draft COBOL
standard. Information on where to direct such comments
was provided to this group recently in the thread "Press
Release on COBOL Public Review".
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

```

FYI - this was previously discussed and "rejected" - but I think that *if*
it came in from one or more "real users" there *might* be support for doing
it.

I think it would be PARTICULARLY useful if this came in from some (any?
many?) national bodies OTHER than the US/ANSI.

P.S.  I would certainly support it.  If you do make this as a comment, I
suggest that you request putting *all* occurrences of "procedure-name-1 THRU
procedure-name-2" in the Archaic category.  This would include SORT/MERGE
procedures as well as PERFORM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<96agdi$qqf$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
You know... we have hundreds if not thousands of programs in
my shop (os/390) that use "PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT" along with "GO TO A-EXIT"
somewhere in the paragraph. If PERFORM THRU suddenly didn't work it is
really going to #@$$ off alot of programmers and managers. What's so
archaic about it anyway? There times in the real world when you just
need to get out of the paragraph and "going to the exit" is the only
way to do it, at least with the current mainframe compiler.

On the PC MicroFocus has an 'EXIT PARAGRAPH'. Is this feature included
included in the new standards? That would help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-12T23:30:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96agpg$gr7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <969btj$lrg$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <96agdi$qqf$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
In reverse order,

Yes, EXIT PARAGRAPH is in the draft of the next Standard (so should be
available in *all* standard conforming compilers - when/if such become
available.

Now for making something "archaic".

There are 3 "levels" of support for features that are *in* the (draft)
Standard:

1) "Fully supported now and forever" (no other classification)

2) "Obsolete" - fully supported in this "version" of the Standard - but will
come out when/if there is one AFTER this one (i.e. 201x).

3) "Archaic" - fully supported in this version - and "guaranteed" to also be
supported in the next Standard, but they really are "not nice" things to
do - and there are better ways of doing it.  So when/if programmers STOP
using them, then they will be made obsolete in some future Standard - and
will go away in the Standard *after* that.

   ***

Vendors are required to provide a "flagging" mechanism (that programmers can
turn on OR OFF) - for either OBSOLETE or ARCHAIC features.

Therefore, do *not* worry about THRU going away "soon" if it were made
archaic.  However, it would be possible for you to start a *new* shop
standard to move away from it - both in new development and maintenance -
and at some VERY distant time in the future, it may drop out of the Standard
(but even then vendors are allowed to keep it as an extension - as IBM has
already indicated that they will do with some features that were marked
"OBSOLETE" in the '85 Standard - and which are "dropped" in the draft
revision).
```

- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <969btj$lrg$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <96agdi$qqf$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <96agpg$gr7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3A88D7AE.7C858A3D@worldnet.att.net>`

news:3A88D7AE.7C858A3D@worldnet.att.net...
 <snip>
>
> Question:  Is ALTER archaic, obsolete, or fully supported, and in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

ALTER was designated as "OBSOLETE" in the '85 Standard - so it has been
dropped in the draft of this (2002-ish) Standard.  Arnold, as I know you use
IBM mainframe compilers, run with FLAGSTD(H,O) compiler option - and you
will get a warning message on the use of the ALTER statement.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T00:47:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96ai0k$14o1$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <969btj$lrg$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <96agdi$qqf$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
The draft COBOL standard has many of the EXIT
statements that are available in Micro Focus (MERANT)
compilers. This feature is also available with other
compilers.

PERFORM ... THRU ?-EXIT is archaic because the
draft COBOL standard has statements which eliminate
the need for PERFORM ... THRU ?-EXIT and
GO TO ?-EXIT.

If the syntax were made archaic, compilers would
still support the syntax. Mostly, making the syntax
archaic is a strong suggestion to use PERFORM
with the EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION syntax in
new programs.

It also serves as a strong suggestion to avoid
using PERFORM ... THRU with multiple paragraphs
or sections in new programs.
------------------
Rick Smith

Ron wrote in message
<96agdi$qqf$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>...

>my shop (os/390) that use "PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT" along with "GO TO A-EXIT"
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

> You know... we have hundreds if not thousands of programs in

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> included in the new standards? That would help.

It is in the new standard.  I don't think making it archaic would fly
otherwise.

Calling something archaic won't break code which uses it.  It is sort of like a
yellow light telling us that somewhere down the line will likely be a red light
- if you follow that road to the new compiler.

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
> During the few years I have been following this group,
> I have seen no one provide justification for the continued
> availablility of PERFORM ... THRU in the COBOL language.

When EXIT PARAGRAPH is available, I agree.  However, every other
procedural language with which I am familiar provides a way to exit
a code block.  Perhaps all the other language designers aren't as
perceptive as yourself, but I would hate to see the only means to do
that in COBOL being removed before another is available. :-)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-15T21:12:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>> During the few years I have been following this group,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>that in COBOL being removed before another is available. :-)

Me? ... A language designer?

I haven't thought about it in a while; but yes, the language
is coming up on its 6th anniversary and is actively used.
I have not written a program in it for about five years.

I wrote the LRM, the "compiler" (macros for an assembler),
and the loader. A consultant added the interpreter to the
existing control program.

There was a "review comment" two years ago; but after
considering the change, the "standard committee" (me)
declined. :-)

Removing PERFORM .. THRU is, first and foremost, an
issue of volume. If there is concensus for elimination of
PERFORM ... THRU, then the sooner the standard states
that, the better. If PERFORM ... THRU were made archaic
now, it will not be made obsolete until the volume drops
to a mere fraction of the existing count. Once obsolete,
it is removed on the next full revision. Since not all review
cycles result in full revisions of the standard, I expect that
PERFORM ... THRU would continue as part of the standard
for at least 15 and more likely 20 years.

Concensus determines the start.
Volume determines the end.

One factor that will affect volume, is the willingness of users
to upgrade their compilers.

All of this is my understanding or opinion, as appropriate,
within the context. :-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T10:09:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl> <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>
> Removing PERFORM .. THRU is, first and foremost, an
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Volume determines the end.

I very much disagree!  Let's say that in some hypothetical language
a particular construct is needed in only 5% of the programs, but it
is critical for that 5%.  Would you remove it, just because it isn't
needed 95% of the time?  Consider breathing masks in airplanes, or
home fire insurance.  Both are needed only a tiny fraction of the
time, but few would question their need. :-)

Personally, I use PERFORM...THRU.  But let me explain why, which is
the crux of the issue.  In the vast majority of my COBOL programming,
even without EXIT PARAGRAPH, I could and would be quite happy without
PERFORM...THRU.  But as I have stated here several times, there is
a particular type of program, frequently used by my clients, in which
there are long series (dozens) of field validation tests, the failure
of any of which should cause a paragraph exit.  In addition, there is
no logical point where one would be justified in breaking up the
paragraphs.  Also, my clients still use COBOL 74 because the vendor's
COBOL 85 compiler is much slower, so we do not even have END-IF, etc.
There just is no simpler, clearer or as efficient way to do this as
PERFORM...THRU / GOTO EXIT.  With newer technology, this particular
type of code will probably be abandoned by my clients, and/or EXIT
PARAGRAPH will be available.  In either case, I will happily abandon
PERFORM...THRU.  But even if 99% of all programmers wanted to get rid
of PERFORM...THRU, it would in no way lessen my client's need for it
right now.  In fact, we don't know when, or even if, the new draft
standard will ever be implemented.

The simple fact is that it has been determined over the years that
good structured programming requires an exit code block construct to
enable writing clean, clear code.  This is why (AFAIK) all structured
procedural languages provide one.  Because COBOL was designed before
these principles were realized, PERFORM...THRU/GOTO EXIT is the only
standard COBOL construct to provide this capability.  The number of
people who agree or disagree doesn't change this reality. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T17:15:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wedj6.148986$KP3.39660720@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl> <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net> <Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl>`

```
Would it be so terribly unclear and inefficient if the code had been
structured like this?

01  TEST-SW        PIC X VALUE "Y".
    88  TEST-OK                VALUE "Y".
    88  TEST-FAILED        VALUE "N".

TEST-ALL-FIELDS.
    SET TEST-OK    TO TRUE
    PERFORM TEST-FIELD-1.
    IF TEST-OK PERFORM TEST-FIELD-2.
    IF TEST-OK PERFORM TEST-FIELD-3.
    ...

Of course, any validation is required to SET TEST-FAILED TO TRUE if it
fails, and probably provide a message. Personally I prefer error codes and
message lookup. The declaration of TEST-SW gets a little more complicated
but it can handle severity better and avoids hard-coding messages.

- Karl


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
> >
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T14:16:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DUfj6.2219$466.146488@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl> <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net> <Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl> <wedj6.148986$KP3.39660720@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Of course, this would work.  But it would approximately double the
amount of code needed, and add hundreds of otherwise unnecessary tests,
and paragraphs.  I started a thread on this subject quite a while ago,
and several clever proposals were made to get around PERFORM...THRU/
GOTO EXIT, but all of them involved more code, were more complex or
suffered from some other deficiency when compared to a simple GOTO
EXIT, which is never ambiguous.  Which is, of course, why language
designers put exit code block constructs into languages in the first
place. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T07:21:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A912BCD.3B577FFE@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl> <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net> <Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl> <wedj6.148986$KP3.39660720@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```


"Oscar T. Grouch" wrote:

> Would it be so terribly unclear and inefficient if the code had been
> structured like this?

...

The best arguments for PERFORM THRU aren't about design.  You're right that it
isn't hard to design code which doesn't need exits.   The best arguments are
for maintenance where you want to exit some code, but don't want to
restructure/redesign it.   Without an exit command, we are left with either GO
TO 1000-THIS-PARAGRAPH-EXIT or switches (or restructuring the code).   For
this reason, I wouldn't support calling PERFORM THRU as obsolete without the
EXIT commands which will soon be available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T16:08:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96k53r$eb$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl> <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net> <Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
[...]
>> Concensus determines the start.
>> Volume determines the end.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>needed 95% of the time?

First, nothing will be removed if there is not a better
technique to do the same thing in the new standard.

Second, _in the context of removing an item from the
standard_, concensus occurs among the voting members
of the committee, not the community at large (though
concensus may be influenced by the community).

Third, my concern is not whether PERFORM ... THRU is
needed (I already know it is not). My concern is that I have
found some uses of PERFORM ... THRU to be unclear,
mistakable, and difficult to maintain. It is the possibility of
further abuse I want to eliminate.

>Personally, I use PERFORM...THRU.  But let me explain why, which is
[...]
>  But even if 99% of all programmers wanted to get rid
>of PERFORM...THRU, it would in no way lessen my client's need for it
>right now.  In fact, we don't know when, or even if, the new draft
>standard will ever be implemented.

There is no immediate change. Change is only required when
the syntax is made obsolete and then only if the client changes
to a compiler for which the syntax is not supported.

If the new standard is not adopted or never implemented, there
is no effect.

>The simple fact is that it has been determined over the years that
>good structured programming requires an exit code block construct to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>people who agree or disagree doesn't change this reality. :-)

PERFORM ... THRU / GO TO EXIT works in COBOL 74.

The END- teminators provide a similar ability in COBOL 85.

More options, including additional EXIT statements, are
availible in the draft COBOL standard.

I doubt that very many are aware that, several months ago,
WG4 voted on whether to make GO TO archaic. GO TO
will not be made archaic, yet! :-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T07:15:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A912A70.98DD1953@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl> <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net> <Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>  With newer technology, this particular
> type of code will probably be abandoned by my clients, and/or EXIT
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> right now.

But this will be available in the next version of CoBOL.   Making PERFORM
THRU archaic cannot be implemented before then, and probably can't be
implemented until after then.  Making it archaic also is just a flag
telling people that sometime in the future it could go away.  What would
be your guess on how long we will have PERFORM THRU after EXIT PARAGRAPH
is available?  20 years?  30?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T11:09:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Sqck6.39711$LB6.41990@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <Nvbi6.775$466.63206@news1.atl> <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net> <Egcj6.2053$466.142339@news1.atl> <3A912A70.98DD1953@brazee.net>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> telling people that sometime in the future it could go away.

I agree.  My argument was against the sentiments "PERFORM...THRU is
an obsolete construct that isn't needed now", and "the number of
programmers who want it should determine how long it stays in the
language".  For certain types of programming, a block exit statement
is necessary to avoid obfuscated, complex or inefficient code.  This
makes PERFORM...THRU necessary right now, for the language to be
complete.  Programmers who think differently are simply speaking
from experience that is limited in scope.  Even language designers
who refuse to put GOTO within their languages still provide block
exit statements.  They're not all fools, they're quite aware of the
need for them. :-)

> What would be your guess on how long we will have PERFORM THRU
> after EXIT PARAGRAPH is available?  20 years?  30?

Personally, I'll be happy if I live to see EXIT PARAGRAPH implemented
in a real standard. :-)

But I was arguing against a theoretical position.  I'm not concerned
that the construct will go away. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T16:31:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010216113115.24965.00000252@nso-fm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
In article <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net>, "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@aiservices.com> writes:

>
>Concensus determines the start.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am not sure of where my stance would be on this issue.
Personally I still prefer to use PERFORM THRU as it clearly 
defines the scope of what code you expect to have exercised.
Inline Performs have reduced the need for some of the out-of-line
coding, but there are instances where an out-of-line perform is 
acutally more clear than an inline perform.  Namely when the procedures
span more than a page of code or you get into nested if logic.

In maintenance coding, it is easier to identify where TO or NOT TO
put a new paragraph by observing where other procedures start and end.

Maybe this is part of 20+ years in structured development and application
maintenance,  but it's my opinion and I'm stickin' to it.  For now that is :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T12:25:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96jpcf$6u0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net> <20010216113115.24965.00000252@nso-fm.aol.com>`

```

"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:20010216113115.24965.00000252@nso-fm.aol.com...
> In article <96i42d$n51$1@news.hitter.net>, "Rick Smith"
> <ricksmith@aiservices.com> writes:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintenance,  but it's my opinion and I'm stickin' to it.  For now that is
:-)
>

Personally, I use none of the above.  I do not use inline, I do not use
goto, I do not use thru.  On occasion, I use the various "END" constructs,
but I far prefer to just use plain, ordinary paragraph names in a structured
manner.  In my opinion, 95% of readability lies in the choice of paragraph
names and data names.  Most of the other stuff just adds clutter/verbosity,
and is seldom even desirable.
```

#### ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-02-13T21:43:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> During the few years I have been following this group,
> I have seen no one provide justification for the continued
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> problems in maintaining source code.

This strikes me almost as a troll ...  I don't know how long Mr. Smith
has been using Cobol but there exists a generation of programmers who
have used PERFORM ... THRU and have no qualms about its use.    It is
simple, understandable and reliable.  It isn't true that "it is a poor
programming practice that leads to and causes problems in maintaining
source code"; the correct statement is "it is a practice that, badly
used, leads to and causes problems in maintaining source code".  (This
is, of course, the case with any programming method).  The suggestion
is, in fact, the GOTO controversy in disguise; those who have been
around long enough know that it has been shown that progrtams CAN be
written without GOTO's, but nobody has ever demonstrated
once-and-for-all that they MUST be written that way.  And, after having
had to deal with programs without labels, programs with pages and pages
of in-line code, to which I have had to apply a ruler to a listing to
find out where a particular IF or PERFORM ends - I insist that programs
WITH labels which clearly define the limits of a particular piece of
execution are infinitley easier to code, maintain, and understand than
programs without them! And this certainly includes PERFORM ... THRU.  If
it ain't broke, don't fix it!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T04:49:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010215234906.17631.00001123@ng-cm1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
>Rick Smith wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The standards would have to be changed for CICS as well to remove HANDLE
CONDITION.  While I prefer using response codes to check for a sucessful read
of a file for instance, a PERFORM THRU with a GO TO EXIT is required with the
HANDLE CONDITION which generates it's own GO TO DEPENDING ONs.  Without it one
will fall thru to the end of the program and possibly crash the system (been
there - done that - many many many moons ago).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T01:25:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96ikl1$41s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <20010215234906.17631.00001123@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
CICS is *not* Standard conforming "by definition" - check out the
DYNAM/NODYNAM compiler option - just for a starter
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-17T05:36:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8E0DAE.2D8654C9@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <20010215234906.17631.00001123@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama wrote:
> 
> The standards would have to be changed for CICS as well to remove HANDLE
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> there - done that - many many many moons ago).

HANDLE CONDITION is CICS, not COBOL.  The generated GO TO DEPENDING ON
is COBOL.

I have been programming in CICS for about 13 years now, and I have
only encountered two situations where HANDLE CONDITION needed to be
used.  Normally, I would just use NOHANDLE or RESP and check the
EIBRESP code after each CICS API call.

If you do an EXEC CICS ENQUEUE command, and you want your program to
take an alternate action when the enqueued resource is locked, you
have to use a HANDLE CONDITION for "Enqueue Busy".  I've never
actually needed to use that one, but it's possible.  Otherwise your
task would be suspended until the enqueued resource is free for you to
lock it.

I've also seen long-running, conversational tasks that must be
restarted if they abend.  For that we use a HANDLE CONDITION for
ABEND.  The ABEND paragraph will start a new instance of the task to
replace the current one that is being abnormally terminated.

In my opinion, most CICS programs can be coded without using HANDLE
CONDITION and the hidden GO TO's they generate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiammD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-17T07:16:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8E251E.F636A843@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <20010215234906.17631.00001123@ng-cm1.aol.com> <3A8E0DAE.2D8654C9@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> YukonMama wrote:

(snip)

> I have been programming in CICS for about 13 years now, and I have
> only encountered two situations where HANDLE CONDITION needed to be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> lock it.

But you don't have to (and best practice says you don't, IMHO) code
"ENQBUSY(paragraph_name)...". Your code the ENQ like so:

EXEC  CICS  ENQ
        RESOURCE(something_valuable)
        LENGTH     (LENGTH OF something_valuable)
        RESP           (WS-RESP)
        NOSUSPEND
END-EXEC

Check WS-RESP after the command and maintain your structure. IMHO coding "...HANDLE
ENQBUSY(bbbbb)" and the like commands is so 80's. Much better to tell the translator
you're a big boy or girl and you'll handle the exceptions.


>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> replace the current one that is being abnormally terminated.

You're stuck with the GO TO DEPENDING ON generated by the translator for EXEC CICS
HANDLE ABEND LABEL(xxxxx) END-EXEC, but it's not part of your source (the "GO TO
DEPENDING ON", that is). In this case you're not trying to maintain your structure
and continue processing, these paragraphs are terminating logic. The purpose of the
HANDLE ABEND logic is to determine if the task can be restarted and, if so, to do
so; otherwise, write a message, a WTO, and generally alert the world. I don't worry
about it as far as structure is concerned.

Do you really have conversational tasks? We have a lot of long running asynchronous
tasks, i.e., not attached to a terminal, and recovering/restarting after an abend is
critical; but we don't have any conversational tasks, e.g., CEMT.

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS HANDLE CONDITIONS (was [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T08:36:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8F8954.B7FB7255@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <20010215234906.17631.00001123@ng-cm1.aol.com> <3A8E0DAE.2D8654C9@worldnet.att.net> <3A8E251E.F636A843@optonline.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> you're a big boy or girl and you'll handle the exceptions.

We don't actually use the ENQBUSY handle condition.  When we wait for
an enqueued resource we are willing to wait forever.

The whole point of ENQBUSY is that, if the resource is locked and you
do NOT want to wait for it, there's a way to get out of the enqueue. 
But you have to use the ENQBUSY handle condition.  Without the ENQBUSY
handle condition, you will never get an EIBRESP telling you that your
task has been suspended.

The NOSUSPEND option looks new to me.  The original code was written
for CICS 1.6.1 in the late 1980's.  We're currently at CICS 4.1, and
planning to convert to Transaction Server 1.3 later this year.  

When did NOSUSPEND become available?
 
> 
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> LiamD

Yes, we have long-running, conversational tasks, and they are attached
to terminals.  We use them to service external network connections and
the tasks should run until the CICS region is recyled, which is
usually less often than once a month.  They're in an infinite loop
with an EXEC CICS RETRIEVE WAIT command at the top.  Originally they
used BTAM, than VTAM, and now TCP/IP.  With TCP/IP the real terminals
have been replaced with dummy terminals.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS HANDLE CONDITIONS (was [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiammD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T02:31:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A908567.E1850B6@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <20010215234906.17631.00001123@ng-cm1.aol.com> <3A8E0DAE.2D8654C9@worldnet.att.net> <3A8E251E.F636A843@optonline.net> <3A8F8954.B7FB7255@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> Liam Devlin wrote:
> >
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> task has been suspended.

I understand what ENQBUSY means, I was trying to point out that it's not necessary to code
a HANDLE CONDITION ENQBUSY(....) command, you can tell CICS not to suspend you by using
the NOSUSPEND operand in the ENQ command itself. You always get control after the ENQ and
you have to test for DFHRESP(NORMAL) (or ENQBUSY) and process accordingly. We use this
form to limit async transactions to just one instance of itself, i.e., the program issues
the ENQ and if it gets an ENQBUSY we know the transaction is already running, so this one
just issues a RETURN.

The other circumstance where we use it is for a resource we must have; but don't want to
get into a deadly embrace. I put this into a PERFORM VARYING SUB-SCRIPT ... loop until
ENQ-RESP = DFHRESP(NORMAL) or SUB-SCRIPT > +10. If ENQ-RESP isn't DFHRESP(NORMAL), I delay
a second before looping. Test after the loop to see which condition is true and process
accordingly. Generally, if I can't acquire the ENQ after x tries, crank out a message and
restart the transaction.

LiamD
```

```
>HANDLE CONDITION is CICS, not COBOL.  The generated GO TO DEPENDING ON
>is COBOL.

No arguement - I believe I said that in that it generated the GO TO statements.

>In my opinion, most CICS programs can be coded without using HANDLE
>CONDITION and the hidden GO TO's they generate.
>

thing has consequences in another.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-16T23:31:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
It was never my intent to disguise the relationship between
PERFORM ... THRU and GO TO. I continue to support and
defend GO TO. What I do not support is a paragraph
structure where GO TO is used to create pathogenic
connections among the paragraphs. It is these types of
connections that have given GO TO a bad name!

What effect would removal of PERFORM ... THRU have?

Most of the effect is to remove the idea from the mind
of the programmer. If a programmer understands that
PERFORM ... THRU is (will) not available, they will
structure their procedures using current techniques.
The closer production programs are to the methods
shown in "modern" books, the easier it is to understand
the procedure and the easier it will be for new
programmers to become productive.

It does not prevent using such a paragraph structure.
If a programmer simply cannot live without PERFORM ...
THRU, they could use a paragraph with a switch to
simulate.

       program-id. p-t.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       77 proc-1-switch pic 9 value 0.
       procedure division.
           perform proc-1-thru
           perform proc-1-thru
           perform proc-1-thru
           perform proc-1-thru
           stop run.
       proc-1-thru.
           if proc-1-switch = 0
               move 1 to proc-1-switch
               go to proc-1
           end-if
           move 0 to proc-1-switch.
       proc-1.
           go to proc-1-exit.
       proc-1a.
       proc-1-exit.
           go to proc-1-thru.

For multiple entry points with a single exit, change the
switch, store a value and use GO TO DEPENDING at
the exit.

I absolutely insist that it is better to update or rewrite
such procedures using current techniques.
------------------
Rick Smith

Peter Lacey wrote in message <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> It is a poor programming practice that leads to and causes
>> problems in maintaining source code.
>
[...]
>  The suggestion
>is, in fact, the GOTO controversy in disguise; those who have been
>around long enough know that it has been shown that progrtams CAN be
>written without GOTO's, but nobody has ever demonstrated
>once-and-for-all that they MUST be written that way.
[...]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-02-17T23:33:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> It was never my intent to disguise the relationship between
> PERFORM ... THRU and GO TO. I continue to support and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> connections that have given GO TO a bad name!

Well, I apologize for being huffy.  But now I don't understand your
objection.  My own method is:
- all performed paragraphs are labelled (for example)
    100-PRINT.
        ...
    109-EXIT.
        Exit.
(I use numeric prefixes for conveinience and ease of navigation).
- ALWAYS use PERFORM 100-PRINT THRU 109-X.
    No exceptions.  EVER.  Even if, as quite often happens, there are no
branches needed between 100- and 109-.
- NEVER use (Say) PERFORM 105-PRINT-2 thru 109-X.

Nothing can go wrong, it's easy to understand, and it makes it obvious
where code can be added if needed.  Pathogenic connections aren't
possible if you stick to these three rules.  And you certainly never
suffer from a missing period.



> What effect would removal of PERFORM ... THRU have?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>
> It does not prevent using such a paragraph structure.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Wouldn't this just loop?

>
> I absolutely insist that it is better to update or rewrite
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, that's a moving target; and consensus is hard to achieve.  I
remember some early Structured Progamming gurus who insisted that there
was one situation in which a GOTO HAD to be used.  Sic transit gloria!

Again, I'm not sure why you are so determined about this.  Your first
post said that you'd seen no justification for PERFORM THRU therefore
...  in the same way, I can see no justification for the EVALUATE verb,
since its functionality can be accomplished by other verbs (albeit not so
conveniently) ... so would you agree that it should be marked as
archaic?  (Not serious here, just trying to make a point).

Peter Lacey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T13:32:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96p4se$2omi$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
[See note, below, about deleting parts of this message
before responding.]

Peter Lacey wrote in message <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>suffer from a missing period.

Simple cases are easy to understand because of their
simplicity. Pathogenic connections are not possible if
rules preventing such connections are followed. See
below for an example which is not simple and, for which
the programmer followed his own rules!

It is very easy to argue that all such cases are
"programmer error," or as you previously expressed,
"... practice ..., badly used, ...". In analysing
PERFORM ... THRU, it occurred, to me, that THRU
may create problems. Specifically, because
programmers "think" at different levels.

PERFORM is atomic. It performs one and only one
procedure. PERFORM ... THRU is non-atomic.

A programmer could see THRU as a means for
implementing a multi-step procedure with each step
in its own paragraph. The body of some of these
paragraphs, or complete paragraphs, are conditional,
therefore the programmer uses GO TO, the easiest
means available, to transfer control to the next
paragraph in the sequence. This next paragraph in
the sequence may not be the following paragraph
or the exit! Pathogenic connections develop.

A programmer thinking at one level can create code
that, while clear to them, is not clear those who think
at other levels. Code written as non-atomic is more
difficult to maintain, in part, due to pathogenic
connections.

Use of THRU permits, even encourages, non-atomic
code. (Atomic use of THRU requires discipline through
simplicity and rules!)

Therefore, use of THRU "leads to and causes
problems in maintaining source code".

[...]
>
>Again, I'm not sure why you are so determined about this.  Your first
>post said that you'd seen no justification for PERFORM THRU
[...]

Actually, in my first post I said I had not seen anyone provide
justification for continued availability ... in the language.

In other words, there have been discussions concerning
PERFORM ... THRU and new syntax in the draft standard.

Many said, in various threads, they disliked and did not use
PERFORM ... THRU.

Some, such as Judson McClendon, explained why they used
PERFORM ... THRU in current programs and expressed their
willingness to stop using PERFORM ... THRU once the new
syntax is available, _for their use_.

No one, as I recall, provided any justification for using
PERFORM ... THRU once the new syntax was available,
unconditionally.

Therefore, I wrote:

"During the few years I have been following this group,
I have seen no one provide justification for the continued
availablility of PERFORM ... THRU in the COBOL language."

Mr. Miller, in his response, cited "clarifying closure" as
a reason for using PERFORM ... THRU and, presumably,
for continued use after the new syntax is available.
------------------
Rick Smith

[In any response, please delete everything below this line.]
-----------------------------------
The followng code has been modified from the original
to eliminate any copyright, license, or other potential legal
entanglements. This original code was replaced several
years ago and is no longer used in any active program.

Most use of 'statement' means a single statement
followed by a period, or in COBOL parlance, a
'sentence'. In some cases, the use of 'statement'
means the original used a conditional sentence
which had no effect on flow of control.

The code was performed from three places by the
equivalent:
           perform para-1 thru para-49.

       para-1.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           if condition GO TO para-3.
           if condition GO TO para-2.
           if condition GO TO para-2.
           if condition GO TO para-2.
           if condition GO TO para-2.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           statement.
           statement.
           perform.
           statement.
           statement.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-5.
           GO TO para-4.
       para-2.
           if condition GO TO para-3.
           statement.
           statement.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-7.
           GO TO para-5.
       para-3.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-5.
           statement.
           statement.
           perform.
           statement.
           perform thru.
           if condition GO TO para-5.
           statement.
       para-4.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           statement.
           if condition PERFORM para-27 THRU para-31.
           if condition GO TO para-7.
       para-5.
           if condition GO TO para-7.
           if condition GO TO para-6.
           if condition GO TO para-32.
           if condition GO TO para-32.
           if condition GO TO para-6.
           if condition GO TO para-32.
       para-6.
           if condition GO TO para-8.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           if condition GO TO para-8.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           GO TO para-8.
       para-7.
           if condition GO TO para-8.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           if condition GO TO para-8.
           if condition GO TO para-8.
           GO TO para-49.
       para-8.
           if condition GO TO para-49.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-9.
           if condition GO TO para-4.
           if condition GO TO para-9.
           if condition GO TO para-4.
           if condition GO TO para-4.
       para-9.
           statement.
           statement.
           perform.
           statement.
           statement.
           statement.
           if condition perform thru.
       para-10.
           if condition GO TO para-4.
           statement.
       para-11.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-12.
           if condition GO TO para-4.
           GO TO para-11.
       para-12.
           statement.
           perform thru.
           statement.
           statement.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-14.
           if condition GO TO para-14.
       para-13.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-16.
           if condition GO TO para-13.
           GO TO para-16.
       para-14.
           statement.
           if condition GO TO para-34.
           statement.
           statement.
           if condition perform thru.
           if condition GO TO para-14.
           if condition GO TO para-14.
           statement.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ GO TO in Structured Programming Theory (was: Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T13:35:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96p4sf$2omi$2@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

Peter Lacey wrote in message <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>>
[... 'tricky' code snipped]
>>
>> I absolutely insist that it is better to update or rewrite
>> such procedures using current techniques.
[...]
>
>Well, that's a moving target; and consensus is hard to achieve.  I
>remember some early Structured Progamming gurus who insisted that there
>was one situation in which a GOTO HAD to be used.  Sic transit gloria!

The only case, of which I am aware, occurs when it is not
possible for the program to continue in a defined manner.
A GO TO statement may be used to transfer control to a
label in the outermost block to end the program.

Nicholas Wirth, in some Pascal examples, used '99:' as
the label and 'go to 99' to transfer control to the end of
the outermost procedure.

For COBOL programs, STOP RUN, at a point other than
the end of the first procedure block, accompilishes the same
thing as the solitary use of GO TO in Structured Programming
theory. That is, it effectively (not actually) causes transfer of
control to the statement that ends the program normally.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ About the example (was: Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T13:36:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96p4sg$2omi$3@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
The example was given to demonstrate that elimination of
THRU, in the procedure division, is only a small impediment
to any who are determined to use GO TO in [their!] code.

I strongly advise against any such use!

Peter Lacey wrote in message <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> It does not prevent using such a paragraph structure.
>> If a programmer simply cannot live without PERFORM ...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>Wouldn't this just loop?

I presented this as a tested program that does, in fact,
work. There are, at least, two reasons one may think
otherwise. First, one may not understand how the switch
effectively controls the two possible paths in 'proc1-thru'.
Second, one might see 'proc-1-thru' as "falling thru" to
'proc-1' as a result of the path from the 'go to' in
'proc-1-exit'. While these are related, they result from
two different views of the code.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About the example (was: Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T14:46:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96pcbg$bri$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca> <96p4sg$2omi$3@news.hitter.net>`

```
I believe (but am not positive) that whether this sample loops or not is
"implementor defined".  The question is whether the "return from" the end of
Proc1-thru is or is not active when one does a GO TO that header while a
"perform is still active".  This is one of those "tricky" areas of the
Standard.

Rick - I believe from past posts, you have indicated that you have Micro
Focus/MERANT compilers.  If so, and if this is "tested" code - have you
compiled it with the 3 different "supported" values of PERFORM-TYPE? and if
so, does it "work" with all 3?

I believe (but again am not positive) that this will NOT loop in a
"stack-based" PERFORM environment but will loop in a "return bucket"
environment.  Both of these are viewed as "ANSI conforming" (and compilers
exist using both types) - but they give VERY different results when the same
"active" paragraph is both PERFORMed and GO TO'ed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About the example (was: Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T21:27:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96q0gm$o1d$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca> <96p4sg$2omi$3@news.hitter.net> <96pcbg$bri$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I used PERFORM-TYPE"MF", "OSVS", and "RM"; both
interpreted and compiled. The results were the same.
They all work, as described.

The difference, "MF" versus "OSVS" and "RM" behavior,
is with nested or overlapping performs. "MF" recognizes
only the exit of the last executed perform. "OSVS" and
"RM" recognize the return jump for any active perform;
that is, "the first reached."

The example does not "reach" the return jump until the
paragraph is entered the second time.

The method shown in the example should work correctly,
with the emulated compilers, as long as perform ranges
do not overlap.
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <96pcbg$bri$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>...
>I believe (but am not positive) that whether this sample loops or not is
>"implementor defined".  The question is whether the "return from" the end
of
>Proc1-thru is or is not active when one does a GO TO that header while a
>"perform is still active".  This is one of those "tricky" areas of the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>exist using both types) - but they give VERY different results when the
same
>"active" paragraph is both PERFORMed and GO TO'ed.
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Correction to Re: About the example

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T21:46:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96q1lg$p6s$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca> <96p4sg$2omi$3@news.hitter.net> <96pcbg$bri$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <96q0gm$o1d$1@news.hitter.net>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message <96q0gm$o1d$1@news.hitter.net>...
[...]
The following paragraph should read, in part,
... as long as the simulated perform ranges ...
>
>The method shown in the example should work correctly,
>with the emulated compilers, as long as perform ranges
>do not overlap.

The other thing "on my mind" was whether it might
loop in IBM COBOL F. I believe it would not. As I recall,
the PERFORM statement set the next paragraph address
to return and reaching the return address reset the next
paragraph address to fall through. I have the image of
two sets of L and ST at the end of each paragraph; but
it was so long ago, the image is faded.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-02-25T07:39:38+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A981C0A.4432248F@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca> <96p4sg$2omi$3@news.hitter.net>`

```
The problem with removing perform thru is that there is a huge
amount of code that uses it. I do not believe it will ever be
possible to remove it. 

Personally I do not like perform thru. I like to perform sections
and if I need to have exit-para at the end of the section I do.
This gives me a target for gotos to ecit the section. 

But many shops have it as the standard  - to use perform thru,
and not to use sections. It seems to be a matter of preference
and different people have different opinions. There is no
consensus to remove it and therefore it will not get removed.

Tim Joslinbg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mano@mano.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2001-02-25T20:53:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a996b77.11266770@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca> <96p4sg$2omi$3@news.hitter.net> <3A981C0A.4432248F@melbpc.org.au>`

```
On Sun, 25 Feb 2001 07:39:38 +1100, Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
wrote:

>The problem with removing perform thru is that there is a huge
>amount of code that uses it. I do not believe it will ever be
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Tim Joslinbg


I found the threads on this subject quite tedious but was rewarded to
find the EXIT PARAGRAPH/et al under consideration.  The topic took me
back to early morning coffees and thick stacks of listings, staring
down a host of programming tasks, debating with myself the best
approach, debating it with others and staring into a lot of jaundice
eyes.

The debate missed some points.  1) What is the program doing?  2) What
is the most effective to support?

Support groups often take ownership of code, for many years after, so
their view is probably the most important of all.  I think the support
people deserve the right to tell the developers how to structure a
program - with exceptions for solving particular problems.

Would also point out that section *numbers* were extremely usefull in
early PC COBOL, as we know they were in the early days of mainframe
hardware.   Interesting how they came back for a while.  So it's
important to note they were not removed from the language just because
segmenting fell out of favor.

I have vivid memories of debugging 12,000 line on-line programs.
Section made those programs exactly what the words says - sectioned.
A modular approach with source code at a higher level.  They served as
a kind of wrapper for pages of code that had to, for example, edit and
validate terminal forms input.  They also helped us move sections of
code around.  With a bit of revisionism, I think of it as a form of
containment.  Eventually I saw these 12,000 line PERFORM monsters
replaced with 12,000 line structured GO TO DEPENDING monsters.  They
still sectioned the programs because the need to contain code simply
didn't go away.  Can't say which is better, though the later loaded a
heck of a lot faster.  

To use GO TO DEPENDING with these programs required the invention of
control switches which took over the focus of the application.
Typically something like *STATE* as in a state driven program.  I
think some folks might call it *casing* a program.  You always had to
ask yourself, what is the current state?  What is the current mode?
What is the current keypress?   Not clear where I came from - not sure
where it's going.  Very easy to get lost.  In some ways it was easy to
modify, like adding functionality to an F-Key, but it was always hell
to support. 

Then one day I took over an EDI interface.  The program was written
using typical PERFORM structure.  The incoming flat file records were
highly optional.  Sometimes two number 005 record types, sometimes
none.  The PERFORM structure was just nuts trying to deal with *not*
doing anything - sometimes.  Rewrote it using go to depending.
Beautifully simple.

So we must always ask ourselves, what is the program doing, and choose
the best approach.   Good idea to keep options available and much
prefer a tool box brimming over when rolling up the sleeves.  Removing
program flow control contructs just because someone can demostrate
another way to do it in a given example lacks attention to the diverse
business problems programming must solve.

Perform thru was essential, even non conditional.  The whole idea was
to give the switches an elegant back row seat.  The point is, PERFORM
THRU is the basis for PERFORM THRU UNTIL, so debate (as someone
suggested many threads ago) is moot point.  It really made sense with
those online programs.  Granted, we can do things differently today
but the host of business problems to solve are still before us.

freewheelin.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T07:20:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9A662B.F337348C@brazee.net>`

```
Which means that if it was made archaic, it would stay at that level and
never become obsolete.  But programmers would know that this is from an
old style and probably question why.

Tim Josling wrote:

> The problem with removing perform thru is that there is a huge
> amount of code that uses it. I do not believe it will ever be
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tim Joslinbg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T20:30:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9030E6.7722785E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey wrote:
> 
> Well, I apologize for being huffy.  But now I don't understand your
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> - ALWAYS use PERFORM 100-PRINT THRU 109-X.

>     No exceptions.  EVER.  Even if, as quite often happens, there are no
> branches needed between 100- and 109-.
> - NEVER use (Say) PERFORM 105-PRINT-2 thru 109-X.

The idea of even _having_ a 105-Print-2 between 100-Print and 109-X
means that you would allow a GO TO within the procedure (ie spagetti
code) and not just to exit.  What other function could an included
paragraph name have it is not used as a go to target ?

You seem to be at some half-way house between spagetti and structured,
probaly with the disadvantages of both.

> Nothing can go wrong, 

The issue is not so much what you would get right, but what the next
programmer using that code would get wrong.  For example if they just
had a 'PERFORM 100-PRINT' (forgot to add the THRU) then the code may
well work correctly for quite some time until the routine eventually did
a GO TO 109-X, perhaps on some rare exception.  Because of the nature of
loss of control these can be _extremely_ hard to find, mainly because
the eventual notification of failure may be at some distance for some
completely spurious reason.

Of course if we had a grep or text search that would find every PERFORM
that _didn't_ have its correct THRU then the program could be shown to
be correct (in this respect).

> it's easy to understand, 

What is 'easy to understand' or not is entirely what one is used to.

> and it makes it obvious where code can be added if needed.  

Well it doesn't fall down if 'SECTION' is omitted.  But, if, as you say,
you may have several paragraph labels in a procedure then why would the
_next_ programmer think this is 'obvious' (there is no question about it
being obvious to you).

> Pathogenic connections aren't
> possible if you stick to these three rules.  And you certainly never
> suffer from a missing period.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T07:29:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A912DC7.197CBCC3@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey wrote:

>  My own method is:
> - all performed paragraphs are labelled (for example)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> where code can be added if needed.

Interesting.  I have always seen it as 100-PRINT-2 THRU 100-PRINT-2-EXIT or
100-EXIT. The same prefix is used for the paragraph and its exit.  I think
this makes it more clear that these are a unit.

Also.  Something ALWAYS can go wrong.   For instance, I have seen cut and
paste code with the wrong number for an EXIT paragraph.  This is one reason I
don't care for 100-EXIT.  It is too close to 106-EXIT.   Make it
100-PRINT-2-EXIT, which is clearly not 106-CALCULATE-SUM-EXIT.

Of course, I am an advocate for dropping drop through code altogether.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T21:27:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A918FB3.906CFAEF@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca> <3A912DC7.197CBCC3@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> 
> Interesting.  I have always seen it as 100-PRINT-2 THRU 100-PRINT-2-EXIT or
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Of course, I am an advocate for dropping drop through code altogether.

this is our shop standard (at least in the part of Boeing that I
currently support)

perform a-para-name thru a-para-name-end -- we have additional standards
such as avoid GOTO and use NOT logic as little as possible (one manager
forbade it altogether) - nested NOT logic can be tricky.

I will not be re writing any code if perform thru is made archaic, I
have no reason to mess with functional code on legacy systems .

	Susan A

I believe that Ice cream comes in many flavors to suit individual
preference and fully expect programmers to have at least as much
diversity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T22:27:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96srm8$8brq$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <3A89FEEE.7E778B0C@mb.sympatico.ca> <96kv21$uhl$1@news.hitter.net> <3A8F5E94.3EF03DA0@mb.sympatico.ca> <3A912DC7.197CBCC3@brazee.net>`

```
I also use the same prefix for a tightly related set of paragraphs involved
in a PERFORM THRU.
PERFORM M10-BUILD-COST-TABLE
         THRU M10-X.
.
.
M10-BUILD-COST-TABLE.
.
initializing stuff
.
M10-FETCH-NEXT-ROW.
.
fetch
put into table
go to M10-FETCH-NEXT-ROW
.
M10-CALC-PERCENT-FULL.
.
calculate and display
.
M10-X.
         EXIT.
I've omitted the test for end of rows, etc.
```

of a "goto exit" results in code that is anything BUT clear and easily
maintainable.

These are the same folks to would eliminate various language constructs and
communicate only in clicks, whistles, and grunts if the choice were theirs!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Std]; PERFORM ... THRU is Archaic

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-18T16:02:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96pgmq$s0e$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<969a8h$2pdo$1@news.hitter.net> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-uUdcXHYbd1YP@localhost> <3A90334E.B37F46B@da-zoo.com>`

```
In case it has been "lost" in this thread, I would NOT (and do not) support
the removal (or even the "deprecating") of GO TO para-EXIT without the
corresponding introduction of the (now defined in the draft COBOL Standard)
EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION/CYCLE syntax.

This "solves" the need for a GO TO EXIT "algorithm" - while avoiding even
the "accidental" introduction of GO TO anything other than an EXIT.  Once
you no longer need a "labeled" EXIT paragraph, I believe (as was stated at
the beginning of this thread) the need for the THRU option of PERFORM is
unnecessary UNLESS you want to "GO TO" something other than an exit - and I
don't think that anyone in this thread as said that those uses were
"structured" or even "not error-prone".
```

> 
> I need to know something about handling bar codes in COBOL. If somebody knows
> something, please let me know.

Barcodes are only a special way to code
numbers. So you can take any data structure
which fits.
Barcodes can be read with wands, e.g. HP or
DataLogic . This Wand translates usually the
Code in ASCII-Codes which can be treated by
programs. Wands are usually connected via a
serial Interface ( COM-Port) of the PC. They
will not be used in big Iron environments.

hth.

RFr
