## Thread: OO COBOL on the 390 — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO COBOL on the 390

- **From:** "Steve Dearth" <sdeart31@linuxworld.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T22:53:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
I'm curious, what experiences have people had with OO COBOL as implemented
in IBM COBOL for OS/390 and VM?  I'm concerned about the use of SOM, and its
future.  Any thought?
```

#### ↳ Re: OO COBOL on the 390

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-19T23:37:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A91AFF0.E6E804BD@home.com>`
- **References:** `<2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```


Steve Dearth wrote:

> I'm curious, what experiences have people had with OO COBOL as implemented
> in IBM COBOL for OS/390 and VM?  I'm concerned about the use of SOM, and its
> future.  Any thought?

I think Bill Klein will be able to give you a more accurate answer - but last
news was IBM was going through a 're-think' on OO.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: OO COBOL on the 390

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-20T18:11:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lryk6.195519$KP3.48680335@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
IBM seems to be backing SOAP which uses XMLschema to define the data and
http to as the transport. I would guess that they will either retire
SOM, or provide a SOM-like implementation for SOAP. Then again, they're also
big on Java and
EJB so maybe they'll go that way too.

They look to be trying to get most of their new business from Linux
deployment on all their platforms. Of course, this is purely conjecture on
my part. Any resemblance to reality is purely coincidental.

"Steve Dearth" <sdeart31@linuxworld.com> wrote in message
news:2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com...
> I'm curious, what experiences have people had with OO COBOL as implemented
> in IBM COBOL for OS/390 and VM?  I'm concerned about the use of SOM, and
its
> future.  Any thought?
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL on the 390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-20T13:09:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96ufbl$146$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <Lryk6.195519$KP3.48680335@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
The current official "line" on SOM is that they are removing it from being
"strategic" but keeping it as part of the COBOL "section" of LE - to support
applications that have used it already.  They definitely *continue* to
support OO COBOL - but it isn't clear what they will use to replace SOM (or
at least I haven't heard it yet).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL on the 390

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-20T13:24:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A92D26C.FDF0CBAC@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <Lryk6.195519$KP3.48680335@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <96ufbl$146$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

> The current official "line" on SOM is that they are removing it from being
> "strategic" but keeping it as part of the COBOL "section" of LE - to support
> applications that have used it already.  They definitely *continue* to
> support OO COBOL - but it isn't clear what they will use to replace SOM (or
> at least I haven't heard it yet).

What types of applications are currently using OO CoBOL on the 390?  Are these
file server applications, specialized applications, enterprise business
applications?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL on the 390

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-20T16:24:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96uqp3$tpc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <Lryk6.195519$KP3.48680335@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <96ufbl$146$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3A92D26C.FDF0CBAC@brazee.net>`

```
I can't answer this really.  I have tried over several years to "pin down"
my "usually reliable sources" at IBM about who/where OO COBOL is actually
being used by mainframe sites.  The best that I have been able to
UN-officially been able to determine is that there are more than 5 and less
than 100 (probably less than 20) "real" customers doing (significant?) OO
COBOL development on OS/390.  I believe that there have been previous posts
in comp.lang.cobol indicating that at least one shop is a "large bank" in
Germany.

I have the feeling (and this could be wrong) that this is a "chicken and
egg" situation.  IBM is NOT stressing or enhancing its OO COBOL support
because no (few?) customers are using it - and no customers are using it
because IBM doesn't seem to be "pushing" it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL on the 390

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T07:31:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A93D135.D4333A76@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<2uhk6.91130$k8.21168306@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <Lryk6.195519$KP3.48680335@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <96ufbl$146$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3A92D26C.FDF0CBAC@brazee.net> <96uqp3$tpc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I have the feeling (and this could be wrong) that this is a "chicken and
> egg" situation.  IBM is NOT stressing or enhancing its OO COBOL support
> because no (few?) customers are using it - and no customers are using it
> because IBM doesn't seem to be "pushing" it.

I wonder if there are a couple of other reasons:

1.  Few shops are starting up mainframe CoBOL environments from scratch.   If
you already have a large body of non-OO programs, the advantages of OO are less
obvious.

2.  Enterprise environments are finding integrated systems are more vulnerable
than when their components are more isolated.   They concentrate their
integration in the data side, purposefully keeping programs not integrated.

If either of these are important, they are important to the future of CoBOL,
whether or not we are interested in OO.   Are people starting up new CoBOL
mainframe shops from scratch?  If not, why not?   Are new enterprise shops
designed around data warehouses, data marts, and database tools, with
specialized data entry, data retrieval, and data communications tools?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL on the 390

- **From:** "Roland Schiradin" <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T00:16:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96utrq$coo$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de>`

```
C/C++ on OS/390 R10 stop support for SOM.
So Cobol might follow

Roland
```

- **Message-ID:** `<96v5j5$vam$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
> oh ok, sorry.  I wanted to open it to run it.
```

```
>

The following code-snippet might prove useful.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  SAMPLE.
      *
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
      *
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
      *
       DATA DIVISION.
      *
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *
       01  SYSTEM-PARMS.
           05  CMD-LINE                    PIC X(130)
                                           VALUE SPACES.
           05  RDRW-CODE                   PIC X(1)
                                           VALUE "N".
           05  EXIT-CODE                   PIC S9(4) BINARY
                                           VALUE ZEROES.
      *
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *
       Main-Para.
      *
           MOVE "%comspec% /c echo ABCDE" TO CMD-LINE.
           CALL "SYSTEM" USING CMD-LINE RDRW-CODE EXIT-CODE.

           IF EXIT-CODE NOT ZERO
             DISPLAY "Non-zero exit code."
             MOVE 1 TO RETURN-CODE
           END-IF

           EXIT PROGRAM.
```

### newbie: run-time error "statement sequence"

- **From:** "Sean Don" <seandon@loop.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T09:20:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jNSk6.378$qB.30671351@news.randori.com>`

```

Hi, I'm a student learning Fujitsu (ver. 4) COBOL for PC.

In trying to make my friends program identical to my working version --I
already succeeded in making an error free version of the program--; I kept
getting this  error, even after it gave me successful and correct output.

The program is merely reading in some data, and creating a report.
    Note: sysin and sysout are allocated appropriately for the PC.

"JMP0330I-U STATEMENT SEQUENCE ERROR. STM=READ. FILE=SYSIN. 'AT-END   '.
PGM=NAME ADR=00401281"


Is anybody familiar with the general causes of this error?  I can't figure
out what the problem is.

Thanks In Advance,
    Sean
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: newbie: run-time error "statement sequence"

- **From:** "Sean Don" <seandon@loop.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T10:49:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<15Uk6.508$qB.31719934@news.randori.com>`
- **References:** `<jNSk6.378$qB.30671351@news.randori.com> <MPG.14fdc89f5c30686198969d@news.naxs.com>`

```

Jeff Farkas <tazberjef@naxs.nospam.net> wrote in message
news:MPG.14fdc89f5c30686198969d@news.naxs.com...
> In article <jNSk6.378$qB.30671351@news.randori.com>, seandon@loop.com
> says...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > already succeeded in making an error free version of the program--; I
kept
> > getting this  error, even after it gave me successful and correct
output.
> >
> > The program is merely reading in some data, and creating a report.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff...


Yep.  That was it!  Thanks!
    As it turns out, my friend was writing in all upper case; while I was
trying to fix his code in lower case.

So, he had written the 'data-remains-switch' to look for "NO" --in upper
case--; while I was signalling the 'data-remains-switch' with a "no" --in
lower case.
    I fixed this, and now all is happy.


Thanks Again,
    Sean


> >
> >
> > Is anybody familiar with the general causes of this error?  I can't
figure
> > out what the problem is.
> >
> > Thanks In Advance,
> >     Sean
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbie: run-time error "statement sequence"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T02:07:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A947627.FE3EDB5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<jNSk6.378$qB.30671351@news.randori.com> <MPG.14fdc89f5c30686198969d@news.naxs.com> <15Uk6.508$qB.31719934@news.randori.com>`

```


Sean Don wrote:

>
> Yep.  That was it!  Thanks!
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sean,

Word of advice - consider the following :-

01 DataFlag.
    88 DataRemainsFlag        value "NO", "No", "no".
    88 NoDataRemains         value "YES", "Yes", "yes".

01 NewDataFlag.                    pic 9.
    88 ThereIsData                    value 1.
    88 ThereIsNoData                value 0.

You've already found the problems with 'DataFlag', whereas I consider the second
more succint and - DON'T MOVE values into the flag.

    set ThereIsData to true
    perform until ThereIsNoData
        read next record
            at end set ThereIsNoData to true
            not at end .... continue, still looping through the perform
                ( BUT.... it might also be if record-type <> what I want
                                    set ThereIsNoData to true etc.....)
        end-read
    End-perform

The checking is a variation on a theme but setting to ThereisNoData gets you out
of the routine without second-guessing is it "NO" or "no".

Whichever approach, use meaningful condition-names even if they do appear
verbose - IveGotALovelyBunchOfCoconuts, IhaventGotALovelyBunchofCoconuts -
subject of course to size of condition-names <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbie: run-time error "statement sequence"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T02:42:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x0%k6.18524$Sl.798769@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:



>Word of advice - consider the following :-
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    88 ThereIsNoData                value 0.

... and let it be a reminder that 'data' is a plural noun (singular
'datum') so that the names should be closer to 'NoDataRemain' and
'ThereAreNoData'.

DD
```

## Thread: [OT] Mainframe Software Prices? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [OT] Mainframe Software Prices?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T19:13:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<erUk6.18463$Sl.797118@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

So here's the deal... my present client is a goodly-sized shop, multiple
regions, CICS/DB2, 24/7 uptime requirements (that are almost met, at
times!)... but no FileAid for DB2.  I made a bit of conversation with one
of the Systems Guys and there would be two immediate responses:

1) Is there a Business Justification for it?

(this is answered by 'as much of one as there is for the regular FileAid
you got... it'd cut down on folks coding SPUFIs, too!')

2) How Much?

... and that's where my knowledge ends.  I am sure that if someone
broached the subject with the site's CA rep the response would be
(number)... and then, of course, the comeback would be 'Come *on*, we
already lease the sun and the stars from you guys... you can do us this
little asteroid for closer to (smaller number).'

... and so my question: any ideas what (number) and (smaller number) might
be?

Thanks much!

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A94709F.46573D3A@home.com>`

```
> So here's the deal... my present client is a goodly-sized shop, multiple
> regions, CICS/DB2, 24/7 uptime requirements  <snip>

> ... and so my question: any ideas what (number) and (smaller number) might
> be?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Kinda glad you asked that question, 'cos I'm considering another possibility.
Maybe, (still maybe at the moment), I might just switch from COBOL flat files
on a PC to DB2.

So not wishing to divert from the Doc's question - PC-wise any pitfalls,
what's it going to cost me to have DB2, (used with Net Express) and what would
it cost my end-user.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] PC DB2 Prices - WAS :Mainframe Software Prices?

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T02:50:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28%k6.209572$KP3.51673263@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<erUk6.18463$Sl.797118@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3A94709F.46573D3A@home.com>`

```
The last time I checked IBM UDB was available in a number of packages with
different pricing options. Your best bet is to visit their website to check
out the combinations then call IBM Direct to inquire about pricing.

If you choose Microsoft SQL Server then there's a package called Microsoft
Data Engine (MSDE) which is the equivalent of a free sample. It'll support 5
users and is available for distribution with some restrictions. I think you
require a licensed copy of a development product like VB. The idea is you
can distribute a 'lite' version of your product and Microsoft makes
additional money selling SQL Server when your clients scale up.

The database can do a lot of the work for you so you'll save some
maintenance effort in the long run because you can carve out tons of file
navigation code. You'll also get improved ad-hoc query capabilities and
direct support for MS Office products like Excel and Access with no effort
on your part.

Your third option is the Microsoft Jet database. It'll theoretically support
255 users although the practical limit is more like 20. It's highly biased
toward Visual Basic and the SQL datatypes are very non-standard but it too
is available for distribution.

The pitfalls are you need to become familiar with configuration and
maintenance issues for the database product. As well, you've added another
vendor to your mix of dependent products.


- Karl

news:3A94709F.46573D3A@home.com...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] PC DB2 Prices - WAS :Mainframe Software Prices?

- **From:** Paul Raulerson <praul@isot.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-23T22:17:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v9de9tgj74v6ogk4l60b1an0rtpu1tbn7e@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<erUk6.18463$Sl.797118@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3A94709F.46573D3A@home.com>`

```
Well, last time I looked, your development cost with DB/2 (exclusive
of any compilers you choose to use) is less than $200. For a Workgroup
Server, DB/2 is highly competitive, falling in there at something like
$699 before any discounts. 

DB/2 does get more expensive if you move up to multi platform 
enterprise wide servers, but if you are talking oh - 100 people on a 
server per location, DB/2 can't be beat. :) 

You also have some really nice options given eh Web with DB/2.

-Paul
```

## Thread: Wild Card Matches? — 11 msgs

### Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** "Dennis M. Matsudaira" <dmatsudaira@usa.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T21:51:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net>`

```
Has anyone coded a wild card match in COBOL (ala the "LIKE" keyword of a
SQL "SELECT WHERE" statement)? We have the COBOL code that works for a
wild card at the end of a string, but now the requirement is to include
embedded wild cards also.

Example:

User wants to see all part numbers that match his input:  312***59-*
(where '*' is any single character).

Part numbers from a sequential master file that match are:  312A2C59-1

312T2359-43

312UAB59-674-2
Note: Part numbers are 20 characters in length.


Thanks in advance for any help!!!

Dennis
```

#### ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T23:10:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9726or$ng7n8$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net>`

```

Dennis M. Matsudaira <dmatsudaira@usa.net> wrote in message
news:3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net...
> Has anyone coded a wild card match in COBOL (ala the "LIKE" keyword of a
> SQL "SELECT WHERE" statement)? We have the COBOL code that works for a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dennis

Allowing for a single character wildcard is fairly simple...

SET ITS-A-MATCH TO TRUE.
PERFORM VARYING ST FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL ST > 20
                                                                  OR
NOT-A-MATCH
   EVALUATE TRUE
       WHEN SELECT-FIELD (ST:1) = '*'
       WHEN SELECT-FIELD (ST:1) = PART-NUMBER (ST:1)
             CONTINUE
        WHEN OTHER
             SET NOT-A-MATCH TO TRUE
   END-EVALUATE
END-PERFORM.

That should get you on your way....

In your example, you actually allow the match against one or more
characters,
depending on whether it falls at the end or not.  While this works, I would
suggest
that you choose one or the other, or allow for another wildcard character.
IE-
'%' = single character wildcard, '*' = multiple character wildcard

Allowing for imbedded multi-character wildcard is a bit more difficult, but
still very
possible...
```

#### ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T12:30:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A95064B.3C02E868@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net>`

```
Dennis M. Matsudaira wrote:
> 
> Has anyone coded a wild card match in COBOL (ala the "LIKE" keyword of a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> (where '*' is any single character).

Yes, I've done that.  Just write a routine to perform that compares two
strings character by character and allows '*' to mean 'no check' and
returns a flag that indicates match or fail.  If the product code allows
alpha then you may also want to compare upper or lower as equivalent.

If you are reading through the file in key sequence you will also want
to extract the leading characters and length so that the file can be
started and you can detect when no more reads are necessary (ie when
product-key(1:lead-len) not = compare-string(1:lead-len), in this case
lead-len should be 3).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** "Dennis M. Matsudaira" <dmatsudaira@usa.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T13:40:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9516BD.89B535DF@usa.net>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net> <3A95064B.3C02E868@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I was headed in the direction of a byte by byte comparison and thought that
perhaps someone would have thought of an easier way. I think I'll throw in a
front-end INSPECT
to find the first '*' and use the characters before it as a "key" to do the
matching. That should save some iterations of the byte by byte compare loop.

Richard Plinston wrote:

> Dennis M. Matsudaira wrote:
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> lead-len should be 3).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T16:39:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<974141$1h8$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net> <3A95064B.3C02E868@Azonic.co.nz> <3A9516BD.89B535DF@usa.net>`

```
You could save more iterations with more "up front"
preparation. Consider the following:

       prepare-pattern.
           move 0 to first-group-length
           move 1 to second-group-start
           inspect user-pattern
               replacing all space by "*"
           unstring user-pattern
             delimited by all "*"
             into start-key
             count first-group-length
             pointer second-group-start
           end-unstring.

Assuming the part numbers do not have spaces, the
above uses UNSTRING to extract the leading characters,
provides a count (not really needed) of the number of
significant characters in the first group, and points to the
start of the second group.

       prepare-keys.
           inspect start-key replacing all space by low-value
           move start-key to end-key
           inspect end-key replacing all low-value by high-value.

The the above code sets the lowest and highest keys for
comparison with the record key. Any value greater or equal
to start-key and less or equal to end-key is selected for
matching.

       pattern-match.
           set patterns-match to true
           perform varying x
             from second-group-start by 1
             until x > 20 or patterns-do-not-match
               evaluate true
                 when user-pattern (x:1) = "*"
                 when user-pattern (x:1) = work-pattern (x:1)
                   continue
                 when other
                   set patterns-do-not-match to true
               end-evaluate
           end-perform.

Notice the in-line perform begins from second-group-start
saving comparison against all leading positions. If user-pattern
has only a leading group, second-group-start will be 21 causing
the perform to terminate with no comparisons. Otherwise, the
character-by-character comparison will begin with the second
group of significant characters.
------------------
Rick Smith

>I was headed in the direction of a byte by byte comparison and thought that
>perhaps someone would have thought of an easier way. I think I'll throw in
a
>front-end INSPECT
>to find the first '*' and use the characters before it as a "key" to do the
>matching. That should save some iterations of the byte by byte compare
loop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T20:27:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<974hjo$nk89o$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net> <3A95064B.3C02E868@Azonic.co.nz> <3A9516BD.89B535DF@usa.net>`

```
If you are willing to sacrifice some efficiency, something like this might
work:

**  zero out space count (inspect won't do it)
    move zero to space-cnt.
**  count trailing spaces (allows imbedded spaces in pattern)
    inspect function reverse (select-field) tallying space-cnt for leading
spaces.
** compute the length of the pattern as the max length minus the trailing
spaces
    compute len = <<length of select field>> - space-cnt.
**  use hex 01 as a delimiter to mark the end of field
    move x'01' to select-field ((len + 1):1).
**  set both pointers to the beginning of the fields
    move 1 to sel-ptr, field-ptr.
**  assume match until proven otherwise
   set its-a-match to true.
** check until the entire pattern is used, or we run into spaces (assuming
spaces delimit the pattern)
   perform until (sel-ptr > len) or not-a-match
**  zero out the count - unstring won't do it for you
      move zero to del-size
**  set the delimiter to spaces (if case we hit end of field)
      move spaces to sel-delimiter
**  unstring the pattern delimited by the wildcard ('*') or hex 01 (hex 5c
=*)
**  keep non-wildcard (used in second unstring), the length, and delimiter
actually used
      unstring select-field delimited by x'5c01' or  '*' or  x'01'
         into match-delimiter count in del-size delimiter in sel-delimiter
         with pointer sel-ptr
      end-unstring
**  if there is nothing between (or before or after) the wildcard, no need
to look for it
      if del-size > zero
**  put high-values in the delimiter so we can tell if we found the
characters or not
         move high-values to check-delimiter
**  use unstring to look for the non-wildcard characters in the actual field
we want to match
**  keep the delimiter so we can see if we found it or not
         unstring match-field delimited by match-delimiter (1:del-size)
            into dummy-field delimiter in check-delimiter
            with pointer field-ptr
         end-unstring
**  if the delimiter equals what we were looking for, we found it,
otherwise, we have no match
         if check-delimiter (1:del-size) not = match-delimiter (1:del-size)
            set not-a-match to true
        end-if
     end-if
**  if the delimiter from the pattern is spaces, we have reached the end of
the area we want to check
     evaluate true
** end of pattern is a wildcard - it's good
        when sel-delimiter = x'5c01'
           move len to se-ptr
** end of pattern was not a wildcard - match field must be blank from now on
        when sel-delimiter (1:1) = x'01'
           if match-field (field-ptr:) not = spaces
              set not-a-match to true
           else
              move len to sel-ptr
           end-if
     end-evaluate
   end-perform.

   if its-a-match
          .....
   end-if.


I haven't tested this, but it should work to allow your wildcard character
to be one or more characters.
Anything starting with '**' is a comment, not actual code.  Sorry if it is
hard to read....

This routine *should* allow you to use a pattern (select-field) that
includes '*' as wildcard (even with imbedded spaces) and assumes that you
don't want to match the trailing spaces.  It actually looks for the
non-wildcard portions in the match-field, ensuring that they are in the
match, in the same order as the pattern has them.  If your pattern ends in a
wildcard, and the routine has found everything else, it assumes a match.  If
it does not end in a wildcard, then it makes sure that the rest of
match-field is spaces, otherwise, it is not a match.

Just as a note, hex '5c' is the '*' on a mainframe.  I'm not sure about the
PC.

Well, I'll go ahead and don my flame-proof suit now.  Feel free to try it
and tear it apart.  I'll try to get a chance to actually try this code
tomorrow at work.  Have fun....

the

loop.

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Has anyone coded a wild card match in COBOL (ala the "LIKE" keyword of

> > > SQL "SELECT WHERE" statement)? We have the COBOL code that works for a
> > > wild card at the end of a string, but now the requirement is to

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Dennis M. Matsudaira" <dmatsudaira@usa.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-23T11:49:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A964E33.CA2D516D@usa.net>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net> <3A95064B.3C02E868@Azonic.co.nz> <3A9516BD.89B535DF@usa.net> <974hjo$nk89o$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`

```
This is great! Actually, the code does more than is required. The tech. project
leader told the user that an '*' can represent only 1 character of the part
number. The exception is if the '*' is at the end. We have a program that
handles the '*' at the end, so the spec. for this new program is to leave the
trailing '*' in the input, pattern match any imbedded '*'s  in the input and
replace with matching part numbers from the part file and write out a new input
file to pass on to the existing program. I think I'm going to give the code
presented here a try. If it works, I'll propose that the new program replace the
existing one, because it'll have more capability. Its' a fairly safe bet that
the user is going to push for to allow his '*' to match any number of
characters... "I don't want to have to key so many '*'s". We'll be in a position
to accept his requirement and the take a week off ;-}

Thank you all for your time and ideas. You've been most helpful!

Buck wrote:

> If you are willing to sacrifice some efficiency, something like this might
> work:
…[128 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
oo  )-:

Let me know how it works for you....

Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** "Peter B" <Compulaat@tip.nl>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T23:27:53
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9742sq$t1f$1@nereid.worldonline.nl>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net>`

```
Or try this :

03 WS-PARTNR            PIC X(12)
03 REDEF-PARTNR.
      05 A-MATCH            PIC X(03)
      05 B-MATCH            PIC X(03)
      05 C-MATCH            PIC X(03)
      05 D-MATCH            PIC X(03)

MOVE FILE-PARTNR TO WS-PARTNR.

IF      A-MATCH EQUAL input_a
AND C-MATCH EQUAL input_c  THEN PRINT
ELSE READ-NEXT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Wild Card Matches?

- **From:** "Peter B" <Compulaat@tip.nl>
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T22:08:48
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97efo7$f1i$1@nereid.worldonline.nl>`
- **References:** `<3A943878.F03DDD9A@usa.net> <9742sq$t1f$1@nereid.worldonline.nl> <3A966C28.82293D16@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I was just giving a tip...
Of course there are many ways to do the job. Just play a little with the
redefines.

03 WS-PARTNR            PIC X(12)
 03 REDEF-PARTNR    REDEFINES WS-PARTNR
                                         PIC X(01) OCCURS 12.

MOVE FILE-PARTNR TO WS-PARTNR.

IF input-A EQUALS REDEF-PARTNR (1) OR
    input-B EQUALS REDEF-PARTNR (2) a.s.o.
```

<a id="t-b24ddf9c87"></a>
## Thread: Easytrieve alternative — 4 msgs

### Easytrieve alternative

- **From:** John Matheson <john_matheson@acpe.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T15:28:08-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A945D17.26E54BED@acpe.state.ak.us>`

```
Can anyone recommend an alternative to CA-Easytrieve as a report writer
on an RS-6000 using COBOL and VSAM files? I have to make a procurement
for our mainframe guys that are used to using Easytrieve on our S390. CA
wants over $30,000 for the privilege of using their product on a little
2 user RISC development box. Sorry if this the wrong forum but I know
nothing about UNIX and I see some Easytrieve folks pass through here
from time to time.
Thanks
john_matheson@acpe.state.ak.us
```

#### ↳ Re: Easytrieve alternative

- **From:** Jeff Baynard <jeffbaynard@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T02:14:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6B9E052.7A7%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3A945D17.26E54BED@acpe.state.ak.us>`

```
Dunno about the AIX environment, but on OS390 we also have a product called
QuikJob.  It is basically the same as Easytrieve (or is that Vision:Report
or something), but I don't know if it is available for AIX.  Have you ever
thought about using Perl?

Jeff
```

#### ↳ [OT] Re: Easytrieve alternative

- **From:** "Anthony Borla" <ajborla@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T16:30:14+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ci1l6.713$v5.2736@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<3A945D17.26E54BED@acpe.state.ak.us>`

```
"John Matheson" <john_matheson@acpe.state.ak.us> wrote in message
news:3A945D17.26E54BED@acpe.state.ak.us...
> Can anyone recommend an alternative to CA-Easytrieve as a report writer
> on an RS-6000 using COBOL and VSAM files? I have to make a procurement
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The standard *NIX utility for file manipulation, which means that it can
also act as a 'report writer' is 'awk'. It is part of any standard *NIX
installation, no additional cost or license fees applicable; I would assume
AIX would also offer it on these terms.

The 'awk' utility is *very* easy to use. It even bears a slight similarity
to Easytrieve in that it automatically loops on each record of the input
file. A typical 'awk' program has the form:

    /* Startup */
    BEGIN {
    ...
    }

    /* Main loop */
    {
    ...
    }

    /* Cleanup */
    END {
    ...
    }

While 'awk' is designed to work primarily with 'delimited' files, it is
able, through its 'substr' function, to read fixed-length record files as
well. I often use it for prototyping, that is, to code preliminary versions
of reports that will later be coded up in COBOL.

Finally, while 'awk' offers may features, it is, as are many such utilites,
quite resource hungry. However this is often a small price to pay for
getting a job done quickly.

I hope this helps.
```

---

## Thread: MERANT - Press Release on 3rd Quarter Results - and COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MERANT - Press Release on 3rd Quarter Results - and COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-21T20:14:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<971sm6$op7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I thought that this might interest the readers of this note - from today's
(February 21, 2001) MERANT Press Release,

"... MERANT's Egility e-business software products revenue grew 18% at
constant currency exchange rates and made up 59% of MERANT's total revenues
for the quarter. Revenue from Cobol solutions, which represented 38% of our
revenue, declined 18% at constant currency exchange rates due to the
expected decline in demand for Cobol and mainframe-related products. ...

Forward-Looking Statements  ...
 Future results will be difficult to predict as MERANT transforms its
business strategy to provide e-business solutions and away from certain of
its past primary markets, including the market for Cobol and
mainframe-related products. ..."
```

#### ↳ Re: MERANT - Press Release on 3rd Quarter Results - and COBOL

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T10:50:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lRum6.13$H61.191@client>`
- **References:** `<971sm6$op7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I guess we could look at this a number of ways:
1. The market for "pure play" COBOL products is decreasing.
2. The market for Legacy Renewal products (i.e. web enablement) is
increasing.
3. Merant is getting some serious competition in the COBOL market and it's
starting to hurt.
4. Merant is refocusing their business from selling Compilers to selling
EBusiness solutions.

From what I've seen, number 4 is the most accurate although I think its
probably a mistake.  It's a strategy they started during the e-boom and now
that we are in an e-crash it may not pay off.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MERANT - Press Release on 3rd Quarter Results - and COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-27T03:19:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9B1EAA.42665846@home.com>`
- **References:** `<971sm6$op7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <lRum6.13$H61.191@client>`

```

Editor wrote:

> I guess we could look at this a number of ways:
> 1. The market for "pure play" COBOL products is decreasing.

Yes. Decrease in 'techs' teaching COBOL. I've ascertained  that it's still going
strong in Texas, (Thane Hubbell). Against that it has been dropped in some
institutes in Australia, (Ken Foskey); will be dropped in Limerick, Eire, in two
years, (Michael Sheehan). With possible exception of one private school here in
Calgary, (which I haven't checked out yet), the last 'tech' that was teaching
COBOL dropped it three years ago, "With no gnashing of teeth from the commercial
community". Will Price of objectz.com did an 'introductory' on OO in Quebec -
now that institute no longer teaches COBOL.

If it isn't taught, how do students learn it, and where is it going to be used ?

(Meanwhile of course, the folks here in the clc group, make the occasional bitch
about what COBOL 'should be doing' and still keep pecking away like ostriches
looking for grubs in the sand. Perhaps the older ones are just waiting for
retirement and don't care).

> 2. The market for Legacy Renewal products (i.e. web enablement) is
> increasing.

Yes. The two prime OO vendors (Fujitsu and Merant) are addressing this - but
without a firm COBOL standard as to where we are going - J4 target date of
December 2002 ????. (Please don't get me going about J4 and WG4 !)

>
> 3. Merant is getting some serious competition in the COBOL market and it's
> starting to hurt.

Yes and No. Not serious in the sense that their product is lousy, which it
ISN'T  - BUT - on pricing definitely. They, Micro Focus only have COBOL to fall
back on, whereas the other half of the Merant equation is interested in selling
'tools'. From the 'rogues list' of directors' photos - only one shows up as
ex-Micro Focus. A lady - and the reason she's there, the caption said something
to the effect , "she was responsible for handling the Micro Focus side of the
merger" - so was that a big 'thank you' from the opposition ? (It seems like an
echo of the Liant/Ryan McFarland story).

Meanwhile, if by 'serious competition' you mean Fujitsu - even if the latter
dropped COBOL they are still HUGE.

>
> 4. Merant is refocusing their business from selling Compilers to selling
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that we are in an e-crash it may not pay off.

As an 'interested' observer, (I use Net Express), Your 4 and comments are
accurate. There will in all probability be a market for e-commerce where the
site has a 'trustworthy' name - e.g. Sears catalogue sales. But like me,
probably you wouldn't buy a car/auto without first kicking the tires/tyres.
Against this, Amazon.com has a real ways to go to start showing ANY profit. Here
in Canada, Chapters (big box bookstores) has been taken over by Indigo. Chapters
on-line (a separate entity) is looking sick - you and me both, want to browse
through books before we buy them

Jimmy, Calgary AB.

> Buddy Ray
> Editor
> About Legacy Coding
> www.aboutlegacycoding.com
```

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL Calling another program — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL Calling another program

- **From:** James Walker Belgium NV <r20136@glo.be>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T13:11:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A950202.7221D121@glo.be>`

```
Hello,
From within my Cobol program, I can call another Cobol subprogram using
the Call statement.  This works well.
But how can I call another compiled Cobol program from within my main
program.  The others program is a batchprogramma reading a configuration
file.  Since I want to use it in all my other program's it is compiled
and build as an individual EXE file.
When I do a call to that file the build of my mainprogram is always
aborted on an error.
I'm using FSC V5.
Does anyone know the solution ? Thanks.
Frank
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL Calling another program

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T13:50:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A951954.BA72B84@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3A950202.7221D121@glo.be>`

```
From Fujitsu COBOL 97 you can start an EXE program by creating a windows
process.  There is an example named "CALLEXE" on www.adtools.com under
"V4 and V5 downloads".

One possible problem with this approach is that the new process is
independent of the calling program, which continues to execute and does
not wait for the CALLED program to terminate.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL Calling another program

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-23T13:40:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WKtl6.53$Q55.43626@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3A950202.7221D121@glo.be>`

```
If you are calling this EXE from many programs, why don't you re-engineer it
as a DLL so it runs in the same process space as the call-er and you can
share data with it?
```

## Thread: Fujitsu v3 & console screen/keyboard I/O — 12 msgs

### Fujitsu v3 & console screen/keyboard I/O

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T16:08:50
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<973df5$crd$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I'm trying to convert my MF WB v2-4(10 years old) code to Fujitsu v3.
I stopped using the accept/display commands a few years ago in favour
of my own sub programs which use calls to the MF rts to read/write to/
from the screen. I thought I could replace these with the calls to
CBL_READ_SCR_CHATTRS / CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHATTRS but,
these are not included within the F3BICBLR.LIB as stated within the
Fujitsu doc's. How can I access these routines within Fujitsu's product ?

Anyone know where the latest cobol standards can be found ? I was
amazed to find that split-keys were not supported by Fujitsu's v3 product.
Hope support for split-keys has been added in later versions.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu v3 & console screen/keyboard I/O

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T12:24:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<973lgd$n2a$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<973df5$crd$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Split keys are a part of the draft Standard (but with slightly different
syntax than Micro Focus used).

Have you checked the www.adtools.com download section?  I think that they
have some/all of the cbl_ routines there - but I don't know if they work
with V3 or not.

As far as getting the Standard on line - are you interested in the draft of
the NEXT COBOL Standard?  (That draft is currently out for Public Review) -
or are you interested in the '85/'89/'93 existing Standard?  Those are
available - but not free.
```

```
What's a split-keys?

Consider how the following ALTERNATE KEY's are defined :
(sorry about formatting, I just pasted the text from some copy
files).

     SELECT fl-cust    ASSIGN fh-fname
       ORGANIZATION    IS INDEXED
       ACCESS          IS DYNAMIC
       LOCK MODE       IS MANUAL
       RECORD KEY      IS cust-key-flds
       ALTERNATE KEY   IS cust-acnt-indx
                          = cust-acnt-key
                            cust-key-flds
       ALTERNATE KEY   IS cust-shrt-name-indx
                          = cust-shrt-name
                            cust-key-flds
       ALTERNATE KEY   IS cust-tel-indx
                          = cust-tel-key
                            cust-key-flds
       FILE STATUS     IS fh-gstat.

....

   FD  fl-cust.
   01  cust-fd.
     03  cust-rec.
       05  cust-key-flds.
         07  cust-key              PIC  X(15).
       05  cust-data-flds.
         07  cust-tel-key          PIC  X(14).
         07  cust-acnt-key         PIC  X(15).
         07  cust-sper-key         PIC  X(02).
         07  cust-terr-key         PIC  X(02).
         07  cust-nmnl-key         PIC  X(15).
         07  cust-cdpt-key         PIC  X(02).
         07  cust-qanl-grp.
           09  cust-qan1-key       PIC  X(02).
           09  cust-qan2-key       PIC  X(02).
           09  cust-qan3-key       PIC  X(02).
           09  cust-qan4-key       PIC  X(02).
         07  cust-addr-grp.
           09  cust-addr           PIC  X(30)          OCCURS 5.
           09  cust-addr-pcode     PIC  X(10).
         07  REDEFINES cust-addr-grp.
           09  cust-shrt-name      PIC  X(15).
           09                      PIC  X(145).
         07  cust-cntc-name.
           09  cust-cntc-sname     PIC  X(14).
           09  cust-cntc-fname     PIC  X(14).
           09  cust-cntc-title     PIC  X(05).
         07  cust-tel-num          PIC  X(14).
         07  cust-fax-num          PIC  X(14).
         07  cust-comment-grp.
           09  cust-comment        PIC  X(45)          OCCURS 2.
         07  cust-quot-ctrl-flg    PIC  X(01).
           88  cust-quot-ctrl-okay         VALUE "O".
           88  cust-quot-ctrl-warn         VALUE "W".
           88  cust-quot-ctrl-stop         VALUE "S".
           88  cust-quot-ctrl-vld          VALUE "O" "S" "W".
         07  cust-jobs-ctrl-flg    PIC  X(01).
           88  cust-jobs-ctrl-okay         VALUE "O".
           88  cust-jobs-ctrl-warn         VALUE "W".
           88  cust-jobs-ctrl-stop         VALUE "S".
           88  cust-jobs-ctrl-vld          VALUE "O" "S" "W".
         07  cust-acnt-info-grp.
           09  cust-stlmnt-dy      PIC  9(04)          COMP.
           09  cust-stlmnt-pc      PIC  9(02)V9(02)    COMP-5.
           09  cust-stlmnt-pc-mult REDEFINES cust-stlmnt-pc
                                   PIC  V9(04)         COMP-5.
           09  cust-markup-pc      PIC  9(02)V9(02)    COMP-5.
           09  cust-markup-pc-mult REDEFINES cust-markup-pc
                                   PIC  V9(04)         COMP-5.
           09  cust-comisn-pc      PIC  9(02)V9(02)    COMP-5.
           09  cust-comisn-pc-mult REDEFINES cust-comisn-pc
                                   PIC  9(04)          COMP-5.

The alternate key data names are defined within the Select statement. A
split-
key allows a key(primary/alternate) to be defined on many data items (which
do not have to be consecutive) within the record.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu v3 & console screen/keyboard I/O

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-23T08:00:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A961888.B38388DE@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> I'm trying to convert my MF WB v2-4(10 years old) code to Fujitsu v3.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hope support for split-keys has been added in later versions.

One of the nice things about the MF language manual is that it specifies
what is part of the Cobol standard and what is an extension.  Anything
that is non-standard may not be in other vendors' Cobols, or may be
implemented in an entirely different way.

When you say 'latest' Cobol standards do you mean 1985/89 (which you can
work out from the MF manual) or 20xx which aren't set in concrete yet ?
```

- **References:** `<973df5$crd$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3A961888.B38388DE@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Was the last Cobol standard set back in 1989 ?

I need to know what standard is available within each compiler
and what defines that standard.

   'The reason ENTRY will not become part of the COBOL
   standard is because it is considered "unstructured."
   This does not mean ENTRY is not the best way to solve
   some problems' posted by Rick Smith (04/02/2001).

I adore the entry statement when writing utility routines as they
reduce the amount of code required therefore placing less of
an overhead on the both the cpu and my fingers. Who decides
what goes in and what comes out ? Can we as Cobol coders
contribute ?

news:3A961888.B38388DE@Azonic.co.nz...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Standards (was: Fujitsu v3 & console screen/keyboard I/O

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-23T13:22:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<976d56$1nn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<973df5$crd$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3A961888.B38388DE@Azonic.co.nz> <974mba$cgs$2@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
There are slightly different answers for ANSI and ISO to these question.  I
noticed that your email ID is ".uk" - so I'll give both answers (to as much
as I can).

1) The last full revision was in 1985 (ANSI = X3.23-1985; ISO = 1989:1985)

2) There have been two (not just one) Addenda since then;
 the Intrinsic Function Addendum - ANSI X3.23a-1989; ISO 1989 amd/1 1993
    (there was a bureaucratic delay in getting the ISO version out)
 the Correction Amendment (Addendum) - ANSI X3.23b-1993; ISO 1989 amd/2
1994)
    (the "1989" in all of the ISO numbers identifies it as "COBOL" and is
NOT a year)

3) The current "full revision" is presently out for both ANSI and ISO Public
Reviews.  It is still THEORETICALLY possible to ask for things to be added
or deleted - but unlikely to get either.  If you check the comp.lang.cobol
archives or www.cobolreport.com you will find an "article" that I did on how
to get the draft and submit reviews (either in or outside the US).

4) The ENTRY statement has never been part of any ANSI or ISO Standard,
although it is a "relatively" common extension.  The same functionality (but
certainly NOT the same syntax) can be accomplished with nested programs
(among other approaches).  J4 (the ANSI COBOL group maintains a list of
"candidates for a future revision") If there is something that you would
like "added", then you should send your suggestion (with as much detail and
supporting information as possible) to the J4 chair at:
    doncobol <at> mediaone.net

5) Besides documenting (in their "vendor documentation") what are extensions
and what is standard, every ANSI/ISO conforming compiler is REQUIRED to
provide a mechanism for flagging the use of extensions at compile-time.  If
you are using a compiler that supports ENTRY statements, my GUESS is that
you should look for a "FLAGSTD" compiler option or directive.  If your COBOL
compiler doesn't have that, tell us which compiler you are using - and we
can tell you how to get compile-time warnings of your use of extensions.  (S
ome of which you MIGHT want to suggest get added to a future COBOL
Standard.)

6) "Real COBOL coders" are encouraged to "participate" in the COBOL
standards development at all stages - suggestions for new features, review
of draft documents, etc.  HOWEVER, the responses are *not* "speedy".

     ***

I hope this helps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu v3 & console screen/keyboard I/O

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-02-24T07:55:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9768E2.B4FC5D71@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<973df5$crd$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3A961888.B38388DE@Azonic.co.nz> <974mba$cgs$2@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> Was the last Cobol standard set back in 1989 ?

The last language standard was 1985, the 1989 addendum added the
intrinsic functions. The next standard has been scheduled since about
1997 but probably won't be this year either. Did you want new standards
more frequently ?  Did you want a language like VB where each release
requires programs to be rewritten (as will occur again with the next
release).

> I need to know what standard is available within each compiler
> and what defines that standard.

Every major compiler should be implementing the full and correct 85/89
standard.  I suspect that you really want to know what _non-standard_ is
in each compiler.  If you look at the MF manual you will see that the
extensions are marked 'MF', 'OS/VS', 'X/Open', etc.

For other versions read the manuals.

>    'The reason ENTRY will not become part of the COBOL
>    standard is because it is considered "unstructured."
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> an overhead on the both the cpu and my fingers. 

You confuse size of source code (ie fingers') with executable size (ie
cpu).  Even though ENTRY may be less source code than, say, nested
routines, does not mean that there is less executable code.

In fact the way that Cobol works with dynamic loading of routines there
is a process that must be executed for each call and that is to search a
table of loaded routines.  If the routine name is not found then the
disk is searched and the program loaded.  All routine names are then
loaded into the table so that later CALLs can find them.  If you use
ENTRY extensively then this table will get larger and there will be a
larger search overhead on every call.

> Who decides
> what goes in and what comes out ? Can we as Cobol coders
> contribute ?

Yes, you can submit to the standards committee, but they have closed off
for the next standard (2002?).  Next one after that may be another
decade or more later.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu v3 & console screen/keyboard I/O

```
Thanks for the feedback, just joking about my fingers 8)

I'm careful about using the Entry statement as I appreciate its overheads
and
use it only within my background support files which are loaded at the start
of my applications.

The nice thing about the Entry Statement is that it allows similar programs
to
share code and data whilst being called with variable numbers of parameters,
or at least that's why I use it.

Any suggestions on profiling the executable code are welcome. The best tool
I have for low level testing is Xilerator (part of the MF WB tool-set) which
allows animation at assembler level and is the area of coding that I most
enjoy.

Regards

Alister

PS. Dont want to do complete rewrites unless it makes my code more efficient
but recompiling is okay. (VB ? you sick person)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>

- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com> <Fsgl6.2965$Qd4.345204@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3A95C3DE.B6A4FE70@home.com>`

```
OH - you mean the individual records are 64k.

Wow - I coded a program with 45K records, and got in a heap of trouble.
It was almost impossible to get my job to run quickly. I had about 1.5
million input records and 4.5 million output. My job ran for about 3 days
each month. It was regularly cancelled, as it had control of a critical
database - thank goodness for XRST !

I would strongly recomment splitting into shorter records with a key .
Sort into key sequence order. Then you can process them like a batch.
Save any intermediate data to apply to each record with the same key value.

P.S. IMS won't let you use spanned records for a GSAM file. I tried and
got smacked around by the DBA.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-23T07:21:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<010c9tsjuukjsbu95c4ch6p3d20aalcctr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com> <Fsgl6.2965$Qd4.345204@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3A95C3DE.B6A4FE70@home.com>`

```
On Fri, 23 Feb 2001 01:58:49 GMT, Frank Kovacs <kovacsfs@home.com> wrote:

>Yes RECFM=FBS. The records would be about 64K. The would be 10-20K worth
>of them
RECFM=FBS does _NOT_ not Fixed Blocked Spanned!!!!!  There is no such beast.  FBS stands
for Fixed Block Standard,which means that all the blocks (except the last one) are
completely filled.  If you want spanned records longer than 32K you need LRECL=X (yes,
this is uppercase character X), and RECFM=VBS, and a blocksize less than or equal to 32760

And -- never use FBS when you want to you the file in a concatenation!

(Learned this the hard way 20 years ago)


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,

      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         In C we had to code our own bugs. In C++ we can inherit them.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-24T10:47:28+01:00

- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com> <Fsgl6.2965$Qd4.345204@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3A95C3DE.B6A4FE70@home.com> <010c9tsjuukjsbu95c4ch6p3d20aalcctr@4ax.com> <2Hsl6.628$sy.55323@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
On Fri, 23 Feb 2001 12:27:42 GMT, "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:
Rufio,

I am talking MVS here as well.  A direct quote from the JCL manual:

<quote>
 ï¿½ S     (1) For fixed-length records, indicates that the records are       ï¿½
 ï¿½       written as standard blocks, that is, no truncated blocks or        ï¿½
 ï¿½       unfilled tracks within the data set, with the exception of the     ï¿½
 ï¿½       last block or track. (2) For variable-length records, indicates    ï¿½
 ï¿½       that a record can span more than one block.                        ï¿½
</quote>

and

<quote>
 LRECL=bytes

 bytes
    Specifies (1) the length, in bytes, for fixed length records or (2) the maximum
    length, in bytes, for variable-length records.
 
    The value of bytes is:
 
       1 to 32760 for non-VSAM data sets.
 
       1 to 32761 for VSAM key-sequenced (KS), entry-sequenced (ES), or relative record
        (RR) data sets. (LRECL does not apply to VSAM linear space, RECORG=LS, data sets.)
 
        For VSAM key-sequenced (KS) data sets, a record length must be specified, either
        explicitly with the LRECL or LIKE parameter, or in the data class for the data
        set. The record length must be greater than the key length.
 
 
    Note:  When RECFM is F or U, the length must not exceed DCB BLKSIZE. For RECFM=D or V,
           the length must not exceed BLKSIZE minus 4. For RECFM=VS, the length can exceed
           BLKSIZE. For unblocked records when DCB RKP=0, the length is for only the data
           portion of the record. LRECL=0 is valid only for RECFM=U.
</quote>

This is from GC28-1757-08: OS/390 MVS Jcl Reference Manual



>Ah - we are talking different OS's.
>MVS does do FBS (or VBS for variable block spanned).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-02-25T12:09:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Wam6.135962$Ch.25108808@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com> <Fsgl6.2965$Qd4.345204@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3A95C3DE.B6A4FE70@home.com> <010c9tsjuukjsbu95c4ch6p3d20aalcctr@4ax.com> <2Hsl6.628$sy.55323@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
If your are truly commited to using / creating records that SPAN a more than
the
BLOCKSIZE, then here you go:

5.2.10.1 Under OS/390 and VM

The RECORDING MODE clause specifies the format of the physical records in a
QSAM file.  The clause is ignored for a VSAM file.

Permitted values for RECORDING MODE are:

Recording Mode F (Fixed)
    All the records in a file are the same length and each is wholly
contained within one block.  Blocks can
    contain more than one record, and there is usually a fixed number of
records for each block.  In this
    mode, there are no record-length or block-descriptor fields.

Recording Mode V (Variable)
    The records can be either fixed-length or variable-length, and each must
be wholly contained within
    one block.  Blocks can contain more than one record.  Each data record
includes a record-length field
   and each block includes a block-descriptor field.  These fields are not
described in the Data Division.  They are  each 4 bytes long and provision
is automatically made for them.  These fields are not available
to you.

Recording Mode U (Fixed or Variable)
    The records can be either fixed-length or variable-length.  However,
there is only one record for each
    block.  There are no record-length or block-descriptor fields.

    Note:  You cannot use RECORDING MODE U if you are using the BLOCK
CONTAINS clause.

Recording Mode S (Spanned)
    The records can be either fixed-length or variable-length, and can be
larger than a block.  If a record is
    larger than the remaining space in a block, a segment of the record is
written to fill the block.  The
    remainder of the record is stored in the next block (or blocks, if
required).  Only complete records are
    made available to you.  Each segment of a record in a block, even if it
is the entire record, includes a
    segment-descriptor field, and each block includes a block-descriptor
field.  These fields are not
    described  in the Data Division; provision is automatically made for
them.  These fields are not
    available to you.


Note:  When recording mode S is used, the BLOCK CONTAINS CHARACTERS clause
must be used.
Recording mode S is not allowed for ASCII files.

If the RECORDING MODE clause is not specified for a QSAM file, the IBM COBOL
for OS/390 & VM compiler determines the recording mode as follows:

F   The compiler determines the recording mode to be F if the largest
level-01 record associated with the file is not greater than the block size
specified in the BLOCK CONTAINS clause, and you do one of
the following:

       Use the RECORD CONTAINS integer clause (for more information, see
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  Compiler and Run-Time Migration Guide.)

       Omit the RECORD clause and make sure all level-01 records associated
with the file are the same size and none contain an OCCURS DEPENDING ON
clause.


V   The compiler determines the recording mode to be V if the largest
level-01 record associated with the file is not greater than the block size
specified in the BLOCK CONTAINS clause, and you do one of
the following:

       Use the RECORD IS VARYING clause

       Omit the RECORD clause and make sure all level-01 records associated
with the file are not the same size or some contain an OCCURS DEPENDING ON
clause

       Use the RECORD CONTAINS integer-1 TO integer-2 clause with integer-1
the minimum length and integer-2 the maximum length of the level-01 records
associated with the file.  The two integers must
be different, with values matching minimum and maximum length of either
different length records or
record(s) with an OCCURS DEPENDING ON clause.

S   The compiler determines the recording mode to be S if the maximum block
size is smaller than the largest  record size.

U   Recording mode U is never obtained by default.  The RECORDING MODE U
clause must be explicitly used.


SO THAt means something that looks like this:

FD MY-FILE-OF-LONG-RECS
       RECORD CONTAINS 65536 CHARACTERS
       BLOCK CONTAINS 32760 CHARACTERS
       RECORDING MODE S.

01  REALLY-LONG-REC.
    05  Field-1                           PIC X(20).
    05  FILLER                          PIC X(60000).
    05  Field-2                           PIC X(516).

Of course, the Blocksize could be 512 Bytes, and the Record could contain
2048 Bytes,
Then with RECORDING MODE S, the records would 'SPAN' a Block.


Rufio <davecawdell@home.com> wrote in message
news:2Hsl6.628$sy.55323@news1.rdc1.az.home.com...
> Ah - we are talking different OS's.
> MVS does do FBS (or VBS for variable block spanned).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > On Fri, 23 Feb 2001 01:58:49 GMT, Frank Kovacs <kovacsfs@home.com>
wrote:
> >
> > >Yes RECFM=FBS. The records would be about 64K. The would be 10-20K
worth
> > >of them
> > RECFM=FBS does _NOT_ not Fixed Blocked Spanned!!!!!  There is no such
> beast.  FBS stands
> > for Fixed Block Standard,which means that all the blocks (except the
last
> one) are
> > completely filled.  If you want spanned records longer than 32K you need
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T18:09:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9A9B85.B7207CE4@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com>`

```
Frank Kovacs wrote:
> 
> Can anyone provide an short example of how to create a fixed spanned
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

RECFM=FBS does NOT mean SPANNED.  The S is STANDARD.  This means
that there can be no short blocks.

Just how you intend to do SPANNED within FB or F is beyond me. 
Now, many years ago there was something called spread cards. 
This is where a record would exceed the 80 byte limitation of the
old cards, and so, one would read multiple of these to get the
full record in (assuming it was all on cards).

For what you are doing, it would seem that you would want to
define a record that is x bytes in size, but, has multiple record
types all the same size.  This would then allow you to write out
a 60K (or larger) F/FB record.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T15:46:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97eion$ajd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com> <3A9A9B85.B7207CE4@neo.rr.com>`

```
I haven't gotten into this entire thread - because I do NOT have an OS/390
system to play with.  However, several years ago, I did try "this" and found
that the following type of FD (with no Record Format clause) did cause a
spanned record to be created.

  FD Span-File
        record contains 1000 characters
        block contains 500 characters
      .

i.e. simply define a record with the record size larger than the blocksize
and COBOL "does the rest" for you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T01:27:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9C53C7.C559F73E@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com>`

```
Frank Kovacs wrote:
> 
> Can anyone provide an short example of how to create a fixed spanned
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

I think I figured it out.  By using RECFM=VBS, BUT, writing an
"F" type record of 60K, what the compiler will do is span that
fixed record across the blocks the way it would a large SMF
record.  The difference is, the RDW will always contain the same
value, with the exception of the second half which will contain
the segment ID.

This is the only way I can see for the RECFM=VBS to work with
this large file.  

Meanwhile, until the "128K restriction" is lifted on "GETMAINed"
areas, I wouldn't try to make a record bigger than that.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T03:13:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01_m6.1282$q9.76454@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com> <3A9C53C7.C559F73E@neo.rr.com>`

```
Well, it's time to agree that my original suggestion of FBS was wrong - as
has been pointed out by "a few" people.

I have coded RECFM=VBS for 32K records, and foolishly figured FB
would be FBS for spanned records - since when was anything in this
business logical ? My process with 32K VBS records ran monthly in
production for about a year. But I spent most of that time regretting
having ever thought it was a good idea.

I threw together a program to process batches of records, that held the same
data as the individual records, and instead of running for days, it took a
few
hours. I tweeked it and squeezed all the performance I could manage and I
have 2 separate programs (different input formats) that each run for about
an hour.

While the old process was running I used to get threats from the production
DBA's about my use of the IMSLOG (running BMP), and I used to prevent
a daily synch point job from running against a core-application DB.

The sooner I get into the web world, where they apparently don't care about
performance, the better!

"Steve Thompson" <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3A9C53C7.C559F73E@neo.rr.com...
> Frank Kovacs wrote:
> >
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> in use
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fixed Spanned Records

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T07:00:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9D0466.FD32D683@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3A9592A7.1F77A5F9@home.com> <3A9C53C7.C559F73E@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:

> Meanwhile, until the "128K restriction" is lifted on "GETMAINed"
> areas, I wouldn't try to make a record bigger than that.

As long as we have better tools, I wouldn't try to make a record bigger
than that.

I suppose there might be blobs which would want such a record size, but I
am not thinking of a business application which didn't use them which
should be designed with such records.
```

```
JOB >> Consulting >> 1 Year+ >> Brooklyn NY

To build and tailor as many as 20 separate ADABAS regions that will
accommodate transaction server and batch transactions called by Natural
and COBOL programs, as well as client server technology (ADAESI calls)
and the Entire-X WEB enabling software and brokers.  The consultant will
develop and maintain backup/recovery/archiving procedures and perform
all database administration functions using Software Ag products
(Natural Security, Predict, Review, etc.) and IBM utilities (IDCMAS,
IEBCOPY, etc.).  Other tasks will include:  provide ongoing technical
support to ensure applications availability and provide comprehensive
documentation and skills transfer for customer staff and client
agencies.

What skills must the person have?

ADABAS, Natural, COBOL, ADAESI calls, Entire-X, IBM Utilities,
SagaVista, OS/390, USS, RACF, Web Enablement, SMP/E, Lotus Domino, Linux

Gentlemen/Ladies,

I am a recruiter and seeking out candidates with the above technologies
is not my specialty, but it won't stop me from trying. If you can handle
the above tasks and/or know someone who can, please send me your resume
and desired hourly rates.

I am a recruiter, working with a recruiter on this and a few other gigs,
so third parties should not reply.

The location is Brooklyn, NY and the candidate should live in the
tri-state area or be willing to move here for the duration of the
project.

My client is the innovator of the industry's most advanced information
technologies, including computer systems, software, networking systems,
storage devices and microelectronics.

Money:  Pretty much anything youï¿½re used to getting per hour.  Nothing
too, too crazy, but then again.

references and a writing sample to ben@kandidates.com

Ben Holden
```

## Thread: Novell + icon + batch file — 2 msgs

### Novell + icon + batch file

- **From:** "Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T18:59:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wLhl6.1292$t51.17673@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Hello all,

I have a strange problem.  I install a runtime rmcobol 7.0 and it seems to
work well.

I call my program from a batch file.

When I open an MSDOS window and call my batch file, everything work great.

My problem is if I create an icon in windows calling this same batch file, I
got some DOS windows when I isued Endcap or a Capture, command from Novell.
In those Dos Windows we can see something like
"Usage: ENDCAP [/cancel]
 or ENDCAP [/cancel] [/local=n]
 or ENDCAP [/cancel] [ALL]"

Just like it wasn't a good command or good syntax.

We have done a lot of test and we do not understand.

Is it a path problem, is the DOS path differe from the path in Windows?
Is there a special configuration we have to do in Novell so it could work
correctly in Windows?
Is there a problem with the runtime? or the client32?
Any idea?

Thank's in advance!
Eric.
```

#### ↳ Re: Novell + icon + batch file

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-02-23T15:47:07
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9760o8$mig$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<wLhl6.1292$t51.17673@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Sounds like a problem with the param's on the capture command.
Double check the name of the Novell print queue.

    CAPTURE L=n Q=x NFF NB AU

        Will start capture where

            L        Lpt port number. Replace n with 1 - 9.
            Q        Novell print queue (replace x with its name).
            NFF    No Form Feed.
            NB      No Banner.
            AU      AUto end capture.

    CAPTURE L=n EC

    Will end capture where.

            L        Lpt port number. Replace n with 1 - 9.
            EC     End Capture. Just as it says....

Hope this helps...

Alister

"Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca> wrote in message
news:wLhl6.1292$t51.17673@weber.videotron.net...
> Hello all,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> My problem is if I create an icon in windows calling this same batch file,
I
> got some DOS windows when I isued Endcap or a Capture, command from
Novell.
> In those Dos Windows we can see something like
> "Usage: ENDCAP [/cancel]
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-02-22T19:53:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pqil6.124570$Ch.22763723@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net> <94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qVXb6.1124$uN3.96554@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <94qg88$g5m$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <fWec6.695$23.49654@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
and still have the attitude, 'if I ask enough, maybe someone will tell me

> I guess I'm just old-fashioned enough to believe that both COBOL and BASIC

an
```

```
Maybe one of the problems is that the instructors do not know how to teach.
Too many instructors have an attitude  that all you have to do is throw the
student a reference guide for the programming code and people can just
figure it out on their own.  I am taking a class now in Java and I think the
Book was written by some linux C programmer that thought everyone that read
the book already programmed in C and could just read between the lines.  Too
many books dont have enough practice programs where they explain why they do
something they did.  The key to teaching students well is a good lesson
plan, and being prepared to ask a few questions, instead of becoming the
disappearing the Instructor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T07:16:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9D084E.AF4314F3@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net> <94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qVXb6.1124$uN3.96554@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <94qg88$g5m$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <fWec6.695$23.49654@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <pqil6.124570$Ch.22763723@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <SY_m6.29099$t67.441827@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```


charles wrote:

> Maybe one of the problems is that the instructors do not know how to teach.
> Too many instructors have an attitude  that all you have to do is throw the
> student a reference guide for the programming code and people can just
> figure it out on their own.

A trouble with library based languages is that a complete reference manual
includes the whole library.   With CoBOL, we have no operator overloading, no
key-word overloading, and no overloading of the built in functions.  So
everything fits in one manual.  We don't ever need to read the code behind our
key words.

The biggest nightmare for CoBOL programmers is testing everything which uses a
Procedure division copy member which gets changed.  With Java and C++, doesn't
that mean testing the whole world every time virtually anything gets changed?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T10:33:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vY8n6.25$DQ2.295@client>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net> <94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qVXb6.1124$uN3.96554@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <94qg88$g5m$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <fWec6.695$23.49654@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <pqil6.124570$Ch.22763723@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <SY_m6.29099$t67.441827@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3A9D084E.AF4314F3@brazee.net>`

```
Not unless your C++ program runs in a Windows environment and calls a DLL or
other operating system component.  In this case you need to test not only
every time anything gets changed but any time the OS is updated or, for that
matter, any time any other application is installed on the machine that
might have updated  or overwritten that DLL.

You also have to teach the entire Windows environment - as you must with any
other OS/Hardware platform for almost any language.

All languages and platforms have problems with shared code/libraries.  They
just come under different guises.  And they all have to be managed with good
coding and system management techniques.
```

```
Posting-Frequency: twice a month

Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

```
now. I've coded my program according to the userguides
that came with the compiler (i.e. declared my host
variables and sqlstate/sqlcode) and I've got myself a
simple program that will do a simple bit of sql.

and it looks like I've got this all set up ok
according to the manuals.

John
```
