- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9796nn$n53$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Seen quite a few comments about code performance yet no request for
an inline assembler. That would be nice 8). No more overheads of calling
these tiny little bin modules.

Any comments ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance ?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-02-25T13:08:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B225F5F69C0B825D.19FD98C8233F0CC7.11F48B41FABC2538@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<9796nn$n53$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Sat, 24 Feb 2001 20:50:46 -0000, "Alister Munro"
<alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> enlightened us:

>Seen quite a few comments about code performance yet no request for
>an inline assembler. That would be nice 8). No more overheads of calling
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm sure Mr. Klein may jump in and correct me if my memory is wrong,
but I remember working in Cobol-D and there was available a statement:

ENTER ASSEMBLER

After which you could write BAL code.  You ended it with EXIT
ASSEMBLER.  

Now I don't know when that went away, but it did.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I have kleptomania,
but when it gets bad I take something for it.


Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-25T20:16:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97ce67$ck5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9796nn$n53$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <B225F5F69C0B825D.19FD98C8233F0CC7.11F48B41FABC2538@lp.airnews.net>`

```
The ENTER statement is still part of the '85 Standard - but was obsolete
there - and is removed in the draft 200x Standard.

I checked a few years ago and couldn't find any "current" implementations
supporting it. Historically (I think) it was replaced by the CALL statement
combined with EXEC/END-EXEC statements (the former for "full procedures" in
foreign/non-COBOL languages - and the latter for "escapes" into temporary
foreign syntax).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance ?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T02:30:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010225213057.13433.00005385@nso-cs.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<B225F5F69C0B825D.19FD98C8233F0CC7.11F48B41FABC2538@lp.airnews.net>`

```
In article <B225F5F69C0B825D.19FD98C8233F0CC7.11F48B41FABC2538@lp.airnews.net>,
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> writes:

>
>ENTER ASSEMBLER
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I remember ENTER SYMBOLIC/EXIT SYMBOLIC from the 
Unisys COBOL68 days.  The SYMBOLIC access stuff went 
away with the advent of COBOL74.  Something about not 
wanting to allow coders access to system level controls that 
might be less than safe to be playing with.
```

```
Psyborg:

The best place to get an inexpensive compiler or to learn how to write
COBOL code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

The book only costs about $20.00 US$ and includes the compiler at no
additional charge.

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.chapters.ca
http://www.indigo.ca
http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com
http://www.amazon.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler for a beginner

```
Merant, previously known as Micro Focus, sells various compilers.  
Some versions of compilers are designed for the web and some are not.
Usually a free one or cheaper student edition does not make object code.
I think the Micro Focus Cobol for windows (Win3.1), which is the older 
version came in a student edition for about $49.00 and a version that 
could make Object code ran about $100.00 or so.  The newer compilers are
a little more expensive because they have ways to interact with the 
Application Proragrammer Interface(API) for windows and are designed with
internet applications in mind.

If you want to learn basic coding skills maybe try one of the Books like 
Learn COBOL in 24 Hours which comes with a Fujitsu student edition compiler.
Try looking in places like Amazon.com or other online book stores if you 
can not find the books in a mall bookstore.  I think it is easier to order
books online than to go to a mall.
```

<a id="t-8dc6be7c1f"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices? — 4 this month / 54 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-26T14:44:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl>`

```
Pardon me if this topic has been discussed recently; I have not
been reading the newsgroup closely for several weeks.

I just received my Fujitsu COBOL 6.0 upgrade, and on opening it was
shocked to read the following text in the cover letter:

  "We have introduced a new license control system with this release
  so you will need to send an email note (which can be generated
  automatically) to our license server during the installation
  process to obtain your site key.  You have 30 days in which to
  register your system after your first install. ..."

Well fellow COBOL'ers, that kills Fujitsu as a compiler vendor, as
far as I'm concerned.  I have yet to use Fujitsu COBOL for a single
application, but I have been maintaining my license for several
years, as a hedge against this practice by MERANT.  If this keeps
up, I will simply start writing my applications using another
language.  Even Microsoft doesn't put you in a situation where you
can no longer install their compiler products if Microsoft goes
out of business.  And I dare say, Microsoft is less likely to do so
than MERANT or Fujitsu's software company.  And compounded by the
fact that Fujitsu has joined MERANT in charging exorbitant prices,
I see the day rapidly approaching when I sadly must abandon COBOL,
which has been my primary development language for over 3 decades.
Sigh.
```

```

I have been maintaining my licence for years now and I have been

I completely agree with Judson that maybe it's time for us Cobol'ers
to look for alternatives.

To other Cobol'ers using Fujitsu, maybe you should voice your opinion
or send email to Fujitsu. I for sure will send an email to Fuijtsu
voicing my objection to this licencing nightmare. 


Tony M. Mina
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T22:26:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97kf8s$8aqs$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl>`

```
Judson,

In favor of Fujitsu, the one fact I've noticed missing in this entire thread
is the fact that those of us on "Maintenance" have gotten Version 6.0
"free".  The maintenance prices have not changed, so far as I can see, i.e.
$500 for Enterprise Edition, $250 for Professional Edition, etc.  So we'll
still stick with the product, at least for a while.

However, as a person that is directly involved in a Disaster Recovery
Service, Fujitsu needs to understand that:

a.  It's not a matter of "if" hard drives will fail, it's a matter of
"when".  So one would be hard pressed to "kill" the license key on a system
with a broken hard drive.

b.  A footprint of the system that the software was installed on rarely
would help if you keep your Original Software off-site, as all prudent
businesses do, and need to reload it at your Disaster Recovery Center.

c.  Most "Disasters" (keep in mind Disasters are not necessarily fires or
tornados, but for real hardware failures) are not declared during normal
business hours.  Therefore, it would be tough to call Fujitsu during their
"9am to 5pm" normal business hours.  Since I work for a Bank, the bulk of
the processing is after 5pm.

A company that has followed a similar concept for Mainframe/PC
interconnectivity is Attachmate, which puts a Mainframe Serial number in
their software, thus rendering it inoperable in a Disaster Recovery mode
without calling them in advance.  Tough to do in a "Disaster" mode.
Therefore, the organization that I work for dropped that software entirely,
in favor of a company which at least allows to backup the software for
emergency use.  Will we do the same with Fujitsu?  Hard to tell at this
point in time.

I totally understand the Piracy issue, but isn't it about time we get out of
kindergarten, where the whole class is punished because there was one or two
"bad eggs" in the class?

Denny
```

<a id="t-7a60c7ac6d"></a>
## Thread: What are Spanned Records ? — 4 this month / 8 total

### What are Spanned Records ?

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T02:01:11
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
What are they and what are they used for ? I've been writing Cobol apps
for 15 years using unix and dos as the target op-sys. Only once have I
shoved code at a mainframe(didn't have a clue what I was doing but it
did work).
```

#### ↳ Re: What are Spanned Records ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T08:02:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97j0c2$m8r$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Well, ...
  First you need to understand about "blocking" records which is NOT
something that normally (ever?) occurs in UNIX and PC environments.  (The
COBOLs accept the "block contains" clause - but does NOTHING with them)

Once you understand what a "block" is, then a "spanned" record is one were
the record "spans" multiple blocks, i.e. the "record contains" clause is
LARGER than the "block contains" clause (in COBOL terms).

Even in systems that "fully support" blocked records, this is fairly unusual
(as "blocking" is usually an I/O efficiency issue - and spanning blocks
usually defeats the operating systems "blocking" efficiencies.)

Does this help at all?
```

#### ↳ Re: What are Spanned Records ?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T14:46:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9D0EEB.62DE3DE@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> What are they and what are they used for ? I've been writing Cobol apps
> for 15 years using unix and dos as the target op-sys. Only once have I
> shoved code at a mainframe(didn't have a clue what I was doing but it
> did work).

The idea of spanned records is foreign to those using computers
that do not deal in "block I/O".  Most PC systems that I've dealt
with are byte I/O oriented, as are most UNIX platforms that I've
been exposed to.

To make this very simple, I will explain this in terms of the IBM
mainframe systems.

There are two types of records and they are fixed and variable.

If you take the fixed record format and you decide to take
advantage of the architecture, then you may find that you can put
multiple logical records (the way you and your program sees
things) into one physical record (the way the hardware sees
things).

This also holds true for variable length records.  You can put
them into blocks as well.  Here is where we run into a problem. 
How do you know one record from the other if they are not all the
same size?  Well, with MVS & DOS/VS based I/O, you have an RDW
(Record Descriptor Word).  This word tells you how long the
record is that follows it.  Then, if the blocks are variable in
size, you have the BDW (Block Descriptor Word), which tells you
how long the block is.

Suppose that I'm working with telemetry where the records are
very long.  To deal with this, we need to have something called
spanned records, so that the logical record can be longer than
the block (or physical record).

The area where this is used on a daily basis is the resource
accounting system called SMF (System Management Facility).  The
operating system writes out a VBS (Variable Blocked Spanned) file
that contains all the records for charge-back accounting for this
operating system.  Basically, all the I/O that your tasks do get
recorded in a record.  All the CPU usage gets recorded in a
record. All the memory usage and so forth.

Hope this is helpful.
```

#### ↳ Re: What are Spanned Records ?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T15:02:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cq8n6.1634$7Y1.212430@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
The trivial answer is: a spanned record is one which requires more
than one physical record. Suppose a customer record requires four
punched cards to contain all the information about a customer. You
have one customer record spanned over four physical records.

Hardware reads physical records - programs process logical records.

A physical record may contain: 1)multiple logical records, 2) one
logical record, 3)part of a logical record, 4)(from 0 to) multiple
physical records and (up to two) parts of additional physical records.

In cases 3 & 4, you have spanned records.

The case above on punched cards may seem silly, but most hardware has
some limitation as to the maximum physical size of a record. For
example, tape drives were once limited to 32k bytes per physical
record (and may still). Sometimes this physical limitation is
insufficient to accommodate the logical record requirements.


"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk...
> What are they and what are they used for ? I've been writing Cobol
apps
> for 15 years using unix and dos as the target op-sys. Only once have
I
> shoved code at a mainframe(didn't have a clue what I was doing but
it
> did work).
>
>
>
>
```

<a id="t-49fcba2487"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices? — 7 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T05:29:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z00n6.1166$%4.120236@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ga2s$d1l$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:97ga2s$d1l$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk...
> You didn't say how the license works.... Do you receive an email
> containing a serial/activation key which can be keyed at any time ?
> This would make sense where trying to combat piracy.
>
Here's how Fujitsu's install works:
1. You install the product and enter the serial number of the CD.
2. The installation process generates half of the required unlock key
and the installation process e-mails this half-key to Fujitsu's
license-registry computer. The half-key presumably contains a hash of
the installation computer's physical characteristics.
3. Their computer updates its license file (subtracting 1 from the
number of licenses you've bought) and emails the second half of the
unlock key.
4. You enter the second half of the key and everything is golden.

Except for the following:

1. In the best of all possible worlds, you CAN move the license from
machine "A" to machine "B."  IF you've done something to return the
license from "A" back to FJ and fresh installed on "B," you can then
move the license from "B" and "A" via a floppy (and back again). Their
license server does not have to get involved (unless something goes
wrong, of course, in which case, you're in trouble).
2. If the machine on which the license is installed has to have a new
hard drive, you're in trouble.
3. If you have to install/reinstall the OS, you're in trouble.
4. You can't take your laptop from home directly to a customer's site;
you must first go to the office and perform the swap in #1 (and hope
it works). You're going to a lot of trouble.
5. If you - for whatever reason - remove (as in "Add/Remove") the
software, the software cannot be re-installed without a lot of begging
and whining via a voice call. You're in trouble.
6. If you have a dual-boot machine (say Win95/W2K) and want the
compiler in both partitions for compatibility testing, you're in
trouble.


Perhaps it got lost in the translation:
'Site' to Fujitsu means 'seat' - to the rest of the world it means
'location'
'Enterprise Edition' to Fujitsu means a set of features, to most of
the world it means 'unlimited license.'

So a Fujitsu Site License for their Enterprise Edition means a lot of
features are available on one computer.

All in all, a most unsatisfactory situation.

P.S.,
I may have a few of the details slightly wrong, but it's tough to get
through their thirty-seven page installation instructions (replete
with phrases like: "Uninstalling a registered product may result in
losing your license to the product" and "Failure to transfer or kill
your license prior to uninstalling will require you to buy a new
license to the product if you want to reinstall it" ), without
throwing a hissy fit and losing one's sanity.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T02:59:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97iemv$l1c$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ga2s$d1l$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <z00n6.1166$%4.120236@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
One thing that I think *might* have been lost in this thread is that the
Fujitsu licensing issue is a "development" license - NOT a run-time license
(unlike MERANT mentioned in the subject of the thread).

A "production" Fujitsu developed application may STILL be distributed to as
many separate machines as "desired" with no run-time license fee.  I have
even heard (but not confirmed for myself) that the "previous"
multi-threading run-time fee is being removed with the latest release of
Fujitsu's products.

However, this change (and the problems listed below) do apply to those
trying to develop the same application (or even multiple applications) on
multiple machines.
```

```
Jerry P wrote:

> 2. If the machine on which the license is installed has to have a new
> hard drive, you're in trouble.

I expect my current hardware to last forever and always be sufficient.


> 3. If you have to install/reinstall the OS, you're in trouble.

We're talking Windows here.  To be that popular, it must be stable and

> 4. You can't take your laptop from home directly to a customer's site;

Microsoft created a boot manager (I preferred IBM's, but this is OK).  But
every single computer you can buy comes with Windows on C:.   Obviously
they thought this through and decided that nobody would ever use this boot
manager software intelligently.

Of course we're referring to a compiler here.  Nobody should EVER want to
do development work with more than one version of Windows.

So let Microsoft do our thinking for us!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T19:52:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9D56BF.5311CDF1@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ga2s$d1l$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <z00n6.1166$%4.120236@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A9D06C6.6741A7D3@brazee.net> <4w8n6.1640$7Y1.214511@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
<snip>
> ## Yeah, but, see, some of our users are not as diligent about
> immediately upgrading operating systems as we so we have to make sure
> our product works on their dinosaur operating systems (Win98, WinNT,
> etc.).

This brings up an interesting idea.  Why, as a user, do I have to
change to the latest and greatest operating system?  

Suppose that I'm using an accounting system written in COBOL
(since this is a COBOL n/g), and nothing about my business has
changed, except, I've gotten more business.  Do I need to go from
W/3.0 to W/3.1 to W/95 to W/98 to W/me just because M$ put them
out there?

If I am running a printer that works just fine, and I have forms
that meet all my requirements, why do I need to upgrade computers
and/or O/Ses?  So, why should I have to upgrade my accounting
system? [I have a good one for this later on.]

We all know that payroll changes.  It is the nature of the beast
because the US Congress and the several states can't leave things
alone (they have to do some thing to earn their perks and get
contributions to their various political parties).  However, most
of those changes are in the form of tables that must be
upgraded.  So, why would I have to get the latest and greatest
payroll system once I've gotten one that does what I need?

This particular line of thinking is one that IBM has taken --
compatibility.  When they put out a new release of the O/S, the
old code doesn't break just because!  From what I could see, IBM
used this same methodology with OS/2.

So, how did IBM make money on these things?  By something called
license fees.  You paid them monthly (well, actually, quarterly
or better), for the use of their code.  This then covered all the
maintenance costs of your software (hey, we found a new way to
break something so, here is a patch so that you won't stumble
into this).  All the ISVs do the same thing.  

The problem is, with the PC world, this was not taken into
account when software was developed, and so, M$ in order to
generate profits, has to make sure that one release of Office is
not compatible, in some way, with a prior release.  This forces
businesses to upgrade because their customers may get the new
Office, and won't be able to exchange documents as a result.

If you will notice, M$ software does not get better, it gets more
functions/features, which in turn require more Service Packs (or
is that service releases), and for some products, this just means
that M$ puts out a new release period, which you have to buy, to
get the fixes for the bugs in your current release.

I much prefer to pay a license fee on an annual basis for my
software.  I have one product that we are currently not using. 
So we did not renew the license, and on a certain day, it stopped
working (well, I think it has stopped)!  Well, since we aren't
using it, not a problem.  But, if you had to get an annual
license to keep functioning with that software, you'd pay the
fee.

Now, competition comes in.  The competitor to product xxx does
not charge a fee once you have purchased the product.  So, what
will you do?  Most people buy the product with the pay once fee
structure.  Now, they get locked into having to buy the latest
copy every 2-3 years.  

Looking at Windoze, what was the cost for the W/95 Upgrade?  What
was the W/98 Upgrade cost?  And the W/ME Upgrade cost?  Now look
at Office and see what it costs to buy it new, and what it costs
to get the upgrades.

What would it have cost had you been paying an annual
license/maintenance fee?

Now let us go back to the IBM mainframe world.  In 1991 I knew of
a company that was running VM/SP w/ OS/VS1 on a 4381 and had
gotten the 4381 used.  Why?  Because COBOL still worked, JCL was
still functional, they could still get support for their
printers, CMS handled the online users just fine.  All of this
for the cost of electricity and A/C -- since their software was
not supported by IBM, they weren't paying any fees!  That changed
when they had to upgrade for Y2K -- I hear they went to NT
systems with COBOL because it was cheaper than the upgrade to
OS/390!

In the business world, particularly manufacturing, you don't
replace a machine just because it is old.  You replace it because
it is too expensive to keep it running (e.g., maintenance), or,
because you need some new function/feature that can't be put on
the machine in the field and the two machines can't co-exist
(e.g., not enough room for both).

So, since we all know that software wears out ;-) , what do we do
to make/earn money on what is essentially a turn key situation?

Now, add to this the problem with piracy (or a perceived piracy
problem), and what decisions do the immature IT people bring to
the table (I say immature because most of the PC only people do
not have the experience with multiple platforms that we got in
the '60s-'80s with DEC, Honeywell, Univac, etc.)?  Ones that only
make use of a billing idea that they know and understand.

Hopefully I've given some food for thought here that can be taken
to Fujitsu (and others) to stop this nonsense.  And, something
that will get us all to think about the software that we develop
and why people would want it (and its upgrades).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

```


Steve Thompson wrote:

> Jerry P wrote:
> <snip>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> change to the latest and greatest operating system?

As a user, you don't.  But as a developer, you must test your applications
with a bunch of operating systems.  Don't make the user leave his dinosaur
- and don't make him stay there.

Of course, if you make high end, video cards or sound cards, you make sure
the newest card has drivers for all of the operating systems your customers
will use.  But then you don't spend any money to create new drivers for
cards you stopped selling a year ago - let your loyal customers buy a new
card if they want a new OS!   (which they will do - buying from your
competitor!).
```

<a id="t-205bcdc37a"></a>
## Thread: COBOL 2000 pointers to functions/procedures? — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL 2000 pointers to functions/procedures?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T05:09:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97iivt$1h3k$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<GKSm6.27273$B5.178761@typhoon.bart.nl>`

```

Ronald wrote in message ...
>
>Does the upcoming COBOL standard provide support for pointers to functions?
>(as in: PERFORM function-pointer)

Not "as in" PERFORM.

Not for COBOL functions or COBOL procedures.

COBOL functions are always invoked in-line. COBOL
procedures are, by definition, paragraphs or sections.

There is a USAGE PROGRAM-POINTER. This pointer may
be set to the address of a COBOL program or non-COBOL
procedure or function. The CALL statement is used to
transfer control to the program (or non-COBOL routine), as in:

    CALL program-pointer-1
        [USING arg-1 ...] [RETURNING arg-2].
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: illegal operation error

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-02-28T23:35:17
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97k2df$7r1$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<V48n6.1863$U33.36944@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Hi Eric,

My news server wont allow me to reply to your previous post
regarding the Novell capture.

I am still using a DOS based compiler that does not allow calls
to the windows API and as such I capture ports 9-3 via a batch
file which calls my app. When the user selects a report they are
given a selection of output device names (i.e.. Media[LPT9],
Stores[LPT8], etc. ) to which the output will be sent.

I have no experience with RM Cobol but, you should be able
to call the windows print dialog with a 32bit compiler which
will allow you to select the printer's network url.

illegal operation ... That's a tough one...

I presume you have checked the parameters you are passing
with the call to be the same in both the parent and child. Tidy
up your working directories as intermediate code files with
different date/time stamps can cause this and is probably why
your book suggest re-compilation. If the problem still exists
then check all areas in your data where you are using redefines.

"Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca> wrote in message
```
